title: Technical Report: Assessment of the Impacts of Climate Change on Human Health and Nutrition
summary_snippet: This technical report presents a comprehensive assessment of how climate change impacts human health and nutrition in the Philippines, specifically examining the effects of typhoons, floods, and drought on food security and nutritional status of vulnerable populations. The study analyzes cross-secti...
bm25_text:
Title: Technical Report: Assessment of the Impacts of Climate Change on Human Health and Nutrition
Resource type: Academic Paper
EE pillars: M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems, Nutrition & Health
Overview: This technical report presents a comprehensive assessment of how climate change impacts human health and nutrition in the Philippines, specifically examining the effects of typhoons, floods, and drought on food security and nutritional status of vulnerable populations. The study analyzes cross-sectional data from national nutrition surveys (2013 and 2015) merged with climate exposure data to understand relationships between extreme weather events and nutrition outcomes.
Audience: Researchers, policymakers, and development practitioners working on climate change adaptation and nutrition programs; Technical/Expert Level; Government agencies, research institutions, NGOs, and international development organizations
Where it applies: countries: Philippines; regions: Asia, South-eastern Asia; context: Tropical archipelago vulnerable to typhoons, floods, and drought; scale: National
How it works: The study merges national nutrition survey data with climate exposure data from multiple sources to analyze associations between extreme weather events and nutrition outcomes using logistic regression models.
Key steps: Collect household and individual nutrition data from national surveys. Gather climate exposure data on typhoons, floods, and drought from government and research sources. Merge datasets at provincial and municipal levels based on timing of exposure. Apply statistical models controlling for socioeconomic and demographic factors. Analyze associations between climate exposure and nutrition outcomes. Generate findings on vulnerable populations and time-dependent effects. Provide recommendations for climate adaptation programs
Expected outcomes: Statistical models showing associations between climate exposure and nutrition outcomes, prevalence estimates by population groups and regions; Inform climate change adaptation and mitigation programs targeting vulnerable populations, guide policy responses to protect nutrition during disasters
Key takeaways: Socioeconomic status, household size, and food security are key determinants of nutrition outcomes during climate events. Timing of climate exposure matters - effects on nutrition vary significantly between short-term (1-3 months) and longer-term (5-6 months) exposure periods. Agricultural households are more likely to meet energy requirements but children in these households face higher stunting risks during drought. Vulnerable populations (poorest quintile, large households, food insecure) consistently show worse nutrition outcomes during climate events. Climate adaptation programs should prioritize nutrition protection for indigenous peoples, agricultural communities, and households with young children
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Tropical archipelago vulnerable to typhoons, floods, and drought
Publisher: CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)

title: Training workshop report: Implementing Nationally Determined Contributions (NDC) Commitments in Agriculture
summary_snippet: This is a training workshop report documenting a 3-day capacity building program held in Nairobi in April 2018 to enhance understanding and implementation of agricultural components within Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) for climate action. The workshop targeted government officials from ...
bm25_text:
Title: Training workshop report: Implementing Nationally Determined Contributions (NDC) Commitments in Agriculture
Resource type: Training Manual
EE pillars: Policy & Regulatory, M&E & Learning, Financial Services
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems
Overview: This is a training workshop report documenting a 3-day capacity building program held in Nairobi in April 2018 to enhance understanding and implementation of agricultural components within Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) for climate action. The workshop targeted government officials from Kenya, Tanzania, Uganda, and Ethiopia to address knowledge gaps about UNFCCC processes and provide practical tools for NDC implementation in agriculture.
Audience: Government officials from ministries of agriculture, environment, finance, and planning; Practitioner Level; National government ministries, regional organizations like NEPAD, and development agencies working on climate policy implementation
Where it applies: countries: Ethiopia, Kenya, Tanzania, Uganda, Ghana; regions: Eastern Africa, Africa; context: None; scale: National
How it works: The training consists of eight modules delivered over three days, combining presentations, group discussions, and practical exercises to build understanding of NDCs, climate finance, monitoring systems, and stakeholder engagement in agricultural climate action.
Key steps: Module 1: Introduction to UNFCCC initiatives relevant to agriculture. Module 2: Understanding Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs). Module 3: Climate Smart Agriculture for transformative change. Module 4: Analysis and tools for priority setting in agriculture. Module 5: Climate finance (parts 1 and 2). Module 6: Role of private sector in NDC development and implementation. Module 7: Monitoring NDC implementation (parts 1 and 2)
Expected outcomes: Enhanced capacity among ministry staff for NDC implementation, concrete action plans for next 6-12 months, improved understanding of climate finance and MRV systems; Better implementation of agricultural components in national climate commitments, improved coordination between ministries, increased private sector engagement
Key takeaways: Climate finance, private sector engagement, and MRV systems were identified as the most valuable learning areas by participants. Group exercises reviewing country NDCs were particularly useful as some participants were unfamiliar with their own country's commitments. Key MRV challenges identified were capacity, data, funding, and coordination - with specific solutions proposed for each. Participants committed to concrete actions including greater private sector involvement and improved gender integration in NDCs. The training model is being replicated in other African regions, indicating its effectiveness and scalability
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: None
Publisher: CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)

summary_snippet: This training manual documents a farmer business school model that links smallholder farmers producing indigenous vegetables to school feeding programs in Busia County, Kenya. The approach addresses malnutrition and limited market access for nutrient-rich indigenous crops by creating sustainable pro...
bm25_text:
Title: Linking farmers, indigenous vegetables and schools to improve diets and nutrition in Busia County, Kenya
Resource type: Training Manual
EE pillars: M&E & Learning, Market Systems
Thematic areas: Nutrition & Health, Value Chains, Biodiversity
Overview: This training manual documents a farmer business school model that links smallholder farmers producing indigenous vegetables to school feeding programs in Busia County, Kenya. The approach addresses malnutrition and limited market access for nutrient-rich indigenous crops by creating sustainable procurement relationships between farmer groups and educational institutions.
Audience: Development practitioners, agricultural extension workers, school feeding program managers, and farmer group facilitators; Practitioner Level; Agricultural research organizations, NGOs, government agencies implementing school feeding programs, farmer cooperatives
Where it applies: countries: Kenya; regions: Eastern Africa; context: Areas suitable for indigenous vegetable production with existing school feeding programs; scale: Community
How it works: The model uses a three-pillar framework linking supply-side capacity building for farmers with demand-side awareness raising in schools. Farmer Business Schools train groups on production, marketing, and business management while nutrition education activities promote acceptance of indigenous vegetables in institutional markets.
Key steps: Train farmer groups using adapted Farmer Business School model on production, processing, handling, marketing and financial management. Conduct nutrition education and cooking demonstrations in schools, health centers and communities. Facilitate negotiations and memorandums of understanding between farmer groups and schools. Provide nutritional composition analysis of indigenous vegetables to support marketing. Organize field days and awareness events to promote local foods. Establish monitoring systems to track supply contracts and outcomes. Scale successful partnerships to additional farmer groups and institutions
Expected outcomes: Trained farmer groups, established supply contracts, increased production and marketing of indigenous vegetables; Improved nutrition in schools, increased farmer incomes, enhanced food biodiversity conservation, strengthened local food systems
Key takeaways: Farmer Business School model effectively links smallholder farmers to institutional markets for indigenous vegetables. Nutritional data and education are critical for overcoming negative perceptions of indigenous vegetables. Successful implementation requires simultaneous capacity building on both supply and demand sides. Local procurement policies can support biodiversity conservation while improving nutrition outcomes. Scaling requires addressing infrastructure, water access, and stakeholder engagement challenges
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Areas suitable for indigenous vegetable production with existing school feeding programs
Publisher: Bioversity International

title: Diversification for climate change resilience: Participatory assessment of opportunities for diversifying agroecosystems
summary_snippet: A participatory assessment framework designed to help farming communities identify opportunities for diversifying their agroecosystems to strengthen climate change resilience. The framework guides communities through evaluating their current agricultural biodiversity and exploring diversification op...
bm25_text:
Title: Diversification for climate change resilience: Participatory assessment of opportunities for diversifying agroecosystems
Resource type: Framework
EE pillars: M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems, Biodiversity
Overview: A participatory assessment framework designed to help farming communities identify opportunities for diversifying their agroecosystems to strengthen climate change resilience. The framework guides communities through evaluating their current agricultural biodiversity and exploring diversification options at multiple levels - from species and varieties to landscape-scale interventions.
Audience: Farming communities, NGOs, extension agents, development practitioners; Community/Farmer Level; NGOs, extension services, development organizations working with smallholder farmers
Where it applies: countries: Guatemala, India, Mali; regions: Central America, Southern Asia, Western Africa; context: Diverse agroecosystems including milpa systems, millet-based systems, rice-based systems; scale: Community
How it works: A four-step participatory assessment that engages communities to understand climate impacts, evaluate current agricultural diversity, and identify diversification opportunities at species, farm, and landscape levels.
Key steps: Understanding local experiences of climate change through timelines and seasonal calendars. Evaluating species, varieties, and breeds for climate resilience traits. Assessing field and farm diversification practices like intercropping and agroforestry. Evaluating landscape-level land uses for climate buffering and resource provision
Expected outcomes: Climate change timeline, seasonal calendar, species/variety trait evaluations, cropping system assessments, land use evaluations; Enhanced climate resilience through targeted diversification strategies, improved harvest security, better adaptation to climate variability
Key takeaways: Diversification at multiple levels (species, farm, landscape) is essential for climate resilience. Local communities possess valuable knowledge about climate-adapted varieties and practices. Participatory assessment can reveal both opportunities and barriers for agricultural diversification. Traditional and neglected crops often have important climate resilience traits. Supporting diversification requires addressing market, institutional, and resource access barriers
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Diverse agroecosystems including milpa systems, millet-based systems, rice-based systems
Publisher: Bioversity International and Platform for Agrobiodiversity Research

title: CLIFF-LAMNET Summer School Course
summary_snippet: The CLIFF-LAMNET Summer School Course is an intensive 5-day training program focused on climate change mitigation through innovative measurements, models, and policies in agricultural systems. The course covers greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions from soils, livestock, and rice systems, along with method...
bm25_text:
Title: CLIFF-LAMNET Summer School Course
Resource type: Training Manual
EE pillars: M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Livestock, Agri-Food Systems
Overview: The CLIFF-LAMNET Summer School Course is an intensive 5-day training program focused on climate change mitigation through innovative measurements, models, and policies in agricultural systems. The course covers greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions from soils, livestock, and rice systems, along with methodologies for quantification and strategies for developing low-emission production systems.
Audience: Researchers, technical specialists, and practitioners working on climate change mitigation in agricultural systems; Technical/Expert Level; Research institutions, agricultural development organizations, policy agencies
Where it applies: countries: Colombia; regions: Latin America; context: Tropical agricultural systems including livestock, rice, and mixed farming systems; scale: Regional/Multi-country
How it works: Five-day intensive course combining theoretical presentations, hands-on practical sessions, and group discussions on GHG measurement methodologies and mitigation strategies in agricultural systems.
Key steps: Day 1: Understanding GHG sources and sinks in agriculture (soils, livestock, rice). Day 2: Learning quantification methodologies including in-vitro and static chamber techniques. Day 3: Using tools and models for GHG estimation and scaling methodologies. Day 4: Exploring low-emission development strategies, NAMA, and MRV systems. Day 5: Planning future work and applying learned methodologies
Expected outcomes: Enhanced individual and institutional capacity to monitor GHG emissions in agro-ecosystems, validated methodologies for GHG quantification; Development of competitive livestock production systems with low GHG emissions, improved technical capacity for climate change mitigation in agriculture
Key takeaways: Comprehensive approach covering multiple agricultural systems (soils, livestock, rice) and their GHG contributions. Combines theoretical knowledge with hands-on practical training in measurement methodologies. Integrates technical measurement approaches with policy frameworks like NAMA and MRV systems. Emphasizes scaling methodologies from field-level measurements to broader applications. Includes economic performance evaluation alongside environmental impact assessment
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Tropical agricultural systems including livestock, rice, and mixed farming systems
Publisher: International Center for Tropical Agriculture (CIAT)

summary_snippet: A case study documenting the collaboration between IITA and Olam coffee company in Uganda's Mt. Elgon region to develop climate-smart agricultural practices for coffee farmers through farmer segmentation and a 'Stepwise' investment pathway approach. The resource provides insights on private sector e...
bm25_text:
Title: Improving coffee sector Climate-Smart Awareness and decision-making
Resource type: Case Study
EE pillars: Market Systems
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Value Chains
Overview: A case study documenting the collaboration between IITA and Olam coffee company in Uganda's Mt. Elgon region to develop climate-smart agricultural practices for coffee farmers through farmer segmentation and a 'Stepwise' investment pathway approach. The resource provides insights on private sector engagement strategies and lessons learned from implementing climate resilience solutions in coffee supply chains.
Audience: Development practitioners, private sector companies, and researchers working on climate-smart agriculture and supply chain resilience; Practitioner Level; Agricultural companies, development organizations, research institutes, and NGOs engaged in smallholder farmer support
Where it applies: countries: Uganda; regions: East Africa; context: Coffee growing regions, specifically Arabica coffee production areas like Mt. Elgon; scale: Community
How it works: The approach combines farmer segmentation based on resources and entrepreneurship levels with a Stepwise investment pathway that breaks down climate-smart practices into incremental, affordable steps. Farmers are grouped into segments and follow tailored pathways that use income from each step to fund the next practice.
Key steps: Conduct farmer segmentation to understand diversity in resources and entrepreneurship. Develop Stepwise investment pathways tailored to farmer segments. Establish demonstration plots for climate-smart practices. Provide training through farmer field schools. Monitor impact on yield, pest/disease pressure, and soil nutrition. Scale successful approaches to broader farmer groups. Facilitate learning exchanges between different regions
Expected outcomes: Farmer segmentation profiles, tailored Stepwise investment pathways, demonstration plots showing incremental improvements, trained farmer groups; Increased adoption of climate-smart practices, improved coffee yield and quality, enhanced farmer resilience to climate change, more efficient use of company extension resources
Key takeaways: Farmer segmentation helps companies allocate extension resources more efficiently by matching training to farmers' specific resources and motivations. Stepwise investment pathways make climate-smart practices more affordable by breaking them into incremental steps funded by previous gains. Private sector engagement requires framing projects in terms of company objectives (supply security, cost savings) rather than development goals. Co-funding arrangements help offset company risk aversion while enabling innovation in sustainability practices. Technology solutions like mobile apps can serve as effective entry points for deeper partnerships on climate resilience
Development stage: Piloted
Agro-ecological context: Coffee growing regions, specifically Arabica coffee production areas like Mt. Elgon
Publisher: International Institute of Tropical Agriculture

title: Improving Cocoa sector Climate-Smart Awareness and decision-making
summary_snippet: This case study documents lessons from private sector engagement in the USAID Learning Community for Supply Chain Resilience, focusing on how cocoa companies in Ghana leveraged climate science tools including climate suitability maps and the Climate Smart Cocoa Manual to improve farmer adaptation an...
bm25_text:
Title: Improving Cocoa sector Climate-Smart Awareness and decision-making
Resource type: Case Study
EE pillars: Market Systems
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems
Overview: This case study documents lessons from private sector engagement in the USAID Learning Community for Supply Chain Resilience, focusing on how cocoa companies in Ghana leveraged climate science tools including climate suitability maps and the Climate Smart Cocoa Manual to improve farmer adaptation and decision-making in response to climate change threats.
Audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, and private sector companies working on climate-smart agriculture and supply chain resilience; Practitioner Level; Agricultural commodity companies, development organizations, research institutions, and government agencies
Where it applies: countries: Ghana; regions: West Africa; context: Cocoa growing zones with varying climate vulnerability; scale: National
How it works: The initiative engaged private sector cocoa companies through convenings and provided them with science-based decision-support tools including climate suitability maps and zone-specific practice recommendations to help target climate-smart investments and farmer training programs.
Key steps: Conduct climate vulnerability mapping using machine learning models. Develop geographically specific climate-smart practice recommendations. Create Climate Smart Cocoa Manual with zone-specific guidance. Engage companies through sector-wide workshops and training sessions. Support companies in integrating tools into their farmer programs. Pilot and validate recommendations through demonstration plots. Monitor adoption and provide ongoing technical support
Expected outcomes: Climate suitability maps, Climate Smart Cocoa Manual, Tree Registration Guide, trained company staff and extension agents; Increased adoption of climate-smart practices by cocoa farmers, improved farm productivity and resilience, reduced deforestation
Key takeaways: Private sector engagement is most effective when providing concrete, actionable tools rather than just convenings. Zone-specific climate recommendations are more valuable than standardized approaches for targeting company investments. Working through industry organizations like World Cocoa Foundation improves coordination and reduces confusion. Companies need cost-benefit analysis of climate-smart practices to make business cases to farmers. Farmer-friendly materials and financing solutions are critical gaps that need addressing for successful implementation
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Cocoa growing zones with varying climate vulnerability
Publisher: International Institute of Tropical Agriculture

title: Improving Grain sector Climate-Smart Awareness and decision-making
summary_snippet: This case study documents how Nile Breweries Limited (NBL) in Uganda used the Guide to Assessing Climate Resilience in Smallholder Supply Chains to understand climate risks and build resilience in their sorghum and barley supply chains. It demonstrates private sector engagement in climate-smart agri...
bm25_text:
Title: Improving Grain sector Climate-Smart Awareness and decision-making
Resource type: Case Study
EE pillars: M&E & Learning, Market Systems
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Value Chains, Agri-Food Systems
Overview: This case study documents how Nile Breweries Limited (NBL) in Uganda used the Guide to Assessing Climate Resilience in Smallholder Supply Chains to understand climate risks and build resilience in their sorghum and barley supply chains. It demonstrates private sector engagement in climate-smart agriculture through a partnership between NBL, TechnoServe, and the Sustainable Food Lab under the USAID Learning Community for Supply Chain Resilience.
Audience: Private sector companies working with smallholder farmers, development practitioners, and organizations seeking to assess climate resilience in agricultural supply chains; Practitioner Level; Private companies, NGOs, development organizations, and research institutions
Where it applies: countries: Uganda; regions: Sub-Saharan Africa; context: Highland areas between 900-1500m for sorghum and 2000-2700m for barley; scale: National
How it works: The case demonstrates application of the Guide to Assessing Climate Resilience in Smallholder Supply Chains through a five-step process: know your risk, know your farmers, know your resilience, know how to build resilience, and know your progress. The approach involved field work, farmer interviews, and resilience capacity mapping.
Key steps: Know your risk - assess climate risks affecting supply chains. Know your farmers - understand farmer populations and their perceived climate risks. Know your resilience - map resilience capacities using Sustainable Livelihoods Framework. Know how to build resilience - identify specific intervention strategies. Know your progress - track implementation and outcomes
Expected outcomes: Climate risk assessment, resilience capacity mapping, prioritized intervention strategies for building supply chain resilience; Improved climate resilience of smallholder supply chains, better informed investment decisions, enhanced farmer livelihoods and productivity
Key takeaways: Initiatives to improve smallholder livelihoods should aim to reduce farmers' risk as well as increase their incomes. Supply chain improvements can be made through an 'ecosystem approach' even when companies don't source directly from smallholders. Long-term trusted relationships between partners are crucial for successful private sector engagement in climate-smart agriculture. Having both global team champions and influential local leads is critical for uptake and success of climate resilience initiatives. Quantified data from assessment tools helps company champions make business cases for climate investments to decision-makers
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Highland areas between 900-1500m for sorghum and 2000-2700m for barley
Publisher: International Institute of Tropical Agriculture

title: Coffee in the Age of Climate Change: Risks and Potential Impacts
summary_snippet: This is a comprehensive reference document that summarizes research on climate change impacts on global coffee production, providing risk assessments and adaptation strategies for key coffee-growing regions. It synthesizes findings from multiple technical partners to help agricultural finance practi...
bm25_text:
Title: Coffee in the Age of Climate Change: Risks and Potential Impacts
Resource type: Framework
EE pillars: Climate Resilience
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems, Value Chains
Overview: This is a comprehensive reference document that summarizes research on climate change impacts on global coffee production, providing risk assessments and adaptation strategies for key coffee-growing regions. It synthesizes findings from multiple technical partners to help agricultural finance practitioners understand projected climate risks and potential responses for coffee farmers and businesses.
Audience: Agricultural finance practitioners, development organizations, and coffee value chain stakeholders; Practitioner Level; Agricultural finance institutions, development organizations, coffee cooperatives and businesses
Where it applies: countries: Honduras, Mexico, Nicaragua, Colombia, Peru, Uganda, Indonesia, Brazil, El Salvador, Guatemala, Ethiopia, Tanzania, Vietnam, India; regions: Latin America, East Africa, Asia-Pacific, Mesoamerica, South America; context: Coffee-growing regions globally, particularly focusing on Arabica and Robusta production zones; scale: Regional/Multi-country
How it works: Uses climate modeling data (CMIP5 ensemble) to assess future suitability changes for coffee production, categorizes risk levels using a 'stoplight' system, and provides region-specific risk profiles with adaptation recommendations.
Key steps: Assess projected climate changes using ensemble climate models. Map suitability changes using tiered threat framework (opportunity, cope, adapt, uncertainty, transform zones). Identify region-specific climate hazards and impacts. Recommend adaptation practices based on risk level. Consider diversification or transition strategies for unsuitable areas
Expected outcomes: Risk maps, region-specific climate impact assessments, adaptation strategy recommendations; Improved climate resilience planning for coffee farmers and value chain actors, informed investment decisions in agricultural finance
Key takeaways: Global coffee production faces severe climate threats, with Arabica area potentially declining 49% and Robusta 54% by 2050. Latin America will be most negatively impacted while East Africa will see relatively less severe impacts. Suitable coffee production zones will migrate upslope by 200-400m, but higher elevation land may not be available or accessible. Adaptation strategies include agroforestry, improved varieties, and stepwise implementation approaches for resource-constrained farmers. Areas becoming unsuitable for coffee will need diversification or transition to alternative crops like cocoa or Robusta varieties
Development stage: Widely Deployed
Agro-ecological context: Coffee-growing regions globally, particularly focusing on Arabica and Robusta production zones
Publisher: Root Capital

title: Probabilistic assessment of investment options in honey value chains in Lamu county, Kenya
summary_snippet: This resource presents the Stochastic Impact Evaluation (SIE) approach for assessing investment decisions in honey value chains in Kenya. It demonstrates how to use probabilistic modeling and Monte Carlo simulation to evaluate uncertain investment outcomes, identify critical variables, and determine...
bm25_text:
Title: Probabilistic assessment of investment options in honey value chains in Lamu county, Kenya
Resource type: Framework
EE pillars: M&E & Learning, Market Systems
Thematic areas: Value Chains, Biodiversity
Overview: This resource presents the Stochastic Impact Evaluation (SIE) approach for assessing investment decisions in honey value chains in Kenya. It demonstrates how to use probabilistic modeling and Monte Carlo simulation to evaluate uncertain investment outcomes, identify critical variables, and determine the value of additional information for decision-making in biodiversity-based value chain interventions.
Audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, and project implementers making investment decisions under uncertainty; Technical/Expert Level; Development agencies, conservation organizations, agricultural intervention planners
Where it applies: countries: Kenya; regions: Eastern Africa; context: Biodiversity conservation areas along Kenya-Somalia border; scale: Community
How it works: The SIE approach uses Monte Carlo simulation to project decision outcomes by incorporating all relevant variables with their uncertainties expressed as probability distributions. It identifies critical uncertainties and calculates the Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI) to prioritize which variables are most valuable to measure.
Key steps: Define the decision question clearly. Develop conceptual model with all relevant variables. Conduct baseline survey and stakeholder consultations. Populate model with probability distributions for variables. Run Monte Carlo simulation to project outcomes. Use Partial Least Squares regression to identify important variables. Calculate Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI) for critical variables
Expected outcomes: Probability distributions of projected outcomes, identification of critical variables, value of information analysis, risk assessment; Improved decision-making for agricultural interventions under uncertainty, reduced chance of project failure, cost-effective measurement campaigns
Key takeaways: SIE approach enables holistic analysis of investment decisions even with uncertain and missing information. Value of Information analysis can substantially reduce costs of data collection by prioritizing only decision-relevant measurements. Tailoring interventions to local contexts is critical - different communities require different investment strategies. Clear definition of the decision question is essential and should receive significant attention in the planning process. The approach provides a clear business case for or against investment in projects with uncertain outcomes
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Biodiversity conservation areas along Kenya-Somalia border
Publisher: World Agroforestry Centre (ICRAF)

title: Sustainable and Equitable Growth in Farmer-led Irrigation in Sub-Saharan Africa: What Will it Take?
summary_snippet: This academic paper analyzes the potential and challenges of farmer-led irrigation in sub-Saharan Africa, examining how small-scale, farmer-initiated irrigation systems can increase agricultural productivity and incomes while highlighting significant equity and sustainability concerns that must be a...
bm25_text:
Title: Sustainable and Equitable Growth in Farmer-led Irrigation in Sub-Saharan Africa: What Will it Take?
Resource type: Academic Paper
EE pillars: Policy & Regulatory, Gender Equality & Social Inclusion, Market Systems, Financial Services, M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Water & Irrigation, Agri-Food Systems, Climate Resilience
Overview: This academic paper analyzes the potential and challenges of farmer-led irrigation in sub-Saharan Africa, examining how small-scale, farmer-initiated irrigation systems can increase agricultural productivity and incomes while highlighting significant equity and sustainability concerns that must be addressed for inclusive growth.
Audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders working on agricultural water management; Technical/Expert Level; Research institutions, development organizations, government agencies, NGOs focused on agricultural development
Where it applies: countries: ; regions: Sub-Saharan Africa; context: Smallholder farming systems with potential for irrigation development; scale: Regional/Multi-country
How it works: Farmer-led irrigation is defined as irrigation initiated, managed and financed by farmers themselves, mostly by individuals but sometimes in small groups, typically covering small areas (less than 2 ha) using low-cost technologies for both high-value horticultural crops and staple crops.
Expected outcomes: Increased agricultural productivity, expanded irrigated areas, diversified crop production including high-value horticultural crops; Improved incomes, poverty alleviation, employment generation, enhanced nutrition, greater resilience to seasonal weather variability and climatic shocks
Key takeaways: Farmer-led irrigation has significant potential to improve productivity and incomes but current growth patterns are inequitable, primarily benefiting wealthier male farmers. Key barriers include high upfront costs, limited access to appropriate financing, underdeveloped supply chains, and inadequate information services. Women face particular disadvantages due to limited decision-making power, restricted access to technologies and credit, and high labor requirements. Sustainability risks include groundwater depletion, water pollution from agrochemicals, and lack of collective resource management institutions. Achieving inclusive growth requires contextualised interventions addressing finance, supply chains, gender equity, environmental sustainability, and institutional governance
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Smallholder farming systems with potential for irrigation development
Publisher: Water Alternatives

title: Gender analysis in farming systems and action research: A training manual
summary_snippet: This is a comprehensive training manual for conducting gender analysis in farming systems and action research, developed for the Africa RISING program. It provides facilitators and researchers with practical tools, concepts, and step-by-step guidance for integrating gender perspectives into agricult...
bm25_text:
Title: Gender analysis in farming systems and action research: A training manual
Resource type: Training Manual
EE pillars: Gender Equality & Social Inclusion
Thematic areas: Agri-Food Systems
Overview: This is a comprehensive training manual for conducting gender analysis in farming systems and action research, developed for the Africa RISING program. It provides facilitators and researchers with practical tools, concepts, and step-by-step guidance for integrating gender perspectives into agricultural research and development activities.
Audience: Facilitators conducting gender analysis trainings, agricultural researchers, and development practitioners; Technical/Expert Level; International and national research institutes, universities, agricultural development organizations, and CGIAR centers
Where it applies: countries: Tanzania, Malawi, Ghana, Mali; regions: Africa, Eastern Africa, Southern Africa, Western Africa; context: Smallholder farming systems; scale: Community
How it works: The manual provides a structured 2-day training program based on action research, farming systems approach, and gender transformative principles. It combines theoretical concepts with practical tools through participatory learning methods.
Key steps: Introduction to research approaches and basic gender concepts. Learning gender roles, relations, and intersectionality concepts. Applying participatory tools like linkage diagrams and activity profiles. Using seasonal calendars and daily activity clocks for gender analysis. Implementing matrix scoring with Sustainable Intensification Assessment Framework. Developing gender-sensitive survey research principles. Evaluation and planning for application in participants' work
Expected outcomes: Participants gain skills in gender concepts, participatory analysis tools, and survey research principles for agricultural contexts; Enhanced capacity for gender mainstreaming in agricultural research and development projects
Key takeaways: Integrates action research, farming systems, and gender transformative approaches for comprehensive analysis. Provides practical participatory tools like linkage diagrams, activity profiles, and matrix scoring for field application. Emphasizes the importance of gender-separate groups and intersectional analysis beyond just male-female categories. Includes detailed facilitation guidance with step-by-step instructions and learning materials. Focuses on building sustainable capacity through follow-up support and application planning
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Smallholder farming systems
Publisher: International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA)

title: National food security act supports climate smart agriculture in India by stimulating the sourcing of small Millets: An evaluation of Bioversity’s efforts to promote small Millets in India
summary_snippet: This evaluation study examines the outcomes of Bioversity International's collaboration with the MS Swaminathan Research Foundation to promote minor millets in India through participatory research, conservation, and value chain development. The study analyzes the impact of interventions on millet pr...
bm25_text:
Title: National food security act supports climate smart agriculture in India by stimulating the sourcing of small Millets: An evaluation of Bioversity’s efforts to promote small Millets in India
Resource type: Report
EE pillars: M&E & Learning, Policy & Regulatory
Thematic areas: Agri-Food Systems, Biodiversity, Nutrition & Health, Climate Resilience
Overview: This evaluation study examines the outcomes of Bioversity International's collaboration with the MS Swaminathan Research Foundation to promote minor millets in India through participatory research, conservation, and value chain development. The study analyzes the impact of interventions on millet production, consumption, and income in two study locations over an 11-year period (2007-2018), while also examining the potential effects of India's National Food Security Act of 2013 which mandated millets as a scheduled commodity in the public distribution system.
Audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders working on neglected and underutilized species; Technical/Expert Level; International development organizations, research institutions, government agencies, and NGOs focused on agricultural biodiversity and food security
Where it applies: countries: India; regions: Southern Asia; context: Marginal production environments where millets are traditionally grown by limited resource farmers; scale: Community
How it works: The project used a holistic value chain approach targeting conservation and use of minor millets through participatory research, genetic conservation, agronomic improvements, seed production, and value addition activities. The approach integrated multiple stakeholders including farmers, self-help groups, research institutions, and policy makers.
Key steps: Survey and collection of millet genetic diversity in target communities. Participatory varietal selection with farmers to identify preferred varieties. Establishment and management of community seed banks. Training on improved agronomic practices and crop management. Development of small-scale processing technologies to reduce drudgery. Value-added product development and marketing training. Policy advocacy for inclusion of millets in national food security programs
Expected outcomes: Increased millet production diversity, improved seed quality, enhanced processing capabilities, strengthened value chains; Enhanced livelihoods of limited resource farmers, improved nutrition security, conservation of genetic resources, climate resilience
Key takeaways: Participatory approaches successfully increased millet production diversity and farmer adoption of improved practices. While millet cultivation expanded, the economic contribution to household income remained modest compared to other crops and income sources. Policy achievements like the National Food Security Act create potential for increased demand but implementation remains limited. Community seed banks and self-help groups proved effective mechanisms for technology transfer and capacity building. Future scaling of millet production through government procurement may not necessarily benefit traditional small-scale producers
Development stage: Widely Deployed
Agro-ecological context: Marginal production environments where millets are traditionally grown by limited resource farmers
Publisher: Bioversity International

title: Building resilience through 'Open Source Seed Systems' for climate change adaptation in Kenya, Uganda, and Tanzania: What are the options for policy?
summary_snippet: This policy brief presents recommendations for establishing Open Source Seed Systems (OSSS) in Kenya, Uganda, and Tanzania to enhance climate resilience and food security. It addresses the gap between formal seed systems that serve only 20% of farmers' needs and farmer-managed seed systems that lack...
bm25_text:
Title: Building resilience through 'Open Source Seed Systems' for climate change adaptation in Kenya, Uganda, and Tanzania: What are the options for policy?
Resource type: Policy Brief
EE pillars: Policy & Regulatory
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Biodiversity, Agri-Food Systems
Overview: This policy brief presents recommendations for establishing Open Source Seed Systems (OSSS) in Kenya, Uganda, and Tanzania to enhance climate resilience and food security. It addresses the gap between formal seed systems that serve only 20% of farmers' needs and farmer-managed seed systems that lack policy support, proposing policy reforms to enable free access, use, and exchange of plant genetic materials.
Audience: Policy makers, government officials, and development practitioners working on seed systems and agricultural policy; Technical/Expert Level; Government agencies, international organizations, NGOs, and research institutions involved in agricultural policy development
Where it applies: countries: Kenya, Tanzania, Uganda; regions: Eastern Africa; context: Smallholder farming systems in East Africa; scale: National
How it works: OSSS allows freedom of access and use of plant genetic material, prohibiting exclusive rights and applying this to subsequent derivatives. It requires legal or ethical commitment to maintain freedom to use seed and derivatives through Material Transfer Agreements.
Key steps: Recognize farmer-managed seed varieties in policy frameworks. Develop policies facilitating functional open source seed systems. Establish farmer-inclusive intellectual property rights regimes. Create institutional coordination mechanisms. Promote research and development for OSSS. Mobilize resources for OSSS development
Expected outcomes: Policy frameworks recognizing farmer varieties, simplified registration procedures, protected farmers' rights to save and exchange seeds; Enhanced access to diverse seeds, increased agricultural biodiversity, improved climate resilience, and food security for smallholder farmers
Key takeaways: Only 20% of seeds used by East African farmers come from formal systems, yet policies primarily support formal seed production. Current intellectual property rights and plant variety protection laws restrict farmers' traditional practices of saving, sharing, and exchanging seeds. OSSS can address gaps in seed access by ensuring freedom of use and exchange while maintaining commercial viability. Policy reforms must recognize farmer varieties and domesticate provisions of international treaties like ITPGRFA. Successful implementation requires institutional coordination, research investment, and awareness creation among stakeholders
Development stage: Concept/Research
Agro-ecological context: Smallholder farming systems in East Africa
Publisher: Bioversity International

title: Behaviour Change Scale-Up in Market Systems Development: A literature review
summary_snippet: This literature review examines behavior change scale-up processes in Market Systems Development (MSD) programs, analyzing how promising interventions and institutional innovations in market systems become accepted and used by large numbers of people. It bridges MSD approaches with Diffusion of Inno...
bm25_text:
Title: Behaviour Change Scale-Up in Market Systems Development: A literature review
Resource type: Academic Paper
EE pillars: M&E & Learning, Market Systems, Scaling Innovations
Thematic areas: Scaling Innovations, Value Chains
Overview: This literature review examines behavior change scale-up processes in Market Systems Development (MSD) programs, analyzing how promising interventions and institutional innovations in market systems become accepted and used by large numbers of people. It bridges MSD approaches with Diffusion of Innovations theory to identify key factors and strategies that contribute to successful scaling of new behaviors and practices in market systems.
Audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders working on market systems development; Technical/Expert Level; Development organizations, research institutions, donor agencies implementing MSD programs
Where it applies: countries: ; regions: ; context: None; scale: Global
How it works: The review uses Diffusion of Innovations (DOI) theory as a reference framework to analyze MSD programs and identify factors that contribute to behavior change scale-up. It synthesizes literature to develop a six-domain model covering Design, Perceptions, Business Model, Relationships, Communications, and Capacity.
Key steps: Conduct desk-based literature review of MSD, diffusion of innovations, and behavior change fields. Identify scale-up strategies used by MSD programs. Apply DOI theory framework to assess relevance of factors. Categorize findings into six domains with 13 key factors. Compare MSD practices with DOI components to identify gaps. Develop recommendations for future research and practice
Expected outcomes: Six-domain framework with 13 factors for behavior change scale-up in MSD programs; Improved understanding of how to generate lasting behavior change in market systems development programs
Key takeaways: MSD programs use five scale-up strategies: network-driven, firm-driven, policy improvement, quality standards, and supporting functions/BDS. Six key domains influence behavior change scale-up: Design, Perceptions, Business Model, Relationships, Communications, and Capacity. MSD programs are increasingly aware of behavior change importance but need more systematic application of behavioral science. The demonstration effect is not as powerful as many programs assume - additional interventions are needed for crowding-in. More research is needed on stakeholder innovativeness, network structures, and strategic facilitation approaches
Development stage: Concept/Research
Agro-ecological context: None
Publisher: CGIAR Research Program on Policies, Institutions, and Markets (PIM)

title: Understand, Desing, Act: Climate-proof your supply chain
summary_snippet: A comprehensive learning series and toolkit designed to help coffee sector stakeholders understand, assess, and implement climate-smart agriculture (CSA) practices to climate-proof their supply chains. The resource provides a structured 7-module approach covering climate risk assessment, adaptation ...
bm25_text:
Title: Understand, Desing, Act: Climate-proof your supply chain
Resource type: Training Manual
EE pillars: Market Systems, M&E & Learning, Financial Services
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems, Scaling Innovations
Overview: A comprehensive learning series and toolkit designed to help coffee sector stakeholders understand, assess, and implement climate-smart agriculture (CSA) practices to climate-proof their supply chains. The resource provides a structured 7-module approach covering climate risk assessment, adaptation strategies, scaling mechanisms, monitoring tools, and business case development for CSA investments.
Audience: Coffee sector stakeholders including roasters, traders, producer organizations, development practitioners, and policymakers; Practitioner Level; Coffee companies, cooperatives, NGOs, development organizations, and government agencies working in coffee supply chains
Where it applies: countries: Honduras, Uganda, Guatemala, Tanzania, Vietnam, Brazil, Colombia, Peru, Ethiopia, Kenya, Rwanda; regions: Central America, East Africa, Southeast Asia, South America; context: Coffee-producing regions facing climate variability and change; scale: Regional/Multi-country
How it works: The approach follows a three-phase framework: Understand (climate risks and impacts), Design (appropriate CSA interventions), and Act (implement and scale solutions). It uses triangulation methods combining scientific data with farmer knowledge to develop context-specific adaptation plans.
Key steps: Understand climate change impacts on coffee production. Assess supply chain climate risks using risk profiles and vulnerability tools. Identify appropriate CSA practices, strategies, and enablers for context. Design scaling pathways including financing mechanisms. Implement monitoring and evaluation systems. Build business case for CSA investments. Establish collaborative action at origin
Expected outcomes: Climate risk profiles, vulnerability assessments, adaptation plans, trained extension agents, demonstration plots, and monitoring systems; Increased adoption of climate-smart practices, improved farm resilience, enhanced supply chain sustainability, and reduced climate vulnerability
Key takeaways: Climate change impacts on coffee are already occurring and require immediate action through tailored adaptation strategies. Successful CSA implementation requires understanding local climate risks, farmer capacity, and appropriate financing mechanisms. Scaling CSA requires multiple pathways including direct services, collaboration, and sector-level catalytic action. Effective monitoring combines farm-level indicators with landscape-level assessments to track resilience building. Business case development must connect climate risks to core business concerns like supply security, quality, and reputation
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Coffee-producing regions facing climate variability and change
Publisher: Global Coffee Platform and Alliance for Resilient Coffee

title: Climate-Smart Agriculture in Cocoa: A Training Manual for Field Officers
summary_snippet: A comprehensive training manual for field officers to teach cocoa farmers climate-smart agriculture practices. The manual provides zone-specific recommendations across Ghana's three climate impact zones (coping/opportunity, adjustment, and transformation) with four adaptation levels (minimum, bronze...
bm25_text:
Title: Climate-Smart Agriculture in Cocoa: A Training Manual for Field Officers
Resource type: Training Manual
EE pillars: Climate Resilience
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems
Overview: A comprehensive training manual for field officers to teach cocoa farmers climate-smart agriculture practices. The manual provides zone-specific recommendations across Ghana's three climate impact zones (coping/opportunity, adjustment, and transformation) with four adaptation levels (minimum, bronze, silver, gold) covering eight key topics from establishment to resilience building.
Audience: Field officers and extension agents training cocoa farmers; Practitioner Level; Agricultural extension services, NGOs, development organizations working with cocoa farmers
Where it applies: countries: Ghana; regions: West Africa; context: Cocoa-growing regions with varying climate change impacts; scale: Community
How it works: Uses the SDF (Set up, Delivery, Finish) training model with zone-specific climate-smart agriculture recommendations. Trainers deliver structured sessions covering eight technical topics with hands-on demonstrations and exercises tailored to local climate impact zones.
Key steps: Assess local climate impact zone (coping/opportunity, adjustment, or transformation). Determine farmer adaptation level (minimum, bronze, silver, gold). Deliver topic-specific training using SDF model structure. Provide zone-appropriate recommendations for each agricultural practice. Conduct field demonstrations and practical exercises. Support farmers in implementing climate-smart practices. Monitor and adjust practices based on local conditions
Expected outcomes: Trained field officers capable of delivering climate-smart agriculture training to cocoa farmers across different climate zones; Increased farmer adoption of climate-smart practices leading to improved cocoa productivity and resilience to climate change
Key takeaways: Climate-smart agriculture requires zone-specific approaches based on predicted climate impacts. Four-level adaptation framework allows farmers to choose practices matching their capacity and willingness to invest. Comprehensive approach covers entire cocoa production cycle from establishment to post-harvest and resilience building. Integration of traditional knowledge with scientific recommendations enhances farmer adoption. Community-level approaches and landscape management are essential for effective climate adaptation
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Cocoa-growing regions with varying climate change impacts
Publisher: World Cocoa Foundation and Rainforest Alliance

title: Modelos de Predicción para Mejores Decisiones
summary_snippet: A comprehensive modeling platform developed by CIAT that uses agroclimatic, crop, livestock, and socio-economic models to help farmers and decision-makers make better agricultural decisions in the face of climate variability and change. The platform integrates seasonal climate forecasting with crop ...
bm25_text:
Title: Modelos de Predicción para Mejores Decisiones
Resource type: Tool
EE pillars: M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems
Overview: A comprehensive modeling platform developed by CIAT that uses agroclimatic, crop, livestock, and socio-economic models to help farmers and decision-makers make better agricultural decisions in the face of climate variability and change. The platform integrates seasonal climate forecasting with crop modeling to provide actionable information for agricultural planning and risk management.
Audience: Farmers, agricultural decision-makers, researchers, and development practitioners; Practitioner Level; Agricultural organizations, research institutions, government agencies, and farmer cooperatives
Where it applies: countries: Colombia, Honduras, Kenya, Brazil; regions: Latin America, Asia, Africa; context: Multiple crop systems including rice, beans, maize, banana, coffee, cassava, and livestock; scale: Regional/Multi-country
How it works: Integrates agroclimatic modeling, crop and livestock modeling, and socio-economic modeling to create seasonal forecasts and long-term climate projections. Uses interdisciplinary research team to develop and implement modeling tools that predict climate, crop, and livestock behavior.
Key steps: Generate agroclimatic models using tools like CPT, R-CPT, and global/regional climate models. Apply crop and livestock models (ORYZA, DSSAT, GLAM, MANIHOT) with climate data. Integrate socio-economic modeling (IMPACT, Surplus, GTAP) to show economic scenarios. Provide seasonal forecasts and climate projections through platforms like AClimateColombia. Deliver information to farmers through various channels and platforms. Evaluate risks using participatory methods combined with crop and climate models. Support decision-making at farm, sub-national, national and regional levels
Expected outcomes: Seasonal climate forecasts, crop yield predictions, economic scenario projections, and risk assessments delivered through web platforms and agricultural extension services; Improved agricultural decision-making, reduced crop losses, enhanced climate resilience, and better adaptation to climate change
Key takeaways: Integrates multiple modeling approaches (climate, crop, livestock, socio-economic) to provide comprehensive agricultural decision support. Demonstrates significant economic impact with documented avoided losses and leveraged investments. Operates at multiple scales from individual farms to regional level across Latin America, Asia, and Africa. Combines cutting-edge modeling science with practical delivery through web platforms and extension services. Has received multiple UN awards recognizing innovative use of Big Data and seasonal forecasting for agricultural applications
Development stage: Widely Deployed
Agro-ecological context: Multiple crop systems including rice, beans, maize, banana, coffee, cassava, and livestock
Publisher: International Center for Tropical Agriculture (CIAT)

title: Putting Climate Information in the Hands of Farmers
summary_snippet: This resource describes climate information services that deliver site-specific, reliable climate information to farmers to enable better informed farming decisions. It focuses on Local Technical Agro-climatic Committees (LTACs) and Participatory Climate Services (PICSA) as innovative approaches to ...
bm25_text:
Title: Putting Climate Information in the Hands of Farmers
Resource type: Program Description
EE pillars: M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems
Overview: This resource describes climate information services that deliver site-specific, reliable climate information to farmers to enable better informed farming decisions. It focuses on Local Technical Agro-climatic Committees (LTACs) and Participatory Climate Services (PICSA) as innovative approaches to help farmers prepare for climate variability and reduce crop losses.
Audience: Farmers, farmer organizations, agricultural technicians, and national meteorological services; Community/Farmer Level; Farmer organizations, national meteorological services, climate outlook forums
Where it applies: countries: Colombia, Nicaragua, Honduras, Guatemala, Rwanda, Chile, Cambodia, Myanmar, Vietnam, Lao PDR; regions: Latin America, Africa, Southeast Asia; context: Areas where climate is predictable and crops are highly sensitive to climate; scale: Regional/Multi-country
How it works: Climate services generate, translate, transfer, and use climate knowledge to provide site-specific, reliable climate information to farmers in a demand-driven and timely manner. Local Technical Agro-climatic Committees facilitate open dialogues about seasonal climate forecasts and help devise measures to reduce crop losses.
Key steps: Establish Local Technical Agro-climatic Committees (LTACs). Generate seasonal climate forecasts. Translate climate information for agricultural context. Facilitate dialogues between stakeholders about climate variations. Provide planting recommendations and variety selection guidance. Deliver timely, site-specific information to farmers. Monitor and evaluate outcomes
Expected outcomes: Site-specific climate information, planting recommendations, variety selection guidance, seasonal forecasts; Better informed farming decisions, reduced crop losses, improved resilience to climate variability, increased productivity
Key takeaways: Local Technical Agro-climatic Committees (LTACs) provide innovative platforms for stakeholders to access and discuss seasonal climate forecasts. Site-specific, demand-driven climate information enables better farming decisions and can significantly reduce crop losses. Climate services work best in areas where climate is predictable and crops are highly climate-sensitive. Successful implementation requires partnerships between farmer organizations, meteorological services, and climate outlook forums. Evidence shows substantial economic benefits, with documented cases of avoided losses and increased productivity
Development stage: Widely Deployed
Agro-ecological context: Areas where climate is predictable and crops are highly sensitive to climate
Publisher: International Center for Tropical Agriculture (CIAT)

title: NAMA proposal on cattle production
summary_snippet: This is a NAMA (Nationally Appropriate Mitigation Action) proposal framework for sustainable cattle production in Colombia, designed to reduce greenhouse gas emissions from livestock while promoting sustainable productive landscapes. The proposal outlines a 9-month implementation strategy to develop...
bm25_text:
Title: NAMA proposal on cattle production
Resource type: Framework
EE pillars: Policy & Regulatory
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Livestock, Agri-Food Systems
Overview: This is a NAMA (Nationally Appropriate Mitigation Action) proposal framework for sustainable cattle production in Colombia, designed to reduce greenhouse gas emissions from livestock while promoting sustainable productive landscapes. The proposal outlines a 9-month implementation strategy to develop mitigation actions, establish baselines, and create monitoring systems for the livestock sector.
Audience: Government agencies, livestock federations, and development organizations implementing climate mitigation strategies; Technical/Expert Level; National governments, livestock associations, research centers, and international development organizations
Where it applies: countries: Colombia; regions: Latin America; context: Livestock production systems in tropical and subtropical regions; scale: National
How it works: The NAMA framework develops regional-level strategies to reduce GHG emissions from livestock production through improved farm management, enhanced carbon sinks in agro-ecosystems, and conservation of natural ecosystems. It involves establishing baselines, designing monitoring systems, and creating implementation plans.
Key steps: Select NAMA coordinator and describe sector importance. Analyze barriers and establish national/departmental GHG emissions baseline. Develop mitigation scenarios and prioritize regions for implementation. Calculate baseline emissions for slaughterhouses and livestock auctions. Create financial model and measurement, reporting, verification system. Develop implementation plan with logistics evaluation. Design scaling strategy for prioritized geographical regions
Expected outcomes: Four key documents: mitigation strategy, baseline and GHG scenarios, financial component, and implementation plan; 20% reduction in GHG emissions by 2030, increased carbon sinks in agro-ecosystems, conservation of natural ecosystems
Key takeaways: Provides structured approach to developing NAMAs for livestock sector climate mitigation. Integrates multiple stakeholders including government, private sector, and research organizations. Emphasizes regional-level implementation with national coordination. Combines emissions reduction with ecosystem conservation and sustainable production. Requires comprehensive baseline establishment and monitoring systems for effectiveness
Development stage: Concept/Research
Agro-ecological context: Livestock production systems in tropical and subtropical regions
Publisher: International Center for Tropical Agriculture (CIAT)

title: Activity Report: CSV Monitoring and Evaluation
summary_snippet: The CSV Monitoring and Evaluation Activity Report documents the development and piloting of an ICT-based CSA Calculator tool for farm-level monitoring across CCAFS Climate-Smart Villages (CSV). The tool quantitatively assesses climate-smartness of farms by linking indicators from the three CSA pilla...
bm25_text:
Title: Activity Report: CSV Monitoring and Evaluation
Resource type: Report
EE pillars: M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems
Overview: The CSV Monitoring and Evaluation Activity Report documents the development and piloting of an ICT-based CSA Calculator tool for farm-level monitoring across CCAFS Climate-Smart Villages (CSV). The tool quantitatively assesses climate-smartness of farms by linking indicators from the three CSA pillars (productivity, adaptation, mitigation) with farm resource analysis.
Audience: Development practitioners, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders implementing climate-smart agriculture monitoring; Technical/Expert Level; Research institutions, development organizations, agricultural extension services
Where it applies: countries: Colombia, Honduras, Nicaragua, Guatemala, Uganda, Nepal, Bangladesh, Ghana, Vietnam, India; regions: Latin America, West Africa, East Africa, South Asia, South East Asia; context: Diverse farming systems including coffee, sugar cane, maize, cassava, rice production with mixed crop-livestock systems; scale: Household
How it works: The CSA calculator uses a whole-farm modeling approach to assess trade-offs and synergies between CSA pillars through quantitative indicators. It was adapted from an Excel-based tool to an ICT application integrated into the CSV monitoring system.
Key steps: Calibrate tool parameters for specific country contexts and farming systems. Train supervisors and local enumerators on data collection methods. Collect farm-level data through structured surveys covering family size, CSA practices, crop areas, livestock, inputs, and prices. Calculate indicators for productivity (caloric ratio, fodder ratio, cost-benefit ratio), adaptation (biodiversity index, water balance, nutrient balance), and mitigation (CO2 emissions/sequestration). Analyze results using typology analysis to identify different adoption patterns. Compare climate-smartness across different farm types and CSA practice portfolios
Expected outcomes: Quantitative assessment of farm-level climate-smartness across three CSA pillars, typology of CSA adoption patterns, cross-site comparison of farming systems; Strengthened quantitative assessment of CSA at farm level in CSV monitoring, better understanding of CSA practice effectiveness and adoption patterns
Key takeaways: Whole-farm modeling approach enables assessment of synergies and trade-offs between CSA pillars at farm level. Tool successfully adapted across 9 countries with diverse farming systems, though complexity varies significantly between sites. Integration into ICT-based monitoring system requires careful attention to data standardization and enumerator training. Typology analysis reveals distinct adoption patterns linked to socio-economic characteristics and farm constraints. Land tenure arrangements significantly influence potential for land use changes and CSA implementation
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Diverse farming systems including coffee, sugar cane, maize, cassava, rice production with mixed crop-livestock systems
Publisher: CGIAR

title: Rainforest Alliance Sustainable Agriculture Standard: Applicable for Smallholders Farms
summary_snippet: The Rainforest Alliance Sustainable Agriculture Standard for Smallholder Farms is a comprehensive certification framework designed to maximize positive social, environmental, and economic impact in agricultural production. It provides smallholder farmers with an enhanced framework to improve their l...
bm25_text:
Title: Rainforest Alliance Sustainable Agriculture Standard: Applicable for Smallholders Farms
Resource type: Framework
EE pillars: M&E & Learning, Gender Equality & Social Inclusion, Policy & Regulatory
Thematic areas: Agri-Food Systems, Climate Resilience, Biodiversity, Value Chains
Overview: The Rainforest Alliance Sustainable Agriculture Standard for Smallholder Farms is a comprehensive certification framework designed to maximize positive social, environmental, and economic impact in agricultural production. It provides smallholder farmers with an enhanced framework to improve their livelihoods while protecting the landscapes where they live and work through a performance-driven approach that emphasizes continuous improvement.
Audience: Smallholder farmers and producer groups in tree crops, fruits, nuts, and cut flowers; Practitioner Level; Producer groups, cooperatives, associations, and supply chain actors managing smallholder certification
Where it applies: countries: ; regions: ; context: Global scope focusing on tree crops (coffee, cocoa, tea), fruits (bananas, coconuts, pineapples), nuts (hazelnuts), and cut flowers; scale: Community
How it works: The standard uses a performance-driven approach with core criteria for initial certification and improvement pathways for continuous progress. It combines prescriptive practices with outcome measurement through data collection and indicators.
Key steps: Conduct risk assessment using Rainforest Alliance tools. Implement core criteria across management, farming practices, social, and environmental areas. Establish internal management system and member administration. Commit to minimum 12 improvement topics (mandatory, context-specific, and self-selected). Collect and report data through indicators for monitoring progress. Undergo certification audit and continuous improvement cycles. Maintain traceability and premium transparency systems
Expected outcomes: Certified sustainable agricultural production, improved farm management systems, enhanced traceability, and documented continuous improvement; Improved farmer livelihoods, enhanced environmental protection, better working conditions, increased productivity and profitability, and progress toward living income
Key takeaways: Performance-driven approach combines core compliance criteria with flexible improvement pathways for continuous progress. Comprehensive coverage includes management, farming practices, social conditions, and environmental protection. Strong emphasis on data collection and indicator-based monitoring for evidence-based improvement. Differentiated requirements for smallholders versus medium/large producers to ensure relevance. Focus on assess-and-address approaches for sensitive issues like child labor, forced labor, and harassment rather than simple prohibition
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Global scope focusing on tree crops (coffee, cocoa, tea), fruits (bananas, coconuts, pineapples), nuts (hazelnuts), and cut flowers
Publisher: Rainforest Alliance

title: Summary: Climate-Smart Agriculture in the 2017 SAN Sustainable Agriculture Standard
summary_snippet: This document summarizes how the 2017 Sustainable Agriculture Network (SAN) Standard integrates climate-smart agriculture principles into its certification framework. It explains how the standard addresses climate change adaptation and mitigation in agricultural systems through three main principles...
bm25_text:
Title: Summary: Climate-Smart Agriculture in the 2017 SAN Sustainable Agriculture Standard
Resource type: Framework
EE pillars: Policy & Regulatory
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems, Biodiversity
Overview: This document summarizes how the 2017 Sustainable Agriculture Network (SAN) Standard integrates climate-smart agriculture principles into its certification framework. It explains how the standard addresses climate change adaptation and mitigation in agricultural systems through three main principles: effective planning and management, biodiversity conservation, and natural resource conservation.
Audience: Farmers, agricultural producers, and certification bodies; Practitioner Level; Agricultural producers, certification organizations, supply chain companies
Where it applies: countries: ; regions: ; context: Tropical and subtropical agricultural systems, particularly shade-tolerant crops like coffee and cocoa; scale: Community
How it works: The framework integrates climate-smart agriculture into three main principles: effective planning and management (including climate vulnerability assessments), biodiversity conservation (protecting native ecosystems), and natural resource conservation (improving soil health and reducing chemical inputs).
Key steps: Conduct ongoing climate vulnerability assessments. Develop action plans to address climate threats. Implement biodiversity conservation measures including native shade trees. Apply integrated pest management to reduce chemical pesticide use. Maintain or improve soil health and water management. Reduce energy and fossil fuel consumption. Protect existing forests and avoid deforestation
Expected outcomes: Climate-adaptive farm management plans, reduced chemical pesticide use, enhanced biodiversity conservation, improved soil health; Increased farm resilience to climate change, reduced greenhouse gas emissions, enhanced productivity and incomes, landscape-level environmental benefits
Key takeaways: The 2017 SAN Standard integrates climate-smart agriculture into its core principles rather than treating it as an optional add-on. Climate vulnerability assessments and adaptive planning are now mandatory requirements for certified farms. The framework addresses both climate adaptation (through biodiversity conservation and resource management) and mitigation (through reduced emissions and carbon sequestration). Smallholder and indigenous communities are particularly vulnerable to climate change and can benefit significantly from climate-smart approaches. Landscape-level benefits emerge when many producers adopt climate-smart practices across a region or supply chain
Development stage: Widely Deployed
Agro-ecological context: Tropical and subtropical agricultural systems, particularly shade-tolerant crops like coffee and cocoa
Publisher: Rainforest Alliance

How it works: The tool provides a tailored framework for calculating greenhouse gas emissions from cropland systems, with specific operational procedures designed for agricultural contexts.

Key takeaways: A new greenhouse gas calculation tool has been developed specifically for cropland applications. The tool addresses gaps in existing GHG measurement approaches for agricultural systems. Vietnam serves as a case study for demonstrating the tool's potential application. The framework is designed to be operational and practical for agricultural contexts
