title: Validation of CCAFS Outcomes for CIAT in the Philippines
summary_snippet: This is an external validation report that assesses the outcomes and impact of CIAT's collaborative climate research in the Philippines from 2016 onwards. The report validates three specific outcome cases related to the use of CIAT climate science in establishing climate-resilient agriculture villag...
bm25_text:
Title: Validation of CCAFS Outcomes for CIAT in the Philippines
Resource type: Report
EE pillars: M&E & Learning, Policy & Regulatory
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems, Scaling Innovations
Overview: This is an external validation report that assesses the outcomes and impact of CIAT's collaborative climate research in the Philippines from 2016 onwards. The report validates three specific outcome cases related to the use of CIAT climate science in establishing climate-resilient agriculture villages, formulating policy frameworks, and developing Green Climate Fund proposals.
Audience: CGIAR researchers, development practitioners, and policymakers involved in climate change adaptation and agricultural development; Technical/Expert Level; CGIAR centers, national government agencies, international organizations, academic institutions
Where it applies: countries: Philippines; regions: Asia, South-eastern Asia; context: Tropical agriculture systems vulnerable to climate change; scale: National
How it works: The validation used Outcome Harvesting methodology to identify, verify and analyze outcomes by gathering evidence from reports, interviews, and documents to determine how CIAT's climate research contributed to behavioral changes in next-users.
Key steps: Identify proposed outcomes from CIAT's work. Conduct in-depth interviews with stakeholder respondents. Review relevant project reports and documents. Validate and refine outcome statements based on third-party evidence. Assess changes in Knowledge, Attitudes, Skills and Practices (KAS+P). Analyze extent of utilization and foreseen future outcomes. Document evidence of policy and practice changes
Expected outcomes: Validated outcome statements documenting behavioral changes in next-users; Evidence of how CIAT climate science influenced policy frameworks, community-level interventions, and institutional practices
Key takeaways: CIAT's climate science successfully influenced policy frameworks at both national and sectoral levels in the Philippines. The CRVA framework proved effective for evidence-based spatial targeting of climate adaptation investments. Academic institutions demonstrated strong uptake of CIAT methodologies in research and publications. Regular communication and coordination with partners is critical for maximizing impact. Tools need to be adapted for local conditions and connectivity constraints in remote areas
Development stage: Widely Deployed
Agro-ecological context: Tropical agriculture systems vulnerable to climate change
Publisher: CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)

title: Progress Report: Challenges and opportunities for creating a climate-smart food system in the Philippines and Vietnam
summary_snippet: This is a progress report documenting a mixed-methods research study conducted by CIP and CCAFS to identify opportunities and challenges for creating climate-smart food systems in the Philippines and Vietnam. The study examined food production, consumption, processing and distribution systems across...
bm25_text:
Title: Progress Report: Challenges and opportunities for creating a climate-smart food system in the Philippines and Vietnam
Resource type: Report
EE pillars: Policy & Regulatory, Market Systems
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems
Overview: This is a progress report documenting a mixed-methods research study conducted by CIP and CCAFS to identify opportunities and challenges for creating climate-smart food systems in the Philippines and Vietnam. The study examined food production, consumption, processing and distribution systems across rice, maize, sweet potato and livestock value chains to understand how climate change affects food systems and identify pathways for climate-smart agriculture adoption.
Audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders working on climate-smart agriculture and food systems; Technical/Expert Level; Government agencies, research institutions, development organizations, policy institutes
Where it applies: countries: Philippines, Vietnam; regions: Asia, South-eastern Asia; context: Rice-based farming systems in tropical/subtropical regions vulnerable to climate change impacts; scale: National
How it works: The study used a convergent parallel mixed-methods research design, conducting quantitative analysis of household consumption data alongside qualitative focus group discussions and key informant interviews with farmers and value chain actors across selected provinces in both countries.
Key steps: Data collection using household surveys (FIES for Philippines, VHLSS for Vietnam). Selection of study provinces and recruitment of local partners. Conduct focus group discussions with farmers (15 FGDs in Philippines, 14 in Vietnam). Key informant interviews with value chain actors (100 KIIs in Philippines, 76 in Vietnam). Data analysis and report writing. Policy forum organization and stakeholder engagement. Policy brief development and knowledge sharing
Expected outcomes: Detailed country reports, policy briefs for each country, journal manuscripts, policy forum meetings, stakeholder networks; Enhanced understanding of climate-smart food system opportunities, informed policy dialogue, promotion of climate-resilient agricultural practices
Key takeaways: Climate change is already affecting agricultural production through erratic rainfall, temperature changes, and increased pest/disease pressure. Farmers are adapting through variety selection, timing adjustments, and crop diversification, but need better access to information and technologies. Sweet potato and other root crops show promise as climate-resilient alternatives that can improve food security and farmer incomes. Effective climate-smart food systems require coordinated policy action, stakeholder networks, and investment in extension services. Mixed-methods research approaches can effectively capture both quantitative trends and qualitative farmer experiences to inform policy
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Rice-based farming systems in tropical/subtropical regions vulnerable to climate change impacts
Publisher: CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)

title: Effects of long-term diet supplementation with Gliricidia sepium foliage mixed with Enterolobium cyclocarpum pods on enteric methane, apparent digestibility, and rumen microbial population in crossbred heifers
summary_snippet: This is an academic research paper that evaluates the long-term effects of supplementing cattle diets with Gliricidia sepium foliage mixed with Enterolobium cyclocarpum pods on enteric methane emissions, digestibility, and rumen microbial populations. The study addresses the need to validate whether...
bm25_text:
Title: Effects of long-term diet supplementation with Gliricidia sepium foliage mixed with Enterolobium cyclocarpum pods on enteric methane, apparent digestibility, and rumen microbial population in crossbred heifers
Resource type: Academic Paper
EE pillars: M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Livestock
Overview: This is an academic research paper that evaluates the long-term effects of supplementing cattle diets with Gliricidia sepium foliage mixed with Enterolobium cyclocarpum pods on enteric methane emissions, digestibility, and rumen microbial populations. The study addresses the need to validate whether methane reduction strategies using secondary plant compounds maintain their effectiveness over time.
Audience: Animal nutrition researchers and livestock scientists; Academic/Research Level; Research institutions, universities, and agricultural research centers
Where it applies: countries: Mexico; regions: Latin America; context: Tropical regions with access to Gliricidia sepium and Enterolobium cyclocarpum; scale: Not Specified
How it works: The study used a repeated measurements experimental design where crossbred heifers were fed diets with and without 15% mixture of E. cyclocarpum pods and G. sepium foliage over 80 days. Methane production, digestibility, and microbial populations were measured every 14 days using respiration chambers.
Key steps: 14-day adaptation period with control diet for all animals. Random assignment to control (E+G:0) or treatment (E+G:15) diets. 80-day feeding trial with measurements every 14 days. Methane measurement using open-circuit respiration chambers for 2 consecutive days. Collection of rumen fluid samples for pH, VFA, and microbial population analysis. Fecal collection for digestibility calculations. Statistical analysis using repeated measures design
Expected outcomes: Methane emission measurements, digestibility coefficients, rumen fermentation parameters, microbial population data; Validation of sustainable methane mitigation strategies for tropical livestock systems
Key takeaways: Supplementation with 15% E. cyclocarpum pods mixed with G. sepium foliage reduces methane emissions by 2.6-10.5% in cattle. Methane reduction effects persist over 80 days without adaptation by rumen microorganisms. The treatment slightly reduces crude protein digestibility but does not affect dry matter digestibility or VFA concentrations. Rumen microbial populations (bacteria, protozoa, archaea) remain stable despite methane reduction. The approach shows promise for sustainable methane mitigation in tropical livestock systems
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Tropical regions with access to Gliricidia sepium and Enterolobium cyclocarpum
Publisher: International Center for Tropical Agriculture (CIAT)

title: Gender dimension of climate change research in agriculture: Case studies in Southeast Asia
summary_snippet: This book presents case studies from Southeast Asia examining how climate change affects men and women farmers differently in agricultural systems. It explores gender-differentiated vulnerabilities, adaptation strategies, and coping mechanisms in response to climate risks, while providing examples o...
bm25_text:
Title: Gender dimension of climate change research in agriculture: Case studies in Southeast Asia
Resource type: Guidelines
EE pillars: Gender Equality & Social Inclusion, M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems
Overview: This book presents case studies from Southeast Asia examining how climate change affects men and women farmers differently in agricultural systems. It explores gender-differentiated vulnerabilities, adaptation strategies, and coping mechanisms in response to climate risks, while providing examples of participatory approaches that incorporate gender dimensions in climate-smart agriculture research and development.
Audience: Development practitioners, researchers, policymakers, and CGIAR stakeholders working on climate change adaptation in agriculture; Technical/Expert Level; Research institutions, development organizations, NGOs, government agencies, and international agricultural research centers
Where it applies: countries: Laos, Philippines, Vietnam; regions: Asia, South-eastern Asia; context: Rice-based farming systems, coastal areas, flood-prone regions, drought-affected areas, upland farming systems; scale: Community
How it works: Uses participatory research methods including focus group discussions, key informant interviews, participatory rural appraisal tools, and participatory varietal selection to understand gender differences in climate change impacts and adaptation strategies.
Key steps: Conduct participatory vulnerability assessments with separate men's and women's groups. Use gender-disaggregated data collection methods including household surveys and focus group discussions. Apply participatory rural appraisal tools like seasonal calendars and problem trees. Implement participatory varietal selection with both men and women farmers. Organize farmer learning groups to facilitate knowledge exchange. Provide training on climate-smart technologies with gender considerations. Document and analyze gender-differentiated impacts and coping strategies
Expected outcomes: Gender-sensitive research findings, training materials, improved varieties tested through participatory methods, farmer learning groups established; Enhanced understanding of gender dimensions in climate change adaptation, improved access to climate-smart technologies for women farmers, strengthened adaptive capacity of farming communities
Key takeaways: Men and women farmers experience climate change impacts differently based on their gender roles and access to resources. Women often use more anticipatory coping strategies while men focus on reactive technical solutions. Participatory approaches that separate men and women groups are essential for capturing diverse perspectives and ensuring women's voices are heard. Direct access to improved seeds and training significantly empowers women farmers and improves their decision-making confidence. Gender-sensitive research methods are crucial for developing effective climate adaptation strategies that benefit both men and women equitably
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Rice-based farming systems, coastal areas, flood-prone regions, drought-affected areas, upland farming systems
Publisher: SEARCA and CCAFS

title: Scaling climate smart coffee and cocoa
summary_snippet: This resource documents key lessons from using certification as a scaling mechanism for Climate Smart Agriculture (CSA) under the CCAFS-funded Climate Smart Coffee and Cocoa Value Chain Project. It focuses on how the project influenced the development of new Rainforest Alliance standards to incorpor...
bm25_text:
Title: Scaling climate smart coffee and cocoa
Resource type: Guidelines
EE pillars: Market Systems, Scaling Innovations
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Scaling Innovations, Value Chains
Overview: This resource documents key lessons from using certification as a scaling mechanism for Climate Smart Agriculture (CSA) under the CCAFS-funded Climate Smart Coffee and Cocoa Value Chain Project. It focuses on how the project influenced the development of new Rainforest Alliance standards to incorporate climate risk assessment and adaptation planning at farm and community levels.
Audience: Certification organizations, agricultural development practitioners, and coffee/cocoa value chain stakeholders; Technical/Expert Level; Certification bodies, agricultural development organizations, and value chain companies
Where it applies: countries: ; regions: ; context: Coffee and cocoa growing regions; scale: Global
How it works: Uses certification standards as a scaling mechanism to integrate climate smart agriculture practices, incorporating risk assessment and suitability mapping at granular levels down to site-specific applications.
Key steps: Develop training materials for capacity building in certification work. Apply risk assessment and suitability mapping at granular, site-specific levels. Integrate climate risk assessments into standard development. Include adaptation planning at household and community levels. Incorporate future climatic risks into farm-level management practice recommendations
Expected outcomes: New Rainforest Alliance agriculture standard incorporating climate considerations; Reach certified farmer base of around 2.4 million farmers globally
Key takeaways: Certification can serve as an effective scaling mechanism for climate smart agriculture practices. Site-specific risk assessment and suitability mapping enhance the effectiveness of CSA interventions. Integration of climate considerations into global standards can reach millions of farmers. Adoption and implementation will be gradual through continuous improvement processes. Training materials and capacity building are essential components of certification-based scaling
Development stage: Widely Deployed
Agro-ecological context: Coffee and cocoa growing regions
Publisher: Rainforest Alliance

title: Human and technical dimensions of potato integrated pest management using farmer field schools: International Potato Center and partners’ experience with potato late blight management
summary_snippet: This is an academic paper documenting the International Potato Center and partners' experience implementing farmer field schools for integrated pest management of potato late blight. The paper examines both human and technical dimensions of this approach, providing insights into how farmer field sch...
bm25_text:
Title: Human and technical dimensions of potato integrated pest management using farmer field schools: International Potato Center and partners’ experience with potato late blight management
Resource type: Academic Paper
EE pillars: M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Agri-Food Systems, Scaling Innovations
Overview: This is an academic paper documenting the International Potato Center and partners' experience implementing farmer field schools for integrated pest management of potato late blight. The paper examines both human and technical dimensions of this approach, providing insights into how farmer field schools can be used to address potato disease management challenges.
Audience: Agricultural researchers, extension specialists, and development practitioners working on integrated pest management; Academic/Research Level; Research institutions, agricultural extension services, international development organizations
Where it applies: countries: Peru; regions: Asia, South America; context: Potato production systems affected by late blight; scale: Community
How it works: Uses farmer field schools as a platform for implementing integrated pest management approaches for potato late blight, combining technical knowledge with participatory learning methods.
Expected outcomes: None; Improved potato late blight management through farmer field school approaches
Key takeaways: Farmer field schools can be effective platforms for integrated pest management of potato late blight. Both human and technical dimensions need to be considered in IPM implementation. Multi-institutional partnerships are important for scaling IPM approaches. Experience from multiple regions provides valuable insights for broader application

title: Africa RISING Program Communication and Knowledge Management Strategy: 2019‒2021
summary_snippet: A strategic framework for communication and knowledge management (CKM) within the Africa RISING program, designed to facilitate effective sharing of research results and support scaling of agricultural innovations across six African countries. The strategy addresses the challenge of coordinating com...
bm25_text:
Title: Africa RISING Program Communication and Knowledge Management Strategy: 2019‒2021
Resource type: Framework
EE pillars: M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Agri-Food Systems, Scaling Innovations
Overview: A strategic framework for communication and knowledge management (CKM) within the Africa RISING program, designed to facilitate effective sharing of research results and support scaling of agricultural innovations across six African countries. The strategy addresses the challenge of coordinating communication across multiple CGIAR centers and over 100 partner organizations to ensure research outputs reach and benefit target stakeholders.
Audience: Communication specialists, research program managers, and development practitioners working in multi-partner agricultural research programs; Technical/Expert Level; CGIAR centers, national agricultural research systems, NGOs, international research organizations, and development programs
Where it applies: countries: Ethiopia, Ghana, Malawi, Mali, Tanzania, Zambia; regions: Sub-Saharan Africa; context: Smallholder farming systems across diverse agroecological zones; scale: Regional/Multi-country
How it works: The strategy operates through tiered CKM functions at program and project levels, with five core delivery areas: external communication, internal communication, research for impact, knowledge sharing and adaptive learning, and publishing/curating outputs. Implementation occurs through annual workplans with systematic monitoring and evaluation.
Key steps: Establish program-level and project-level CKM functions with designated specialists. Implement five strategic delivery areas across all program activities. Deploy multiple communication tools and platforms for different audiences. Develop annual workplans for each project region and program level. Monitor implementation using specific indicators for each communication tool. Conduct annual reviews and revisions based on performance and emerging needs. Support scaling activities through targeted communication and knowledge packaging
Expected outcomes: Regular updates and publications, communication support for scaling, simplified technical information in local languages, impact documentation, and experience sharing across projects; Enhanced visibility of Africa RISING program, accelerated scaling of validated technologies, improved collaboration among project teams, and better engagement with donors and stakeholders
Key takeaways: Effective CKM in multi-partner agricultural research programs requires both program-level coordination and project-level implementation. Different audiences require different messages delivered through appropriate channels, with technical content simplified for non-technical stakeholders. Successful scaling of agricultural innovations depends heavily on strategic communication and knowledge management approaches. ICT tools, particularly radio, offer significant opportunities for scaling technologies to smallholder farmers in rural Africa. Continuous monitoring and adaptive management are essential for effective communication strategy implementation
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Smallholder farming systems across diverse agroecological zones
Publisher: International Institute of Tropical Agriculture

title: Gender issues in livestock production in Ethiopia: A review of literature to identify potential entry points for gender responsive research and development
summary_snippet: This is a comprehensive literature review that synthesizes existing research on gender issues in livestock production in Ethiopia, identifying key gender dynamics, constraints, and opportunities. The review analyzes 28 publications to document gender division of labor, access to resources, decision-...
bm25_text:
Title: Gender issues in livestock production in Ethiopia: A review of literature to identify potential entry points for gender responsive research and development
Resource type: Academic Paper
EE pillars: Gender Equality & Social Inclusion, M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Livestock, Value Chains
Overview: This is a comprehensive literature review that synthesizes existing research on gender issues in livestock production in Ethiopia, identifying key gender dynamics, constraints, and opportunities. The review analyzes 28 publications to document gender division of labor, access to resources, decision-making patterns, and institutional barriers affecting women's participation in livestock value chains.
Audience: Researchers, development practitioners, and policymakers working on livestock development and gender equity; Academic/Research Level; CGIAR centers, research institutions, development organizations, government agencies
Where it applies: countries: Ethiopia; regions: Africa, Eastern Africa; context: Mixed crop-livestock systems, pastoral systems, Ethiopian highlands and lowlands; scale: National
How it works: Systematic literature review using both manual and electronic searches across multiple databases to identify and analyze gender issues in Ethiopian livestock production. The review synthesizes findings to create a framework for gender analysis and identify intervention opportunities.
Key steps: Literature search using multiple databases and key terms related to livestock and gender in Ethiopia. Selection of eligible studies published from 2000 onwards. Data extraction using standardized template covering study characteristics and gender issues. Synthesis of findings across gender themes including labor division, resource access, and institutional factors. Development of integrated framework for gender analysis in livestock. Identification of research gaps and investable options for interventions
Expected outcomes: Comprehensive synthesis of gender issues in Ethiopian livestock production, integrated analytical framework, identification of research gaps and intervention opportunities; Inform gender-responsive livestock research and development interventions, guide policy development, and improve gender equity in livestock value chains
Key takeaways: Women play major roles in livestock husbandry and management but their contributions are often culturally undervalued or invisible. Men control political aspects of livestock while women handle labor-intensive reproductive activities across all livestock species. Women face greater constraints in accessing, owning, and controlling livestock resources compared to men. Commercialization of livestock products often shifts control from women to men, creating intrahousehold tensions. Gender capacity development among livestock practitioners is essential for effective gender mainstreaming in interventions
Development stage: Concept/Research
Agro-ecological context: Mixed crop-livestock systems, pastoral systems, Ethiopian highlands and lowlands
Publisher: International Livestock Research Institute (ILRI)

title: Policy Action for Climate Change Adaptation (PACCA) II Project: Situation Analysis Report - Uganda
summary_snippet: This is a comprehensive situation analysis report examining the intersection of climate change, food security, and nutrition in Uganda, with detailed case studies from Mbale and Nwoya districts. The report analyzes policy frameworks, institutional arrangements, and community-level experiences to ide...
bm25_text:
Title: Policy Action for Climate Change Adaptation (PACCA) II Project: Situation Analysis Report - Uganda
Resource type: Report
EE pillars: Policy & Regulatory, Gender Equality & Social Inclusion
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems, Nutrition & Health
Overview: This is a comprehensive situation analysis report examining the intersection of climate change, food security, and nutrition in Uganda, with detailed case studies from Mbale and Nwoya districts. The report analyzes policy frameworks, institutional arrangements, and community-level experiences to identify gaps and opportunities for implementing gender- and nutrition-sensitive climate smart agriculture interventions.
Audience: Government policymakers, development practitioners, researchers working on climate-agriculture-nutrition nexus; Technical/Expert Level; Government agencies, international development organizations, research institutions, NGOs
Where it applies: countries: Uganda; regions: Eastern Africa; context: Mixed farming systems in highland and lowland areas with bimodal and unimodal rainfall patterns; scale: National
How it works: The analysis employed a four-step qualitative approach combining desk research, expert interviews, focus group discussions, and policy analysis to understand climate risks, institutional responses, and community experiences.
Key steps: Extensive literature and policy document review (50 documents). Expert interviews with 32 government and non-government actors. Focus group discussions with 25 community groups (women, men, youth). Policy analysis using 10 criteria framework for gender and nutrition sensitivity. Data analysis and validation through stakeholder workshops
Expected outcomes: Comprehensive understanding of policy gaps, institutional arrangements, and community-level climate-nutrition challenges; Inform development of gender- and nutrition-sensitive climate smart agriculture interventions and policy recommendations
Key takeaways: Significant policy frameworks exist but gaps remain in financing, implementation, and coordination of nutrition-focused climate actions. Climate risks disproportionately affect women, youth, and vulnerable populations who have limited access to adaptation technologies and resources. Multi-sectoral coordination platforms are needed to minimize duplication and enhance knowledge sharing among diverse actors. Landscape-level approaches are essential for effective climate smart agriculture adoption rather than individual farmer targeting. Legal frameworks for climate change mainstreaming (similar to gender and equity budgeting requirements) could improve priority and resource allocation
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Mixed farming systems in highland and lowland areas with bimodal and unimodal rainfall patterns
Publisher: International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA)

title: CCAFS midline synthesis report - Ghana (GH0108)
summary_snippet: This is a midline synthesis report documenting changes in agricultural practices, food security, and climate adaptation in Ghana's Lawra-Jirapa region seven years after CCAFS baseline studies. The report assesses whether households and communities are successfully adapting to climate change through ...
bm25_text:
Title: CCAFS midline synthesis report - Ghana (GH0108)
Resource type: Report
EE pillars: M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems
Overview: This is a midline synthesis report documenting changes in agricultural practices, food security, and climate adaptation in Ghana's Lawra-Jirapa region seven years after CCAFS baseline studies. The report assesses whether households and communities are successfully adapting to climate change through improved farming practices, diversification strategies, and enhanced access to information and resources.
Audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders working on climate adaptation and food security; Technical/Expert Level; Research institutions, development organizations, government agencies, NGOs working in climate-smart agriculture
Where it applies: countries: Ghana; regions: Africa, Western Africa; context: Semi-arid savanna region with unreliable rainfall and poor soil fertility; scale: Community
How it works: The midline assessment revisited the same 140 households from the 2011 baseline survey using standardized tools to measure changes in farming practices, food security, asset ownership, and climate adaptation strategies over a seven-year period.
Key steps: Conduct household surveys using quantitative questionnaires. Implement village baseline studies using participatory approaches. Carry out organizational surveys with key community organizations. Compare midline data with baseline indicators. Analyze changes in adaptation, mitigation, and food security indices. Organize community feedback workshops to validate findings. Synthesize results across household, village, and organizational levels
Expected outcomes: Comprehensive data on changes in farming practices, food security status, asset ownership, organizational membership, and information access patterns; Enhanced understanding of climate adaptation effectiveness and improved targeting of future interventions
Key takeaways: Food security and asset ownership improved significantly over the seven-year period, suggesting positive adaptation outcomes. Households shifted from high diversification to more specialized production, potentially increasing market-oriented income but reducing risk management. Climate and weather remain the primary drivers of agricultural changes, indicating continued vulnerability. Access to information improved substantially through mobile phones and radio, particularly benefiting women. Midline assessments provide valuable insights for tracking long-term adaptation effectiveness and can inform future intervention design
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Semi-arid savanna region with unreliable rainfall and poor soil fertility
Publisher: CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)

title: Mitigación con Sistemas Silvopastoriles en Latinoamérica: Aportes para la incorporación en los sistemas de Medición Reporte y Verificación bajo la CMNUCC
summary_snippet: This document provides technical guidelines for incorporating silvopastoral systems (SSP) into national greenhouse gas inventory measurement, reporting, and verification (MRV) systems under the UNFCCC framework. It addresses the challenge that while silvopastoral systems have recognized climate miti...
bm25_text:
Title: Mitigación con Sistemas Silvopastoriles en Latinoamérica: Aportes para la incorporación en los sistemas de Medición Reporte y Verificación bajo la CMNUCC
Resource type: Guidelines
EE pillars: M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Livestock, Agri-Food Systems
Overview: This document provides technical guidelines for incorporating silvopastoral systems (SSP) into national greenhouse gas inventory measurement, reporting, and verification (MRV) systems under the UNFCCC framework. It addresses the challenge that while silvopastoral systems have recognized climate mitigation potential in Latin America, they remain invisible in national climate accounting due to lack of proper MRV frameworks.
Audience: Climate change mitigation focal points, environment and agriculture ministry representatives, and UNFCCC national inventory experts; Technical/Expert Level; Government agencies responsible for national greenhouse gas inventories and climate policy implementation
Where it applies: countries: Colombia, Argentina, Brazil, Costa Rica, Guatemala, Ecuador, Panama, Peru, Dominican Republic; regions: Latin America, South America; context: Tropical and subtropical livestock production systems; scale: National
How it works: The guidelines provide a structured approach using decision trees to help countries determine how to classify silvopastoral systems within IPCC land use categories and develop appropriate measurement and reporting methodologies for national greenhouse gas inventories.
Key steps: Establish national definitions of silvopastoral systems aligned with climate mitigation objectives. Determine parameters and ranges to distinguish between different SSP typologies. Generate methodological guide for attributing SSP to IPCC land use categories. Develop activity data through national statistics or spatial information. Generate country-specific emission factors. Integrate SSP MRV into national inventory systems. Report progress through national communications and biennial update reports
Expected outcomes: National definitions of silvopastoral systems, activity data and emission factors, integration into national greenhouse gas inventories; Making silvopastoral systems visible in national climate accounting, supporting evidence-based policy decisions, enabling access to climate finance
Key takeaways: Silvopastoral systems have significant climate mitigation potential but remain invisible in national climate accounting across Latin America. Establishing clear national definitions aligned with IPCC land use categories is the critical first step for MRV implementation. Countries need coordinated institutional frameworks between environment and agriculture sectors to successfully implement SSP monitoring. The approach should start with broad SSP categories before advancing to specific typologies based on country priorities and capacities. Regional cooperation through networks like RedINGEI can facilitate knowledge transfer and capacity building for MRV implementation
Development stage: Concept/Research
Agro-ecological context: Tropical and subtropical livestock production systems
Publisher: CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)

title: Activity report: CSV Monitoring and Evaluation Plan
summary_snippet: GeoFarmer is a digital monitoring and evaluation tool developed for Climate-Smart Villages (CSVs) that enables efficient data collection through smartphone applications and web dashboards. This ICT-based solution was designed to monitor climate-smart agriculture practices across CCAFS sites, providi...
bm25_text:
Title: Activity report: CSV Monitoring and Evaluation Plan
Resource type: Tool
EE pillars: M&E & Learning, Digital
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems
Overview: GeoFarmer is a digital monitoring and evaluation tool developed for Climate-Smart Villages (CSVs) that enables efficient data collection through smartphone applications and web dashboards. This ICT-based solution was designed to monitor climate-smart agriculture practices across CCAFS sites, providing near real-time data flows to support agricultural development projects and decision-making by farmers and policymakers.
Audience: Agricultural development practitioners, researchers, and local enumerators working in Climate-Smart Villages; Practitioner Level; CGIAR centers, agricultural research organizations, development projects implementing climate-smart agriculture
Where it applies: countries: Colombia, Ghana, Guatemala, Honduras, Nicaragua, Uganda, Nepal, Bangladesh; regions: Latin America, West Africa, East Africa, South Asia; context: Rural agricultural areas with limited mobile internet coverage; scale: Community
How it works: GeoFarmer uses logical survey trees with connectors between questions, allowing facilitators to conduct interviews with farmers using smartphones. Data is collected offline and synchronized when internet is available, then analyzed using R scripts to calculate predefined indicators.
Key steps: Register farmers with electronic consent. Design logical survey trees with conditional connectors. Train local facilitators and enumerators. Conduct interviews using smartphone app. Synchronize data to cloud server when internet available. Analyze data using R scripts to calculate indicators. Generate results and upload to dashboard
Expected outcomes: Standardized data collection on farm performance (7 indicators), household effects (20 indicators), and adoption trends (17 indicators); Better decision-making by farmers and policymakers through near real-time monitoring of climate-smart agriculture practices
Key takeaways: ICT-based monitoring tools are more cost-effective and suitable for large-scale agricultural development projects than traditional methods. Logical survey trees with conditional connectors (5Q approach) make surveys more effective and avoid redundant questions. Offline functionality with smart synchronization is crucial for rural areas with limited internet connectivity. Comprehensive training of local enumerators is essential for proper question translation and cultural adaptation. The tool successfully scaled across multiple regions and collected substantial amounts of monitoring data
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Rural agricultural areas with limited mobile internet coverage
Publisher: CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)

title: Newly introduced AVRDC vegetable technologies in reduction of income poverty: Babati District, Tanzania
summary_snippet: This is a Master's dissertation that evaluates the economic effectiveness of newly introduced AVRDC (Asian Vegetable Research Development Centre) vegetable production technologies compared to traditional farming practices in Babati District, Tanzania, specifically examining their impact on income po...
bm25_text:
Title: Newly introduced AVRDC vegetable technologies in reduction of income poverty: Babati District, Tanzania
Resource type: Academic Paper
EE pillars: M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Agri-Food Systems, Value Chains, Nutrition & Health
Overview: This is a Master's dissertation that evaluates the economic effectiveness of newly introduced AVRDC (Asian Vegetable Research Development Centre) vegetable production technologies compared to traditional farming practices in Babati District, Tanzania, specifically examining their impact on income poverty reduction among smallholder farmers.
Audience: Agricultural researchers, development practitioners, policymakers, and AVRDC stakeholders; Academic/Research Level; Research institutions, agricultural development organizations, NGOs working in vegetable production
Where it applies: countries: Tanzania; regions: Eastern Africa; context: Semi-arid regions suitable for vegetable production with irrigation potential; scale: District/Provincial
How it works: The study used a panel research design with paired sample T-tests to compare the economic performance of 77 farmers before and after AVRDC technology intervention, measuring yield, costs, revenues, and farmer perceptions of vegetable quality.
Key steps: Baseline data collection on traditional farming practices. Introduction of AVRDC technologies including improved seeds, pest management, fertilizer application, and water management. Training farmers on good agricultural practices. Follow-up survey to measure outcomes. Statistical analysis using paired sample T-tests. Assessment of farmer perceptions on vegetable quality. Cost-benefit analysis comparing traditional vs. new technologies
Expected outcomes: Comparative analysis of yield, costs, revenues, and farmer perceptions between traditional and improved farming practices; Income poverty reduction through adoption of cost-effective vegetable production technologies
Key takeaways: AVRDC technologies significantly increased vegetable yields and farmer profits compared to traditional practices. Despite higher production costs, the new technologies generated substantially higher net revenues. 94% of farmers had favorable perceptions of vegetable quality produced under new technologies. Technologies proved cost-effective for income poverty reduction among smallholder vegetable farmers. Success depends on technology affordability and accessibility to farmers with limited resources
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Semi-arid regions suitable for vegetable production with irrigation potential
Publisher: Sokoine University of Agriculture

summary_snippet: The Gendered Feed Assessment Tool (G-FEAST) focus group discussion guide is a methodological tool designed to assess livestock feeding practices through a gender lens. Key takeaways: Focuses specifically on gender dimensions of livestock feeding practices. Developed by CGIAR livestock research experts with gender specialization. Uses focus group discussion methodology for data collection. Part of broader efforts to mainstream gender analysis in livestock research

It enables researchers and practitioners to gather information about how men and women farmers differently engag...
