Title: Gendered Feed Assessment Tool (G-FEAST) individual farmer interview questionnaire
Resource type: Tool
EE pillars: Gender Equality & Social Inclusion, M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Livestock
Overview: The Gendered Feed Assessment Tool (G-FEAST) individual farmer interview questionnaire is a data collection instrument designed to assess livestock feeding practices through a gender lens. It enables researchers and practitioners to gather information about how men and women farmers differently engage with livestock feed resources, decision-making, and management practices.
Audience: Researchers and development practitioners working on livestock systems; Technical/Expert Level; Research institutes, development organizations, and agricultural extension services
Where it applies: countries: ; regions: ; context: Crop-livestock systems; scale: Household
How it works: The tool uses structured individual interviews to collect gender-disaggregated data on livestock feeding practices and decision-making processes.
Expected outcomes: Gender-disaggregated data on livestock feeding practices and decision-making; Improved understanding of gender dynamics in livestock feed management to inform more inclusive interventions
Key takeaways: Provides a structured approach to collecting gender-disaggregated data on livestock feeding practices. Focuses on individual farmer interviews to capture personal experiences and decision-making processes. Addresses the intersection of gender, livestock management, and crop-livestock systems. Developed by CGIAR institutions with expertise in livestock research and gender analysis
Development stage: Not Determinable
Agro-ecological context: Crop-livestock systems
Publisher: International Livestock Research Institute

title: Whole genome based characterization of indigenous chicken populations in Ethiopia
summary_snippet: This is a PhD thesis that presents a comprehensive whole genome-based characterization of indigenous chicken populations in Ethiopia, analyzing genetic diversity, population structure, and selection signatures using advanced genomic techniques including whole genome sequencing and microsatellite ana...
bm25_text:
Title: Whole genome based characterization of indigenous chicken populations in Ethiopia
Resource type: Academic Paper
EE pillars: M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Livestock, Biodiversity
Overview: This is a PhD thesis that presents a comprehensive whole genome-based characterization of indigenous chicken populations in Ethiopia, analyzing genetic diversity, population structure, and selection signatures using advanced genomic techniques including whole genome sequencing and microsatellite analysis.
Audience: Scientists, researchers, and geneticists working on livestock breeding and conservation; Academic/Research Level; Research institutions, agricultural research centers, universities, and breeding programs
Where it applies: countries: Ethiopia; regions: Africa, Eastern Africa; context: Multiple agro-ecological zones across Ethiopia including tepid to cool moist mid highlands, hot to warm humid lowlands, and various elevation ranges from 1800-2400 masl; scale: National
How it works: The study employs whole genome sequencing and microsatellite analysis to characterize genetic diversity, population structure, and selection signatures in Ethiopian indigenous chicken populations across different agro-ecological zones.
Key steps: Whole blood sample collection from 260+ birds across 27 indigenous chicken populations. DNA isolation and quality checking. Library preparation and whole genome sequencing. Variant calling and SNP discovery (21+ million SNPs identified). Population structure analysis using PCA and admixture analysis. Selective sweep detection using pooled heterozygosity and Fst methods. Functional annotation and gene ontology analysis
Expected outcomes: Comprehensive genomic characterization including 21+ million SNPs (72% novel), population structure analysis revealing four ancestral gene pools, identification of 595 candidate genes under selection; Enhanced understanding of genetic diversity for chicken breeding programs, identification of genomic regions for trait improvement, conservation of indigenous genetic resources
Key takeaways: Ethiopian indigenous chickens possess significant genomic diversity with over 21 million SNPs identified, 72% of which were novel. Four distinct ancestral gene pools exist across Ethiopian chicken populations based on genomic analysis. 595 candidate genes under positive selection were identified, related to production and reproduction traits. Indigenous populations show remarkable genetic potential for improvement while maintaining adaptation to local conditions. Whole genome approaches provide superior resolution for understanding genetic diversity compared to traditional microsatellite methods
Development stage: Concept/Research
Agro-ecological context: Multiple agro-ecological zones across Ethiopia including tepid to cool moist mid highlands, hot to warm humid lowlands, and various elevation ranges from 1800-2400 masl
Publisher: Addis Ababa University

title: Targeting new climate-resilient feeds and forages in the smallholder mixed farming systems of East Africa
summary_snippet: This is a master's thesis that develops a spatial mapping approach to identify feed constraints and system characteristics in smallholder mixed farming systems of East Africa. It creates 16 domains based on feed quantity, seasonal scarcity, land availability, and water availability to predict suitab...
bm25_text:
Title: Targeting new climate-resilient feeds and forages in the smallholder mixed farming systems of East Africa
Resource type: Academic Paper
EE pillars: Gender Equality & Social Inclusion
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Livestock, Agri-Food Systems
Overview: This is a master's thesis that develops a spatial mapping approach to identify feed constraints and system characteristics in smallholder mixed farming systems of East Africa. It creates 16 domains based on feed quantity, seasonal scarcity, land availability, and water availability to predict suitable climate-resilient feed interventions using the FEAST/Techfit logic.
Audience: Researchers, development practitioners, and policymakers working on livestock feed systems; Academic/Research Level; Research institutions, development organizations, agricultural extension services
Where it applies: countries: Kenya, Tanzania, Uganda; regions: Eastern Africa; context: Smallholder mixed farming systems; scale: Regional/Multi-country
How it works: Uses GIS mapping to overlay four variables (feed quantity, seasonal scarcity, land availability, water availability) to create 16 spatial domains, then applies FEAST/Techfit logic to predict suitable feed interventions for each domain.
Key steps: Map feed quantity using dry matter productivity and cattle density data. Map seasonal feed scarcity by comparing monthly feed availability against livestock requirements. Map land availability using cropland area per person. Map water availability using precipitation and flow accumulation data. Overlay the four maps to create 16 domains using random forest clustering. Select contrasting domains for analysis. Apply FEAST/Techfit logic to predict suitable feed interventions
Expected outcomes: Spatial maps showing 16 domains of feed constraints and system characteristics, recommendations for suitable feed interventions per domain; Improved targeting of climate-resilient feed interventions to enhance livestock productivity in mixed farming systems
Key takeaways: Feed scarcity is a major constraint to livestock productivity across East African mixed farming systems, with Uganda showing the most severe constraints. Land availability for forage production is significantly limited across all domains, likely due to land degradation and competing uses. Different domains require different feed intervention strategies based on their specific constraints and system characteristics. Integration of multiple feed interventions is necessary to address all constraints within a domain. Policy interventions are needed to support land rehabilitation, rainwater harvesting, and farmer access to feed technologies
Development stage: Concept/Research
Agro-ecological context: Smallholder mixed farming systems
Publisher: NUI Galway / International Livestock Research Institute (ILRI)

title: Influence of temperature on selected life-history traits of black soldier fly (Hermetia illucens) reared on two common urban organic waste streams in Kenya
summary_snippet: This is an academic research paper that investigates how temperature affects the development, survival, and reproduction of black soldier fly (Hermetia illucens) larvae when reared on two common urban organic waste streams in Kenya - cow dung and brewers' spent grain. The study aims to optimize blac...
bm25_text:
Title: Influence of temperature on selected life-history traits of black soldier fly (Hermetia illucens) reared on two common urban organic waste streams in Kenya
Resource type: Academic Paper
EE pillars: M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Agri-Food Systems, Livestock, Climate Resilience
Overview: This is an academic research paper that investigates how temperature affects the development, survival, and reproduction of black soldier fly (Hermetia illucens) larvae when reared on two common urban organic waste streams in Kenya - cow dung and brewers' spent grain. The study aims to optimize black soldier fly production systems for sustainable livestock feed production in sub-Saharan Africa.
Audience: Researchers, entomologists, and agricultural scientists studying insect-based feed production; Academic/Research Level; Research institutions, universities, agricultural development organizations, and insect farming enterprises
Where it applies: countries: Kenya; regions: Africa, Eastern Africa; context: Urban environments with available organic waste streams; scale: Community
How it works: The study used controlled laboratory experiments to test black soldier fly larvae development on two substrates (cow dung and brewers' spent grain) at five different temperatures (15°C, 20°C, 25°C, 30°C, and 35°C), measuring development time, weight, survival, and reproductive success.
Key steps: Collect eggs from stock colony and hatch larvae. Place 100 3-5 day old larvae in containers with test substrates at 70% moisture. Subject containers to different temperature treatments in incubators. Monitor daily and record larval development duration. Remove prepupae, weigh, and place in sawdust for pupation. Monitor adult emergence and record pupal development time. Allow adults to mate and record longevity and fecundity
Expected outcomes: Data on optimal temperature ranges and substrate types for black soldier fly production; Improved black soldier fly rearing methods for sustainable livestock feed production in sub-Saharan Africa
Key takeaways: Black soldier fly larvae reared on brewers' spent grain outperformed those on cow dung in terms of development speed and weight gain. Optimal temperature range for BSF development is 25-30°C regardless of substrate type. Higher temperatures reduce adult longevity but increase fecundity. Substrate nutritional quality significantly affects all life-history traits of black soldier flies. Urban organic waste streams like spent grain can be effectively used for sustainable insect protein production
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Urban environments with available organic waste streams
Publisher: International Centre of Insect Physiology and Ecology (icipe)

title: Climate-Smart Village AR4D Approach: Synthesis of lessons learned
summary_snippet: The Climate-Smart Village (CSV) AR4D Approach is a comprehensive framework developed by CCAFS for testing, evaluating, and scaling climate-smart agricultural practices through participatory action research. This synthesis document captures four years of lessons learned from implementing CSVs across ...
bm25_text:
Title: Climate-Smart Village AR4D Approach: Synthesis of lessons learned
Resource type: Framework
EE pillars: Policy & Regulatory, Gender Equality & Social Inclusion, M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems, Scaling Innovations
Overview: The Climate-Smart Village (CSV) AR4D Approach is a comprehensive framework developed by CCAFS for testing, evaluating, and scaling climate-smart agricultural practices through participatory action research. This synthesis document captures four years of lessons learned from implementing CSVs across 35 sites in 20 countries, providing evidence-based guidance for scaling climate-smart agriculture interventions.
Audience: Agricultural researchers, development practitioners, policymakers, and CGIAR stakeholders working on climate-smart agriculture; Technical/Expert Level; Research institutions, development organizations, government agencies, and international agricultural programs
Where it applies: countries: India, Nepal, Philippines, Myanmar, Kenya, Tanzania, Senegal, Ghana, Mali, Niger, Burkina Faso, Colombia, Vietnam, Honduras, Nicaragua; regions: South Asia, Southeast Asia, East Africa, West Africa, Latin America; context: Multiple agro-ecosystems including rice-wheat systems, dairy systems, groundnut basin, and various smallholder farming contexts; scale: Community
How it works: CSVs combine bottom-up participatory action research with top-down science-led approaches to test portfolios of climate-smart agricultural interventions. The approach focuses on generating evidence of CSA effectiveness in real-life settings and co-developing scaling mechanisms towards landscape, subnational and national levels.
Key steps: Establish benchmark sites based on biophysical and socio-economic context. Engage multi-stakeholder partnerships for participatory research. Test portfolios of tailored technical and institutional CSA interventions. Generate science-based evidence on CSA effectiveness across productivity, adaptation, and mitigation outcomes. Develop adaptation domains to facilitate transferability. Identify business models and incentive mechanisms for scaling. Scale out successful interventions through policy processes and development programs
Expected outcomes: Science-based evidence on CSA effectiveness, adaptation domains for transferability, business models for scaling, and documented best practices; Wide adoption of climate-smart agricultural practices leading to enhanced productivity, increased incomes, climate resilience, and mitigation benefits
Key takeaways: CSVs are testing grounds for building science-based evidence around Climate-Smart Agricultural portfolios to support integration into government policies and scaling mechanisms. One size does not fit all - context-specific theories of change and implementation mechanisms are needed for success. Balance between technology and socio-economic aspects is crucial, with attention to gender, youth, and indigenous knowledge systems. Strong quantitative data collection is essential to support scaling strategies that require cost/benefit evidence. Success requires multi-level institutional buy-in and concentration of resources on sites likely to deliver high-level outcomes through scale out
Development stage: Widely Deployed
Agro-ecological context: Multiple agro-ecosystems including rice-wheat systems, dairy systems, groundnut basin, and various smallholder farming contexts
Publisher: CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)

title: Co-designed solutions for collecting food & nutrition security data with mobile devices: What worked (and did not) from a NGO, government and research partnership
summary_snippet: This resource documents lessons learned from a partnership between NGOs, government, and research organizations in Zambia to develop mobile device-based solutions for collecting food and nutrition security data. It presents findings from pilot tests of voice call surveys and rapid mobile surveys to ...
bm25_text:
Title: Co-designed solutions for collecting food & nutrition security data with mobile devices: What worked (and did not) from a NGO, government and research partnership
Resource type: Case Study
EE pillars: M&E & Learning, Digital
Thematic areas: Nutrition & Health, Scaling Innovations
Overview: This resource documents lessons learned from a partnership between NGOs, government, and research organizations in Zambia to develop mobile device-based solutions for collecting food and nutrition security data. It presents findings from pilot tests of voice call surveys and rapid mobile surveys to address the need for more comprehensive, faster, and cheaper monitoring and evaluation approaches.
Audience: Development practitioners, government officials, and researchers working in food and nutrition security; Practitioner Level; NGOs, government agencies, research institutions, and development programs
Where it applies: countries: Zambia; regions: Sub-Saharan Africa; context: Rural areas with mobile phone penetration; scale: District/Provincial
How it works: The approach involves using mobile technology for data collection through voice calls and rapid surveys to monitor food and nutrition security indicators more frequently and cost-effectively than traditional face-to-face methods.
Key steps: Conduct initial face-to-face interviews with program participants. Collect phone numbers for follow-up callbacks. Train local enumerators to conduct voice call surveys using smartphones and ODK Collect. Implement callback surveys approximately two weeks after initial interviews. Use rapid Rural Household Multi-Indicator Survey (RHoMIS) for impact evaluation. Analyze data to identify program effectiveness and areas for improvement
Expected outcomes: High-frequency monitoring data on dietary diversity, knowledge attitudes and practices (KAP), and WASH practices; More comprehensive, faster, and cheaper monitoring and evaluation for food and nutrition security programs
Key takeaways: Mobile-based surveys offer opportunities to track key indicators over time and reduce monitoring costs. Voice calls outperform other mobile survey options when clarification is needed and questionnaires are longer than a few questions. Success rates for callbacks are high (89%) among those willing to provide phone numbers, but only 56% of participants were willing to do so. Rapid surveys can effectively detect program impacts and identify areas for programming focus. Further innovation and addressing technical, financial, and programmatic barriers are needed for wider implementation
Development stage: Piloted
Agro-ecological context: Rural areas with mobile phone penetration
Publisher: CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)

title: Spore 192: Digitalising agriculture - Bridging the gender gap
summary_snippet: Spore 192 is a quarterly magazine issue focused on digitalizing agriculture to bridge the gender gap. It explores how digital technologies are providing women farmers with better access to finance, information, and markets, while opening opportunities for young entrepreneurs in agribusiness across A...
bm25_text:
Title: Spore 192: Digitalising agriculture - Bridging the gender gap
Resource type: Report
EE pillars: Digital, Gender Equality & Social Inclusion, Market Systems, Financial Services
Thematic areas: Agri-Food Systems, Value Chains, Scaling Innovations
Overview: Spore 192 is a quarterly magazine issue focused on digitalizing agriculture to bridge the gender gap. It explores how digital technologies are providing women farmers with better access to finance, information, and markets, while opening opportunities for young entrepreneurs in agribusiness across Africa, Caribbean, and Pacific regions.
Audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders; Practitioner Level; Development organizations, agricultural research institutions, government agencies, and private sector companies working in agricultural innovation
Where it applies: countries: Nigeria, Ghana, Kenya, Uganda, Mali, Mozambique, Zimbabwe, Belize, Madagascar; regions: Africa, Caribbean, Pacific; context: Diverse contexts from arid regions to tropical zones, focusing on smallholder farming systems; scale: Regional/Multi-country
How it works: The magazine presents a comprehensive analysis of how digital technologies can bridge gender gaps in agriculture through improved access to services, markets, and information. It combines policy analysis with practical case studies and field reports.
Key steps: Identify gender-specific barriers in agricultural value chains. Implement digital solutions targeting women's needs. Provide training and capacity building for technology adoption. Create supportive policy frameworks. Build networks and platforms for women agripreneurs. Monitor and evaluate impact on gender equality. Scale successful interventions across regions
Expected outcomes: Increased women's access to digital agricultural services, improved market linkages, enhanced financial inclusion; Reduced gender gap in agriculture, increased productivity and incomes for women farmers, strengthened food security
Key takeaways: Digital technologies can significantly reduce gender gaps in agriculture when properly implemented with supportive policies. Women farmers show strong adoption rates when digital solutions address their specific needs and constraints. Success requires holistic approaches combining technology, training, policy support, and institutional partnerships. Mobile-based solutions are particularly effective in reaching rural women farmers across Africa. Evidence shows substantial income increases and improved market access when women gain digital access to agricultural services
Development stage: Widely Deployed
Agro-ecological context: Diverse contexts from arid regions to tropical zones, focusing on smallholder farming systems
Publisher: Technical Centre for Agricultural and Rural Cooperation (CTA)

title: Application of genetic engineering for control of bacterial wilt disease of enset, Ethiopia's sustainability crop
summary_snippet: This is an academic review paper that explores the application of genetic engineering approaches to control bacterial wilt disease in enset (Ensete ventricosum), Ethiopia's indigenous sustainability crop that supports 20 million people. The paper discusses the devastating impact of Xanthomonas campe...
bm25_text:
Title: Application of genetic engineering for control of bacterial wilt disease of enset, Ethiopia's sustainability crop
Resource type: Academic Paper
Thematic areas: Agri-Food Systems, Biodiversity
Overview: This is an academic review paper that explores the application of genetic engineering approaches to control bacterial wilt disease in enset (Ensete ventricosum), Ethiopia's indigenous sustainability crop that supports 20 million people. The paper discusses the devastating impact of Xanthomonas campestris pv. musacearum on enset production and reviews genetic engineering solutions successfully applied to banana that could be adapted for enset.
Audience: Plant biotechnologists, agricultural researchers, and scientists working on crop improvement; Academic/Research Level; Research institutions, agricultural research centers, biotechnology companies, and academic institutions
Where it applies: countries: Ethiopia; regions: Africa, Eastern Africa; context: Enset-growing regions in Southern and Southwestern Ethiopia, densely populated areas with small landholdings; scale: National
How it works: The paper proposes developing genetic transformation systems for enset to introduce bacterial wilt resistance genes (such as Hrap and Pflp) that have been successfully used in banana. This would involve establishing tissue culture protocols, transformation methods, and regeneration systems for enset cultivars.
Key steps: Develop transformation and regeneration protocols for enset cultivars. Introduce resistance genes (Hrap, Pflp, or Xa21) via genetic transformation. Screen transgenic lines for bacterial wilt resistance. Evaluate agronomic performance of resistant lines. Conduct field trials under various agro-ecological conditions. Scale up production of disease-free planting material. Deploy resistant varieties to farmers
Expected outcomes: Bacterial wilt resistant enset varieties, improved tissue culture and transformation protocols for enset, disease-free planting material; Reduced crop losses from bacterial wilt disease, improved food security for 20 million people dependent on enset, preservation of enset genetic diversity
Key takeaways: Bacterial wilt disease caused by Xanthomonas campestris pv. musacearum is the most devastating constraint to enset production, with no known resistant varieties available. Genetic engineering approaches successfully used in banana for bacterial wilt resistance could be adapted for enset, as both crops are affected by the same pathogen. Development of transformation and regeneration protocols for enset cultivars is a prerequisite for successful genetic engineering applications. Gene stacking approaches using multiple resistance genes (Hrap, Pflp, Xa21) may provide more durable resistance than single gene strategies. Integration of biotechnology approaches with improved phytosanitary practices and clean planting material systems is essential for comprehensive disease management
Development stage: Concept/Research
Agro-ecological context: Enset-growing regions in Southern and Southwestern Ethiopia, densely populated areas with small landholdings
Publisher: Frontiers Media SA

title: What can the Latin American cities of Cali, Quito and Medellín learn from each other’s efforts to build sustainable food systems?
summary_snippet: This report documents lessons learned from a South-South exchange workshop between three Latin American cities (Cali, Quito, and Medellín) on building sustainable urban food systems. It analyzes different approaches these cities have taken to address food security challenges and provides recommendat...
bm25_text:
Title: What can the Latin American cities of Cali, Quito and Medellín learn from each other’s efforts to build sustainable food systems?
Resource type: Report
EE pillars: Policy & Regulatory, M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Agri-Food Systems, Nutrition & Health
Overview: This report documents lessons learned from a South-South exchange workshop between three Latin American cities (Cali, Quito, and Medellín) on building sustainable urban food systems. It analyzes different approaches these cities have taken to address food security challenges and provides recommendations for developing comprehensive food policies within a city-region framework.
Audience: Urban planners, policymakers, and development practitioners working on sustainable food systems; Practitioner Level; Municipal governments, development organizations, research institutions
Where it applies: countries: Colombia, Ecuador; regions: Latin America, South America; context: Urban and peri-urban environments in intermediate cities; scale: District/Provincial
How it works: The approach involves analyzing different city experiences with sustainable food systems, identifying common challenges, and facilitating knowledge exchange through South-South cooperation to develop comprehensive food policies.
Key steps: Assess local food system challenges and starting points. Develop city-specific interventions (urban agriculture, nutrition programs, supply chain improvements). Engage multiple stakeholders across sectors. Formulate comprehensive food policies. Establish monitoring indicators. Align with international frameworks like Milan Urban Food Policy Pact. Create mechanisms for ongoing South-South exchange
Expected outcomes: Comprehensive food policies, monitoring systems, stakeholder engagement platforms, short supply chains; Improved food security, enhanced nutrition, sustainable urban development, strengthened urban-rural linkages
Key takeaways: Different cities can start from various entry points (social inclusion, health, supply chains) but converge on comprehensive food system approaches. South-South exchanges generate cooperation bonds and inspire innovation among similar cities. Sustainable food systems create ideal spaces for strengthening collaborative work between actors and sectors. Urban-rural alliances are key tools for integral development promoting short supply chains. Public policies on food sovereignty and security are fundamental commitments for social investment and development
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Urban and peri-urban environments in intermediate cities
Publisher: CGIAR Research Program on Water, Land and Ecosystems

title: Opportunities for forage improvement through the ILRI Genebank
summary_snippet: This presentation describes the ILRI Genebank's forage collection and its role in developing improved forage varieties to address feed constraints in smallholder livestock systems. It showcases how genetic resources, combined with modern breeding techniques and nutritional analysis, can help develop...
bm25_text:
Title: Opportunities for forage improvement through the ILRI Genebank
Resource type: Presentation
EE pillars: M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Livestock, Biodiversity, Climate Resilience
Overview: This presentation describes the ILRI Genebank's forage collection and its role in developing improved forage varieties to address feed constraints in smallholder livestock systems. It showcases how genetic resources, combined with modern breeding techniques and nutritional analysis, can help develop drought-resistant and high-yielding forage varieties for tropical and subtropical regions.
Audience: Plant breeders, livestock researchers, and agricultural development practitioners; Technical/Expert Level; Research institutions, agricultural development organizations, and breeding programs
Where it applies: countries: Ethiopia, Tanzania; regions: East Africa, Sub-Saharan Africa; context: Tropical and subtropical regions with varying altitudes (1650-2400 m.a.s.l.) and rainfall patterns (500-1200mm); scale: Regional/Multi-country
How it works: The ILRI Genebank maintains diverse forage collections across four field sites with different agro-ecological conditions, uses modern breeding techniques including Marker Assisted Selection, and provides nutritional analysis through NIRS technology to develop improved forage varieties.
Key steps: Germplasm characterization and evaluation at field genebanks. Identification of promising genotypes using breeding techniques. Nutritional analysis using Near Infrared Spectroscopy (NIRS). Variety registration and basic seed production. Delivery to producers through public and private sector partnerships. On-farm trials and farmer field testing
Expected outcomes: Improved forage varieties with enhanced drought tolerance, disease resistance, and nutritional quality; Increased livestock productivity, improved soil fertility through nitrogen fixation, reduced erosion, and enhanced system resilience
Key takeaways: The ILRI Genebank maintains 18,635 forage accessions representing 1,400 species across four field sites with different agro-ecological conditions. Modern breeding techniques including Marker Assisted Selection and NIRS nutritional analysis can accelerate development of improved forage varieties. Successful forage adoption requires integration with market opportunities, natural resource management, and strong institutional partnerships. Improved forages can significantly increase livestock productivity while providing environmental benefits like soil fertility improvement and erosion control. Strong public and private investment in the feed sector and enabling policies are needed to meet growing feed demands
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Tropical and subtropical regions with varying altitudes (1650-2400 m.a.s.l.) and rainfall patterns (500-1200mm)
Publisher: International Livestock Research Institute (ILRI)

title: Conservation and Use of the North American Plant Cornucopia: The Way Forward
summary_snippet: This is a comprehensive academic chapter that provides strategic recommendations for enhancing the conservation and utilization of North America's native plant genetic resources, including crop wild relatives and underutilized species. It addresses the challenge of realizing the agricultural and nut...
bm25_text:
Title: Conservation and Use of the North American Plant Cornucopia: The Way Forward
Resource type: Academic Paper
EE pillars: Policy & Regulatory, M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Agri-Food Systems, Biodiversity, Nutrition & Health
Overview: This is a comprehensive academic chapter that provides strategic recommendations for enhancing the conservation and utilization of North America's native plant genetic resources, including crop wild relatives and underutilized species. It addresses the challenge of realizing the agricultural and nutritional potential of thousands of North American plant species through improved conservation strategies, research investment, and market development.
Audience: Researchers, policymakers, conservation practitioners, and plant breeders working on genetic resources; Academic/Research Level; Academic institutions, government agencies, genebanks, botanic gardens, conservation organizations
Where it applies: countries: United States, Canada, Mexico; regions: Northern America; context: Temperate and subtropical regions of North America with diverse native plant species; scale: Regional/Multi-country
How it works: The framework proposes a comprehensive approach combining in situ and ex situ conservation with enhanced research, pre-breeding, and marketing efforts to realize the potential of North American plant genetic resources.
Key steps: Improve documentation and monitoring of wild plant populations in protected areas. Enhance ex situ conservation through coordinated information sharing and gap analysis. Strengthen characterization and evaluation of conserved genetic resources. Invest in pre-breeding and breeding programs for underutilized species. Develop marketing strategies for new crops and varieties. Foster cross-institutional and international collaboration. Build public awareness and support for plant genetic resource conservation
Expected outcomes: Enhanced conservation of North American plant genetic resources, improved documentation and accessibility of collections, new crop varieties and products; More diverse, nutritious, and sustainable food and agricultural systems utilizing North America's native plant diversity
Key takeaways: North America possesses thousands of potentially valuable plant species that are currently underutilized. Coordinated conservation efforts combining in situ and ex situ approaches are essential for preserving genetic diversity. Investment in pre-breeding and breeding programs is needed to develop underutilized species into productive crops. Cross-institutional collaboration and information sharing are critical for effective conservation and utilization. Market development and public awareness are necessary to create demand for new crops and products from native species
Development stage: Concept/Research
Agro-ecological context: Temperate and subtropical regions of North America with diverse native plant species
Publisher: Springer Nature Switzerland AG

title: Gender and innovation for climate-smart agriculture. Assessment of gender-responsiveness of RAN's agricultural-focused innovations
summary_snippet: This working paper presents a gender assessment of three agricultural innovations developed by ResilientAfrica Network (RAN) to evaluate their gender-responsiveness for climate-smart agriculture. The study examines ownership patterns, usage, decision-making processes, and benefits of innovations inc...
bm25_text:
Title: Gender and innovation for climate-smart agriculture. Assessment of gender-responsiveness of RAN's agricultural-focused innovations
Resource type: Report
EE pillars: Gender Equality & Social Inclusion, M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems
Overview: This working paper presents a gender assessment of three agricultural innovations developed by ResilientAfrica Network (RAN) to evaluate their gender-responsiveness for climate-smart agriculture. The study examines ownership patterns, usage, decision-making processes, and benefits of innovations including a low-cost solar irrigation pump, mushroom cultivation using crop residues, and M-Omulimisa mobile extension platform among farmers in Northern Uganda.
Audience: Development practitioners, researchers, and innovation managers working on agricultural technology adoption; Practitioner Level; CGIAR centers, agricultural research institutions, NGOs, and innovation networks
Where it applies: countries: Uganda; regions: Africa, Eastern Africa; context: Rural agricultural communities in Northern Uganda with challenges of prolonged dry spells and limited extension services; scale: Community
How it works: The assessment used qualitative research methods including focus group discussions, in-depth interviews, and key informant interviews to evaluate gender-responsiveness of three agricultural innovations using Climate Smart Agriculture framework.
Key steps: Selection of three agricultural innovations based on CSA focus and affordability criteria. Training of research assistants and pre-testing of assessment tools. Demonstration of innovations to farmers before data collection. Conduct sex-disaggregated focus group discussions, in-depth interviews, and key informant interviews. Thematic analysis of transcripts using study objectives framework. Validation of findings through co-creation workshops and community dissemination meetings
Expected outcomes: Gender assessment report with findings on ownership, usage, decision-making patterns, and recommendations for improving gender-responsiveness of agricultural innovations; Enhanced gender-responsive design and implementation of climate-smart agricultural innovations leading to more equitable access and benefits for male and female farmers
Key takeaways: Socio-cultural norms and gender roles significantly influence adoption of agricultural innovations regardless of their technical benefits. Men typically control ownership of technology-based innovations while women are expected to use them more, creating potential for increased work burden. Decision-making remains male-dominated even when women report joint decisions, affecting women's access to innovation benefits. Gender sensitization and community demonstration sites are essential for promoting equitable adoption of climate-smart agricultural innovations. Financial access mechanisms like Village Saving Loan Associations and grants are needed to enable women farmers to afford innovations
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Rural agricultural communities in Northern Uganda with challenges of prolonged dry spells and limited extension services
Publisher: CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)

title: Climate-Smart Agriculture in Malawi
summary_snippet: This is a comprehensive country profile that provides a baseline assessment of Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) in Malawi, analyzing the current state of agricultural systems, climate vulnerabilities, CSA practices, institutional frameworks, and financing mechanisms. It serves as a foundational resou...
bm25_text:
Title: Climate-Smart Agriculture in Malawi
Resource type: Report
EE pillars: Policy & Regulatory, Financial Services, Market Systems
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems, Scaling Innovations
Overview: This is a comprehensive country profile that provides a baseline assessment of Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) in Malawi, analyzing the current state of agricultural systems, climate vulnerabilities, CSA practices, institutional frameworks, and financing mechanisms. It serves as a foundational resource for identifying entry points and investment opportunities for scaling CSA interventions in Malawi's agriculture sector.
Audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders working on agricultural development and climate change in Malawi; Technical/Expert Level; Government agencies, international development organizations, research institutions, NGOs, and donor agencies
Where it applies: countries: Malawi; regions: Africa, Eastern Africa, Sub-Saharan Africa; context: Three agro-ecological zones: Lower Shire valley, lakeshore plains and Upper Shire valley, and mid-altitude plateau with highlands; scale: National
How it works: The profile uses a comprehensive assessment methodology examining agricultural production systems, climate vulnerabilities, CSA practices, institutional frameworks, and financing mechanisms to create a baseline for CSA investment planning.
Key steps: Analyze agricultural production systems key for food security. Assess climate change impacts and vulnerabilities. Evaluate existing CSA practices and their climate-smartness. Map institutional landscape and policy frameworks. Assess financing mechanisms and opportunities. Identify entry points for CSA scaling. Provide recommendations for investment priorities
Expected outcomes: Comprehensive baseline assessment, identification of CSA investment priorities, mapping of institutional landscape, analysis of financing gaps and opportunities; Improved coordination and targeting of CSA investments, enhanced policy coherence, increased adoption of climate-smart practices
Key takeaways: Agriculture accounts for 30% of Malawi's GDP and supports 90% of the population, making climate resilience critical for national development. CSA adoption remains extremely low (1-2% for Conservation Agriculture) despite 308 ongoing projects, indicating coordination and implementation challenges. Climate change will significantly impact agricultural productivity, with maize and potato yields projected to decline by 10.61% and 12.78% respectively by 2050. Major institutional and financing gaps exist, with over 60% of available climate funds unknown to stakeholders and weak capacity for proposal development. Successful CSA scaling requires addressing gender disparities, land tenure issues, and improving coordination among the 70+ institutions involved in CSA activities
Development stage: Widely Deployed
Agro-ecological context: Three agro-ecological zones: Lower Shire valley, lakeshore plains and Upper Shire valley, and mid-altitude plateau with highlands
Publisher: International Center for Tropical Agriculture (CIAT) and World Bank

title: Climate-Smart Agriculture in Belize
summary_snippet: This is a comprehensive country profile that analyzes climate-smart agriculture (CSA) opportunities in Belize, identifying promising practices, institutional frameworks, and financing strategies to help the agricultural sector adapt to climate change while increasing productivity and reducing greenh...
bm25_text:
Title: Climate-Smart Agriculture in Belize
Resource type: Framework
EE pillars: Policy & Regulatory, Financial Services, M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems
Overview: This is a comprehensive country profile that analyzes climate-smart agriculture (CSA) opportunities in Belize, identifying promising practices, institutional frameworks, and financing strategies to help the agricultural sector adapt to climate change while increasing productivity and reducing greenhouse gas emissions.
Audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders working on agricultural development and climate adaptation; Technical/Expert Level; Government ministries, international development organizations, NGOs, research institutions, and private sector actors in agriculture
Where it applies: countries: Belize; regions: Central America, Latin America and the Caribbean; context: Four agro-climatic zones: Northern Zone, Central Coastal Zone, Central Inland Zone, and Southern Zone, with diverse production systems from subsistence to commercial export-oriented agriculture; scale: National
How it works: The CSA approach systematically identifies locally effective practices, diagnoses barriers to adoption, evaluates strategies to overcome barriers, and ensures institutional and financial enablers are in place. It uses a participatory, multi-phase prioritization process involving workshops, interviews, surveys, and focus group discussions.
Key steps: Systematic identification of technically effective, financially profitable, and environmentally sustainable CSA practices. Careful diagnosis of barriers to adoption of those practices. Evaluation of strategies to overcome the barriers. Assessment of climate smartness across productivity, adaptation, and mitigation dimensions. Participatory prioritization process with diverse national actors. Development of best-bet CSA portfolios. Ensuring presence of institutional and financial enablers
Expected outcomes: Identification of best-bet CSA practices, institutional mapping, policy recommendations, financing strategies; Sustainably increase agricultural productivity and farmers' incomes, adapt and build resilience to climate change, and reduce/remove greenhouse gas emissions
Key takeaways: Agriculture in Belize is highly vulnerable to climate change, with projected temperature increases of 1.3°C by 2030s and rainfall decreases of 7-10%. Many promising CSA practices already exist and are being used by farmers, including cover structures, drip irrigation, agroforestry, and improved varieties. Mainstreaming CSA requires systematic identification of effective practices, diagnosis of adoption barriers, and ensuring institutional and financial enablers. Multiple institutions are working on CSA but few address all three pillars (productivity, adaptation, mitigation) simultaneously. Additional financing estimated at US$ 13 million is needed for agricultural adaptation, requiring both public and private sector engagement
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Four agro-climatic zones: Northern Zone, Central Coastal Zone, Central Inland Zone, and Southern Zone, with diverse production systems from subsistence to commercial export-oriented agriculture
Publisher: International Center for Tropical Agriculture (CIAT) and World Bank

title: An evaluation of the outcomes of the calibration and validation of the RUMINANT Model for Colombia
summary_snippet: This is an evaluation report of the calibration and validation of the RUMINANT model for Colombia, which is an animal-level software tool for estimating ruminant methane emissions and livestock productivity. The evaluation uses outcome harvesting methodology to assess how the adapted model has contr...
bm25_text:
Title: An evaluation of the outcomes of the calibration and validation of the RUMINANT Model for Colombia
Resource type: Report
EE pillars: M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Livestock
Overview: This is an evaluation report of the calibration and validation of the RUMINANT model for Colombia, which is an animal-level software tool for estimating ruminant methane emissions and livestock productivity. The evaluation uses outcome harvesting methodology to assess how the adapted model has contributed to improving livestock sector knowledge, enhancing productivity through better feed practices, and supporting Colombia's climate change mitigation commitments.
Audience: Policy makers, technical assistance groups, producer associations, researchers, and livestock sector stakeholders; Technical/Expert Level; Government agencies (IDEAM, Ministry of Agriculture), livestock federations (FEDEGAN), research institutions, and sustainable livestock organizations
Where it applies: countries: Colombia; regions: Latin America, South America; context: Tropical livestock systems with forage-based production; scale: National
How it works: RUMINANT is calibrated and validated for Colombian conditions by comparing model predictions with field measurements using polytunnels and laboratory analysis. The tool takes diet quality as input and predicts animal intake, nutrition, production, and methane emissions.
Key steps: Calibrate RUMINANT model for Colombian livestock types and feed qualities. Validate model through field measurements using polytunnels and laboratory analysis. Train stakeholders on model use through workshops and capacity building. Apply model to develop mitigation scenarios and policy commitments. Disseminate results through bulletins, websites, and training programs
Expected outcomes: Calibrated and validated RUMINANT model for Colombian conditions, training materials, policy scenarios, and technical bulletins; Improved livestock productivity through better nutrition, reduced methane emissions, and enhanced climate change mitigation strategies
Key takeaways: RUMINANT provides a cost-effective alternative to expensive field measurements for estimating livestock methane emissions. The tool successfully contributed to Colombia's climate policy development, including NDC targets and livestock NAMA. Capacity building and knowledge sharing were critical for adoption across government agencies, producer organizations, and academic institutions. The model demonstrated strong correlation (R2=0.7) between predicted and measured results, providing confidence for policy applications. Integration with existing livestock programs enhanced both productivity and sustainability outcomes
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Tropical livestock systems with forage-based production
Publisher: CIAT (International Center for Tropical Agriculture)

title: South-South Collaboration in CCAFS for developing capacity on weather index insurance
summary_snippet: This resource documents a South-South collaboration workshop conducted by CCAFS in 2019 to build capacity on weather index insurance across multiple regions. The workshop addressed the need for climate risk management tools in agriculture by facilitating knowledge exchange and hands-on training on d...
bm25_text:
Title: South-South Collaboration in CCAFS for developing capacity on weather index insurance
Resource type: Training Manual
EE pillars: Financial Services, M&E & Learning, Policy & Regulatory
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems
Overview: This resource documents a South-South collaboration workshop conducted by CCAFS in 2019 to build capacity on weather index insurance across multiple regions. The workshop addressed the need for climate risk management tools in agriculture by facilitating knowledge exchange and hands-on training on developing weather index insurance products for smallholder farmers.
Audience: Development practitioners, researchers, and policymakers working on climate risk management and agricultural insurance; Technical/Expert Level; CGIAR centers, research institutions, insurance companies, government agencies
Where it applies: countries: India, Senegal, Honduras, Nicaragua, Vietnam, Ethiopia; regions: West Africa, East Africa, South Asia, South-East Asia, Latin America; context: Smallholder farming systems, both rainfed and irrigated; scale: Regional/Multi-country
How it works: The workshop used hands-on Excel-based exercises to teach participants how to develop weather triggers and calculate premiums for index insurance products. It combined technical training with cross-regional knowledge sharing and collaborative planning.
Key steps: Introduction to crop-weather relationships and critical growth phases. Hands-on trigger design using Excel-based exercises. Premium estimation and ratemaking calculations. Optimization techniques for improving weather triggers. Regional group discussions on developing weather trigger atlas. Planning for Crop-loss Assessment Monitor (CAM) tool development. Development of regional work plans for scaling insurance products
Expected outcomes: Trained participants capable of developing weather index insurance products, regional work plans for CAM tool development, weather trigger and premium rate atlas for different regions; Enhanced climate risk management for smallholder farmers through improved access to weather index insurance products
Key takeaways: South-South collaboration effectively facilitates knowledge transfer on weather index insurance across regions. Hands-on Excel-based training provides practical skills for developing weather triggers and premium calculations. Regional adaptation is crucial as different areas face distinct perils (drought, floods, salinity, pests) and have varying data availability. Hybrid insurance products combining multiple data sources and models show promise for improving accuracy and coverage. Successful scaling requires strong partnerships with insurance companies, meteorological agencies, and government institutions
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Smallholder farming systems, both rainfed and irrigated
Publisher: CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)

title: Evaluating Diverse Solar Irrigation Systems for their Scalability in India
summary_snippet: This resource documents a comprehensive evaluation framework for assessing diverse solar irrigation systems across India to identify scalable models. It addresses the challenge of scaling solar irrigation technologies despite heavy subsidies, by systematically reviewing existing pilots and developin...
bm25_text:
Title: Evaluating Diverse Solar Irrigation Systems for their Scalability in India
Resource type: Framework
EE pillars: Policy & Regulatory, Market Systems
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Water & Irrigation, Scaling Innovations
Overview: This resource documents a comprehensive evaluation framework for assessing diverse solar irrigation systems across India to identify scalable models. It addresses the challenge of scaling solar irrigation technologies despite heavy subsidies, by systematically reviewing existing pilots and developing indicators for scalability, sustainability, inclusion and climate-smart agriculture.
Audience: Practitioners, researchers, policy makers working on agricultural water management and climate adaptation; Technical/Expert Level; Development organizations, research institutions, government agencies, private sector
Where it applies: countries: India; regions: Asia, Southern Asia; context: Areas with groundwater irrigation potential and solar energy viability; scale: National
How it works: The framework uses a three-pronged approach: developing a compendium of solar irrigation pilots, conducting systematic review using indicator-based evaluation, and promoting suitable models through stakeholder engagement.
Key steps: Develop compendium of major solar irrigation pilot projects across India. Conduct representative sample surveys in states with significant solar irrigation installations. Document problems, socio-economic impacts, and implementation strategies. Develop indicator-based framework integrating scalability, sustainability, inclusion and climate-smart agriculture. Analyze each pilot initiative for strategies that worked in specific contexts. Conduct workshops and stakeholder consultations for feedback and knowledge sharing. Advocate strategies to promote suitable solar irrigation models
Expected outcomes: Compilation of working solar irrigation models, evaluation guide for scalability, knowledge products on implementation practices; Facilitate scaling of efficient solar irrigation models, support climate adaptation and mitigation, contribute to India's green energy targets
Key takeaways: Solar irrigation offers climate-smart solutions but requires careful evaluation to prevent groundwater overexploitation. Systematic evaluation using scalability, sustainability, inclusion and climate-smart indicators is essential for identifying effective models. Success depends on understanding bio-physical, socio-cultural, political and economic contexts of different regions. Collaboration among researchers, practitioners, private sector and policy makers is crucial for scaling. Knowledge sharing through stakeholder engagement supports adoption of suitable models
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Areas with groundwater irrigation potential and solar energy viability
Publisher: CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)

title: High-Throughput Biomass Estimation in Rice Crops Using UAV Multispectral Imagery
summary_snippet: This academic paper presents a high-throughput method for estimating above-ground biomass in rice crops using UAV-mounted multispectral cameras and vegetation indices. The approach combines seven vegetation indices through multivariable regressions calibrated for different rice growth stages (vegeta...
bm25_text:
Title: High-Throughput Biomass Estimation in Rice Crops Using UAV Multispectral Imagery
Resource type: Academic Paper
EE pillars: M&E & Learning, Digital
Thematic areas: Agri-Food Systems, Scaling Innovations
Overview: This academic paper presents a high-throughput method for estimating above-ground biomass in rice crops using UAV-mounted multispectral cameras and vegetation indices. The approach combines seven vegetation indices through multivariable regressions calibrated for different rice growth stages (vegetative, reproductive, ripening) and validated using small 1-meter sampling areas.
Audience: Agricultural researchers, precision agriculture specialists, crop monitoring technicians; Technical/Expert Level; Research institutions, agricultural technology companies, precision agriculture service providers
Where it applies: countries: Colombia; regions: Latin America; context: Both upland (dry soil) and lowland (flooded) rice production systems; scale: Community
How it works: Uses UAV-mounted multispectral cameras to capture near-infrared imagery of rice crops, processes images through clustering algorithms to identify plant areas, calculates seven vegetation indices, and applies multivariable regression models calibrated for specific growth stages to estimate biomass.
Key steps: Capture multispectral NIR images using UAV at 12m altitude. Apply image pre-processing including feature extraction and k-means clustering. Segment crop areas using either column or circle segmentation methods. Calculate seven vegetation indices (NDVI, GNDVI, SR, CTVI, SAVI, DVI, MSAVI). Apply stage-specific multivariable regression models. Generate biomass estimates with GPS geo-referencing. Validate against ground-truth destructive sampling measurements
Expected outcomes: Biomass estimates for rice crop areas with spatial mapping and correlation indices; Non-destructive, high-throughput monitoring of rice biomass for precision agriculture and breeding programs
Key takeaways: First work to use 1-meter sampling areas for calibrating NIR image-based biomass estimation in rice. Circle segmentation method using GPS data achieves higher accuracy than column segmentation. Different vegetation indices perform better at different growth stages, requiring stage-specific calibration. Multivariable regression outperforms neural networks for ripening stage estimation. Method shows potential for breeding programs where plot sizes are limited to 1 linear meter
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Both upland (dry soil) and lowland (flooded) rice production systems
Publisher: International Center for Tropical Agriculture (CIAT)

title: Development and Adoption of Latin American Low-Input Rice Production System through Genetic Improvement and Advanced Field-Management Technologies
summary_snippet: This technical guide presents research outputs and technologies from a 5-year SATREPS project (2014-2019) that developed low-input rice production systems for Latin America through genetic improvement and advanced field management technologies. The guide provides practical tools and methodologies fo...
bm25_text:
Title: Development and Adoption of Latin American Low-Input Rice Production System through Genetic Improvement and Advanced Field-Management Technologies
Resource type: Training Manual
EE pillars: M&E & Learning, Digital
Thematic areas: Agri-Food Systems, Water & Irrigation, Climate Resilience, Scaling Innovations
Overview: This technical guide presents research outputs and technologies from a 5-year SATREPS project (2014-2019) that developed low-input rice production systems for Latin America through genetic improvement and advanced field management technologies. The guide provides practical tools and methodologies for improving water and nitrogen use efficiency in rice production, including new breeding lines with deeper root systems, precision agriculture technologies, and water-saving irrigation methods.
Audience: Rice producers, extension staff, researchers, and agronomists; Technical/Expert Level; Agricultural research institutions, farmer organizations, extension services, and rice breeding programs
Where it applies: countries: Colombia; regions: Latin America, Andean region; context: Tropical lowland rice production systems with contour-levee irrigation; scale: Community
How it works: The project integrated genetic improvement through marker-assisted breeding with precision agriculture technologies and improved water/nutrient management practices to develop resource-efficient rice production systems.
Key steps: Identify and introgress root QTLs into Colombian rice varieties using marker-assisted selection. Implement high-throughput field phenotyping systems for trait evaluation. Develop and test water-saving irrigation technologies like MIRI system. Apply crop modeling and decision support tools for optimized management. Conduct multi-environmental trials to validate breeding lines and technologies. Transfer technologies to farmers through community-based precision agriculture. Integrate all technologies into comprehensive low-input production systems
Expected outcomes: New rice varieties with deeper root systems, water-saving irrigation technologies, decision support tools, and precision agriculture protocols; Increased water and nitrogen use efficiency, reduced production costs, improved yield stability under low-input conditions, and enhanced competitiveness of Colombian rice
Key takeaways: Integration of genetic improvement with precision agriculture can significantly improve resource use efficiency in rice production. Root QTLs can be successfully transferred to Colombian varieties to enhance performance under low-input conditions. Water-saving technologies like MIRI can reduce irrigation needs by 30% while maintaining or improving yields. Decision support tools and crop modeling can optimize management practices for local conditions. Community-based technology transfer through farmer-to-farmer networks is essential for successful adoption
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Tropical lowland rice production systems with contour-levee irrigation
Publisher: Science and Technology Research Partnership for Sustainable Development (SATREPS)

title: Rethinking how gender matters for food security.
summary_snippet: This is an academic journal article that examines how gender matters for food security by challenging individualistic approaches to women's empowerment and advocating for more relational, context-specific understandings of gender dynamics in agricultural and food systems.
bm25_text:
Title: Rethinking how gender matters for food security.
Resource type: Academic Paper
EE pillars: Gender Equality & Social Inclusion
Thematic areas: Agri-Food Systems, Nutrition & Health
Overview: This is an academic journal article that examines how gender matters for food security by challenging individualistic approaches to women's empowerment and advocating for more relational, context-specific understandings of gender dynamics in agricultural and food systems.
Audience: Researchers, development practitioners, and policymakers working on gender and food security; Academic/Research Level; Academic institutions, development organizations, policy bodies, CGIAR centers
Where it applies: countries: ; regions: ; context: None; scale: Global
How it works: The paper advocates for moving from individualistic models of women's empowerment to relational approaches that consider women as actors within networks of social relations, examining their multiple roles in food systems beyond just production.
Key steps: Recognize women's multiple roles in food webs beyond production. Consider relational rather than individualistic approaches to empowerment. Focus on long-term rather than short-term food provisioning. Examine context-specific gender dynamics. Move beyond binary gender categories to understand intersectionality
Expected outcomes: More nuanced understanding of gender dynamics in food security; Improved gender mainstreaming approaches and more effective food security interventions
Key takeaways: Gender analysis should move beyond individualistic approaches to consider relational dynamics. Women play multiple roles in food systems beyond just production. Context-specific understanding is crucial for effective gender mainstreaming. Long-term food provisioning strategies may differ from short-term approaches. Market structures and regulatory regimes at higher levels remain resistant to gender equality
Development stage: Concept/Research
Agro-ecological context: None
Publisher: Tropical Agriculture Association (TAA)

title: Effects of phosphorus fertilizer and inoculation on yield and nutritive values of grain and haulm of selected grain legumes in mixed crop-livestock production system of Ethiopia
summary_snippet: This MSc thesis investigates the effects of phosphorus fertilizer and rhizobium inoculation on grain and haulm (crop residue) yield and nutritional quality of four grain legumes (faba bean, chickpea, haricot bean, and soybean) in Ethiopia's mixed crop-livestock production systems. The research addre...
bm25_text:
Title: Effects of phosphorus fertilizer and inoculation on yield and nutritive values of grain and haulm of selected grain legumes in mixed crop-livestock production system of Ethiopia
Resource type: Academic Paper
EE pillars: M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Agri-Food Systems, Livestock, Nutrition & Health
Overview: This MSc thesis investigates the effects of phosphorus fertilizer and rhizobium inoculation on grain and haulm (crop residue) yield and nutritional quality of four grain legumes (faba bean, chickpea, haricot bean, and soybean) in Ethiopia's mixed crop-livestock production systems. The research addresses the dual challenge of improving both grain production for human consumption and haulm quality for livestock feed in smallholder farming systems.
Audience: Agricultural researchers, extension agents, and development practitioners working on crop-livestock integration; Academic/Research Level; Research institutions, agricultural universities, development organizations focused on mixed farming systems
Where it applies: countries: Ethiopia; regions: Africa, Eastern Africa; context: Mixed crop-livestock production systems in highland areas (1500-3200 m.a.s.l) with adequate rainfall and moderate temperature; scale: District/Provincial
How it works: The study employed field experiments across multiple districts testing four treatments (control, phosphorus fertilizer alone, rhizobium inoculation alone, and combined application) on four grain legume species, followed by laboratory analysis of grain and haulm quality parameters and household surveys.
Key steps: Establish field experiments across N2-Africa project target districts. Apply four fertilizer treatments to grain legume plots. Harvest crops and collect grain and haulm samples. Conduct laboratory analysis for nutritional quality parameters. Administer household surveys on feed use practices. Analyze data using statistical methods. Evaluate treatment effects on yield and quality
Expected outcomes: Quantified effects of phosphorus fertilizer and rhizobium inoculation on grain yield, haulm dry matter yield, and nutritional quality parameters of four grain legumes; Improved whole-plant value of grain legumes to benefit both human nutrition and livestock feeding in mixed farming systems
Key takeaways: Combined application of phosphorus fertilizer and rhizobium inoculation significantly improved both grain and haulm yield and quality in most grain legumes tested. Grain legume haulms are increasingly used as livestock feed by smallholder farmers due to feed shortages and better nutritional quality compared to cereal residues. Improvement in haulm crude protein content and digestibility can enhance livestock nutrition in mixed crop-livestock systems. Majority of farmers (62.2%) recognized positive effects of treatments on haulm biomass yield but were less aware of quality improvements. The technology shows potential for optimizing whole-plant value of grain legumes in smallholder farming systems
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Mixed crop-livestock production systems in highland areas (1500-3200 m.a.s.l) with adequate rainfall and moderate temperature
Publisher: Hawassa University

title: Cost benefit analysis for climate-smart options for sustainable agriculture and resilience on smallholder farms
summary_snippet: This is a presentation outlining a cost-benefit analysis (CBA) methodology for evaluating climate-smart agriculture options on smallholder farms. It provides a systematic approach to assess the economic viability of climate-smart practices by comparing costs and benefits from both farmer and societa...
bm25_text:
Title: Cost benefit analysis for climate-smart options for sustainable agriculture and resilience on smallholder farms
Resource type: Presentation
EE pillars: M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems
Overview: This is a presentation outlining a cost-benefit analysis (CBA) methodology for evaluating climate-smart agriculture options on smallholder farms. It provides a systematic approach to assess the economic viability of climate-smart practices by comparing costs and benefits from both farmer and societal perspectives.
Audience: Agricultural development economists, researchers, and practitioners evaluating climate-smart agriculture interventions; Technical/Expert Level; Research institutions, development organizations, and agricultural agencies
Where it applies: countries: Zimbabwe; regions: Sub-Saharan Africa, Eastern Africa; context: Smallholder farming systems susceptible to climate change; scale: Household
How it works: The methodology compares annual flows of incremental benefits and costs between business-as-usual farming and climate-smart agriculture practices. It follows a systematic data collection process from informal surveys to formal assessments, then calculates profitability indicators.
Key steps: Establish baseline farming system description and problems. Follow funneling process for data collection (informal survey, focus groups, literature review). Estimate installation costs and annual operation/maintenance costs. Calculate incremental benefits from CSA practices vs farmer practices. Compute profitability indicators (IRR, NPV, payback period). Conduct sensitivity analysis at different discount rates and input costs. Estimate aggregate impact through diffusion pattern modeling
Expected outcomes: Profitability indicators (IRR, NPV, payback period, return on investment), sensitivity analysis results, aggregate economic impact estimates; Evidence-based assessment of climate-smart agriculture options to support farmer decision-making and policy development
Key takeaways: Cost-benefit analysis provides systematic approach to evaluate climate-smart agriculture options for smallholder farms. Methodology compares incremental benefits and costs between traditional and climate-smart practices. Assessment should consider both farmer perspective and societal perspective including environmental externalities. Data collection follows funneling process to balance precision needs with resource constraints. Sensitivity analysis is crucial to test robustness under different economic scenarios
Development stage: Concept/Research
Agro-ecological context: Smallholder farming systems susceptible to climate change
Publisher: CGIAR

title: Systèmes d'information sur les marchés agricoles en Afrique 7: Collecter les données
summary_snippet: This is a French-language training manual that provides practical guidance on data collection methods for agricultural market information systems (MIS) in Africa. It covers both primary and secondary data collection approaches, enumeration techniques, and the trade-offs between data quality, quantit...
bm25_text:
Title: Systèmes d'information sur les marchés agricoles en Afrique 7: Collecter les données
Resource type: Training Manual
EE pillars: M&E & Learning, Market Systems, Digital
Thematic areas: Agri-Food Systems, Value Chains
Overview: This is a French-language training manual that provides practical guidance on data collection methods for agricultural market information systems (MIS) in Africa. It covers both primary and secondary data collection approaches, enumeration techniques, and the trade-offs between data quality, quantity, and cost.
Audience: Development practitioners designing and implementing agricultural market information systems; Practitioner Level; NGOs, government agencies, research institutions, and private sector organizations working on agricultural market systems
Where it applies: countries: ; regions: Africa; context: None; scale: Regional/Multi-country
How it works: Provides systematic guidance on collecting both primary data (through enumerators, crowdsourcing, direct observation) and secondary data (from government, commodity exchanges, weather services) for agricultural market information systems.
Key steps: Select products and markets for data collection. Specify product characteristics and measurement units. Decide on collection frequency and timing. Design and test data collection forms. Train enumerators on standardized procedures. Implement data collection with quality controls. Balance quality, quantity, and cost considerations
Expected outcomes: Accurate, timely market price and volume data; trader and farmer profiles; production and weather information; Improved market transparency and information access for agricultural stakeholders to make better trading and production decisions
Key takeaways: Standardized procedures are essential to eliminate interviewer bias and ensure data consistency across enumerators. Both primary and secondary data sources are needed, with APIs enabling automatic data collection from digital sources. Electronic data collection eliminates transcription errors and delays but requires higher upfront investment. Cross-border trade monitoring requires creative approaches due to access limitations at border crossings. Regular quality control and enumerator motivation are critical for maintaining data accuracy over time
Development stage: Widely Deployed
Agro-ecological context: None
Publisher: Centre Technique de Coopération Agricole et Rurale (CTA)

title: Reaching the Unreached: School-on-the-air on Climate-Smart Agriculture (SOA-CSA) in Cagayan Valley
summary_snippet: This working paper documents a radio-based distance learning project called 'School-on-the-Air on Climate-Smart Agriculture (SOA-CSA)' implemented in Cagayan Valley, Philippines in 2018. The project used radio broadcasts to deliver climate-smart agriculture education to over 10,000 rice farmers, dem...
bm25_text:
Title: Reaching the Unreached: School-on-the-air on Climate-Smart Agriculture (SOA-CSA) in Cagayan Valley
Resource type: Case Study
EE pillars: M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems
Overview: This working paper documents a radio-based distance learning project called 'School-on-the-Air on Climate-Smart Agriculture (SOA-CSA)' implemented in Cagayan Valley, Philippines in 2018. The project used radio broadcasts to deliver climate-smart agriculture education to over 10,000 rice farmers, demonstrating the power of radio as a platform for sharing agricultural technologies and support services to farmers under climate change conditions.
Audience: Development practitioners, agricultural extension services, policymakers, and media organizations interested in radio-based agricultural education; Practitioner Level; Government agricultural agencies, NGOs, rural broadcasters, research institutions, and local government units
Where it applies: countries: Philippines; regions: Asia, South-eastern Asia; context: Rice-producing regions; scale: Regional/Multi-country
How it works: The SOA-CSA project used radio broadcasts to deliver educational modules on climate-smart agriculture to farmers. The program followed an 'edutainment' approach with 30-minute segments including technical content, interviews, and interactive elements like Q&A sessions with prizes.
Key steps: Conduct preparatory meetings and stakeholder consultations. Develop modular content through multi-agency collaboration. Validate content through focus group discussions with farmers. Record and broadcast 68 episodes over 12 radio stations. Monitor farmer enrollment and participation. Conduct mass graduation ceremony. Assess outcomes through post-graduation focus group discussions
Expected outcomes: 68 broadcast episodes, over 10,000 farmer enrollees, 5,000+ graduates, educational manual, strengthened partnerships among 22 agencies; Improved awareness and understanding of climate-smart agriculture among rice farmers, enhanced public engagement in government agriculture programs, potential emergence of climate-smart villages
Key takeaways: Radio can effectively reach large numbers of farmers (10,000+) with agricultural education in remote areas. Multi-stakeholder partnerships (22 agencies) are crucial for successful implementation of large-scale extension programs. Educational-entertainment ('edutainment') approach with interactive elements enhances farmer engagement. Climate-smart agriculture education can strengthen public engagement with government agricultural programs. Success of pilot programs can lead to scaling and replication in other regions and crop systems
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Rice-producing regions
Publisher: CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)
