bm25_text:
Title: Changing behavior through agriculture-nutrition integration: field experience and results. Regional Technical Committee meeting of the Scaling Up Sweetpotato Through Agriculture and Nutrition (SUSTAIN) project, 11–12 September 2018, Kigali, Rwanda
Resource type: Report
EE pillars: M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Nutrition & Health, Scaling Innovations
Overview: This document presents findings from the SUSTAIN project's Regional Technical Committee meeting, documenting field experiences and results from agriculture-nutrition integration interventions using orange-fleshed sweetpotato (OFSP) in Rwanda, Tanzania, and Malawi. It provides evidence on behavior change approaches, nutrition education strategies, and lessons learned from implementing community-based nutrition interventions that link agricultural production to improved dietary practices.
Audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders working on nutrition-sensitive agriculture; Practitioner Level; NGOs, government agencies, research institutions, and international development organizations implementing nutrition and agriculture programs
Where it applies: countries: Rwanda, Tanzania, Malawi; regions: Africa; context: Smallholder farming systems in sub-Saharan Africa; scale: Community
How it works: Integrates biofortified orange-fleshed sweetpotato production with community-based nutrition education and social behavior change communication (SBCC) delivered through community health workers and nutrition clubs to improve dietary diversity and nutrition outcomes.
Key steps: Establish community-based nutrition caregiver clubs with trained community health workers. Distribute OFSP planting materials with accompanying nutrition education. Deliver nutrition messages through mass dissemination events and community meetings. Conduct nutrition counseling in small groups and home-to-home visits. Organize cooking demonstrations and nutrition events. Provide ongoing monitoring and follow-up support. Link farmers to markets and value chain opportunities
Expected outcomes: Improved caregiver nutrition knowledge, increased household and child dietary diversity, enhanced vitamin A intake, and better food security; Reduced malnutrition among children under 5 and pregnant/lactating women through improved dietary practices and OFSP consumption
Key takeaways: Community-based nutrition education combined with agricultural interventions can effectively improve dietary diversity and nutrition knowledge. Participation in multiple nutrition club meetings shows greater impact than single exposures, emphasizing the importance of sustained engagement. Men's involvement in nutrition activities is crucial but challenging, requiring targeted strategies such as integration with marketing training. Strong partnerships with government institutions and health systems are essential for sustainability and scale. Behavior change requires addressing cultural barriers and involving local leaders and influencers in the intervention design
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Smallholder farming systems in sub-Saharan Africa
Publisher: International Potato Center (CIP)

title: Climate-Smart Agriculture in Belize: Identifying Investment Priorities
summary_snippet: This document presents a multi-criteria framework for identifying and prioritizing climate-smart agriculture (CSA) practices for Belize. The CSA Prioritization Framework helps policymakers target scarce investment resources more effectively by evaluating CSA practices across technical, economic, and...
bm25_text:
Title: Climate-Smart Agriculture in Belize: Identifying Investment Priorities
Resource type: Framework
EE pillars: Policy & Regulatory, M&E & Learning, Financial Services
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems
Overview: This document presents a multi-criteria framework for identifying and prioritizing climate-smart agriculture (CSA) practices for Belize. The CSA Prioritization Framework helps policymakers target scarce investment resources more effectively by evaluating CSA practices across technical, economic, and sociocultural factors to identify promising options for adoption and scaling.
Audience: Policymakers, program administrators, development agencies, and private firms including finance organizations; Technical/Expert Level; Government agencies, international development organizations, agricultural research institutions, private sector firms
Where it applies: countries: Belize; regions: Central America, Caribbean; context: Low-lying coastal nations vulnerable to climate change, subtropical climate with adequate rainfall; scale: National
How it works: The framework uses a multi-phase participatory exercise to identify and prioritize CSA practices through stakeholder engagement, literature review, and multi-criteria evaluation. It assesses practices across economic criteria (NPV, IRR, payback period) and implementation factors to create portfolios of promising CSA options.
Key steps: Conduct comprehensive literature review and stakeholder consultation. Identify long list of potential CSA practices through participatory workshops. Apply multi-criteria evaluation framework to assess practices. Analyze economic criteria including costs, benefits, and returns. Evaluate barriers and opportunities for adoption. Filter to short list of promising practices by cost category. Develop implementation strategies and financing approaches
Expected outcomes: Prioritized list of CSA practices categorized by cost (low, medium, high), economic analysis of practices including NPV and IRR, identification of barriers and enabling factors; More effective targeting of CSA investments, increased adoption of climate-smart practices, enhanced agricultural productivity and climate resilience
Key takeaways: Multi-criteria frameworks can effectively prioritize CSA investments by considering technical, economic, and sociocultural factors. CSA practices can be categorized by implementation cost, with different financing and policy approaches needed for each category. Low-cost practices often have short payback periods but may face adoption barriers unrelated to profitability. Medium and high-cost practices require coordinated public-private financing approaches and enabling policy environments. The Maximizing Finance for Development approach provides a systematic framework for leveraging private sector resources in CSA investments
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Low-lying coastal nations vulnerable to climate change, subtropical climate with adequate rainfall
Publisher: International Center for Tropical Agriculture (CIAT) and World Bank

Title: Workshop report for the review and validation of the Nigeria Gender Action Plan
Resource type: Report
EE pillars: Gender Equality & Social Inclusion, Policy & Regulatory
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience
Overview: This is a workshop report documenting the review and validation process of Nigeria's National Gender Action Plan for climate change. The workshop brought together key stakeholders to domesticate the UNFCCC Gender Action Plan into Nigeria's national context, providing guidance for mainstreaming gender into climate change plans and programs.
Audience: Government officials, civil society organizations, NGOs, and gender experts working on climate change policy; Technical/Expert Level; Government ministries, development agencies, civil society organizations, and research institutions
Where it applies: countries: Nigeria; regions: Africa, West and Central Africa, Western Africa; context: None; scale: National
How it works: The workshop used a structured review process involving stakeholder consultation, document review, and breakout sessions to validate and improve Nigeria's draft Gender Action Plan for climate change.
Key steps: Opening remarks and objective setting. Overview presentation of the Draft Gender Action Plan. Stakeholder comments and feedback collection. Division into breakout groups for in-depth analysis. Group reporting of observations and recommendations. Document validation with corrections noted. Final recommendations for document revision
Expected outcomes: Validated Gender Action Plan document with stakeholder input and recommended revisions; Improved mainstreaming of gender concerns into national climate change policies and action plans to support Paris Agreement implementation
Key takeaways: Multi-stakeholder consultation is essential for developing nationally appropriate gender action plans for climate change. Document validation requires structured review processes including breakout sessions and expert feedback. National circumstances must be considered when domesticating international frameworks like the UNFCCC Gender Action Plan. Consistent terminology and clear titles are important for policy document effectiveness. Collaborative revision processes help ensure stakeholder buy-in and document quality

title: Gender assessment of local understanding and indicators of empowerment among ACGG project beneficiaries in Nigeria
summary_snippet: This is a gender assessment study that examines local understanding and indicators of empowerment among smallholder poultry farmers participating in the African Chicken Genetic Gains (ACGG) project in Nigeria. The study addresses the need to understand context-specific meanings of empowerment to inf...
bm25_text:
Title: Gender assessment of local understanding and indicators of empowerment among ACGG project beneficiaries in Nigeria
Resource type: Academic Paper
EE pillars: Gender Equality & Social Inclusion, M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Livestock, Agri-Food Systems
Overview: This is a gender assessment study that examines local understanding and indicators of empowerment among smallholder poultry farmers participating in the African Chicken Genetic Gains (ACGG) project in Nigeria. The study addresses the need to understand context-specific meanings of empowerment to inform effective interventions and measurement approaches for women's empowerment in agricultural development projects.
Audience: Development practitioners, researchers, and policymakers working on gender empowerment in agricultural projects; Technical/Expert Level; CGIAR centers, development organizations, agricultural research institutions, and gender-focused NGOs
Where it applies: countries: Nigeria; regions: Africa, Western Africa; context: Rural smallholder farming communities; scale: Community
How it works: The study uses focus group discussions with male and female poultry farmers to understand local definitions of empowerment, features of empowered individuals, constraints to empowerment, and empowerment indicators. It employs a cross-sectional research design with gender-disaggregated analysis.
Key steps: Purposive selection of ACGG project participating communities. Conduct gender-specific focus group discussions. Use pilot ACGG empowerment tool as FGD guide. Transcribe and analyze data using ATLAS.ti qualitative analysis. Code quotations and calculate frequencies by gender. Analyze local understanding and empowerment indicators. Compare gender perspectives across study areas
Expected outcomes: Gender-disaggregated understanding of empowerment definitions, features of empowered individuals, constraints to empowerment, and local empowerment indicators; Inform selection of indicators to measure women's and men's empowerment and identify interventions to close gender gaps in agricultural development projects
Key takeaways: Empowerment is context-specific and has different meanings for men and women in rural Nigerian communities. Women conceptualize empowerment more from a service-providing perspective focused on helping others, while men emphasize personal development and authority. Lack of funds, cultural restrictions, and gender discrimination are major constraints to empowerment, particularly for women. Both genders see business expansion, education of children, and family care as important empowerment goals. Understanding local meanings of empowerment is crucial for designing effective and sustainable interventions
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Rural smallholder farming communities
Publisher: International Livestock Research Institute (ILRI)

title: What works to empower women livestock farmers? A synthesis of GAAP2 Livestock projects
summary_snippet: This report synthesizes qualitative findings from two livestock projects in the GAAP2 portfolio to understand what empowers women livestock farmers and how to measure women's empowerment in livestock contexts. It analyzes case studies from Tanzania and Nepal to reveal empowerment patterns and assess...
bm25_text:
Title: What works to empower women livestock farmers? A synthesis of GAAP2 Livestock projects
Resource type: Report
EE pillars: Gender Equality & Social Inclusion, M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Livestock, Agri-Food Systems
Overview: This report synthesizes qualitative findings from two livestock projects in the GAAP2 portfolio to understand what empowers women livestock farmers and how to measure women's empowerment in livestock contexts. It analyzes case studies from Tanzania and Nepal to reveal empowerment patterns and assess the suitability of pro-WEAI indicators for measuring empowerment of women livestock keepers.
Audience: Development practitioners, researchers, and policymakers working on women's empowerment in livestock and agricultural development; Technical/Expert Level; CGIAR centers, NGOs, development organizations, research institutions working on gender and livestock
Where it applies: countries: Tanzania, Nepal; regions: East Africa, South Asia; context: Pastoralist and semi-pastoralist systems in Tanzania; diverse agroecological zones in Nepal including mid-hills and Tarai; scale: Community
How it works: The study synthesizes qualitative baseline reports from two livestock projects using focus group discussions, semi-structured interviews, and key informant interviews to identify barriers to empowerment and what works to empower women in project settings.
Key steps: Review qualitative baseline reports from livestock projects. Conduct focus group discussions to identify empowerment barriers. Document life histories through semi-structured interviews. Interview project staff for project information. Analyze empowerment patterns against pro-WEAI indicators. Assess suitability of quantitative indicators for livestock contexts
Expected outcomes: Understanding of empowerment mechanisms for women livestock farmers and assessment of pro-WEAI indicator suitability; Improved design of livestock projects to empower women and better measurement tools for women's empowerment in livestock contexts
Key takeaways: Building social capital through groups is crucial for empowering women in livestock projects. Cash transfer gives women more flexibility than physical asset transfer in deciding livestock investments. Supportive intrahousehold relationships are essential for women to exercise agency over livestock. Women's position in households and communities significantly influences their empowerment potential. Pro-WEAI indicators need adaptation for livestock contexts, particularly regarding domestic violence and autonomy measures
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Pastoralist and semi-pastoralist systems in Tanzania; diverse agroecological zones in Nepal including mid-hills and Tarai
Publisher: International Livestock Research Institute (ILRI)

title: Gender strategic research for the African Chicken Genetic Gains (ACGG) project: A qualitative research guide
summary_snippet: A comprehensive qualitative research guide designed for the African Chicken Genetic Gains (ACGG) project to understand gender dynamics in poultry value chains, women's empowerment, and chicken trait preferences. The guide provides detailed methodologies, participatory tools, and step-by-step protoco...
bm25_text:
Title: Gender strategic research for the African Chicken Genetic Gains (ACGG) project: A qualitative research guide
Resource type: Guidelines
EE pillars: Gender Equality & Social Inclusion
Thematic areas: Livestock, Value Chains
Overview: A comprehensive qualitative research guide designed for the African Chicken Genetic Gains (ACGG) project to understand gender dynamics in poultry value chains, women's empowerment, and chicken trait preferences. The guide provides detailed methodologies, participatory tools, and step-by-step protocols for conducting gender-sensitive research in smallholder chicken production systems across Ethiopia, Tanzania, and Nigeria.
Audience: Research teams, gender focal persons, and field researchers conducting qualitative studies on gender and livestock; Technical/Expert Level; CGIAR centers, development organizations, research institutions working on livestock and gender
Where it applies: countries: Ethiopia, Tanzania, Nigeria; regions: Africa; context: Smallholder farming systems where chickens are raised; scale: Community
How it works: Uses participatory qualitative research methods including group discussions with men and women separately, employing six specific participatory tools to understand empowerment, trait preferences, value chains, livelihoods, resource access, and seasonal labor patterns.
Key steps: Planning meeting and 2-4 days training of research team. Field piloting in nearby community to test tools. Village entry and introductions with community leaders. Separate group discussions with men and women using participatory tools. Daily evening debriefings to analyze findings. Team consolidation and synthesis of data. Report writing following structured format
Expected outcomes: Comprehensive qualitative data on gender-differentiated chicken trait preferences, local understanding of empowerment, women's economic advancement indicators, power and agency measures, and gender-sensitive value chain analysis; Enhanced understanding of gender dynamics to inform ACGG project interventions for women's empowerment and improved chicken breeding programs that consider gender-differentiated preferences
Key takeaways: Provides six specific participatory tools for understanding gender dynamics in poultry value chains. Emphasizes separate discussions with men and women to capture different perspectives on empowerment and decision-making. Includes comprehensive training protocols and daily debriefing processes for quality data collection. Focuses on understanding local definitions of empowerment rather than imposing external frameworks. Designed for cross-country application with adaptation to local contexts
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Smallholder farming systems where chickens are raised
Publisher: International Livestock Research Institute (ILRI)

Title: Report of the FAO training workshop 'Gender and Livestock Development in South Asia', Colombo, Sri Lanka, 25–27 April 2017
Resource type: Training Manual
EE pillars: Gender Equality & Social Inclusion, M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Livestock, Value Chains
Overview: This is a comprehensive training workshop report documenting a three-day FAO training event on gender integration in livestock development programs across South Asia. The workshop aimed to build capacity among livestock officers and ministry representatives to address gender issues in livestock programming, from project design through implementation and monitoring.
Audience: FAO livestock officers, ministry representatives from agriculture and livestock departments, and livestock development practitioners; Practitioner Level; Government ministries, FAO country offices, international development organizations working in livestock sector
Where it applies: countries: Afghanistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Nepal, Pakistan, Sri Lanka; regions: Asia, Southern Asia; context: Rural livestock production systems across diverse agro-ecological zones; scale: Regional/Multi-country
How it works: Uses adult learning methodology with interactive exercises, value chain mapping, and project cycle integration. Participants work through gender analysis tools, case studies, and develop country-specific action plans.
Key steps: Pre-assessment of participants' gender knowledge. Foundation training on gender concepts and livestock sector issues. Value chain mapping exercises to identify gender constraints. Country presentations on specific gender challenges. Hands-on practice with gender analysis tools. Project cycle integration training (design, implementation, monitoring). Policy integration session and advocacy strategies. Development of individual and country action plans
Expected outcomes: Enhanced knowledge of gender concepts, practical skills in gender analysis tools, country-specific action plans, validated training methodology; Improved integration of gender considerations in livestock projects leading to better outcomes for rural women and men, enhanced project design and implementation quality
Key takeaways: Interactive, adult learning approaches with peer-to-peer sharing significantly enhance knowledge retention and practical application. Value chain and project cycle entry points provide familiar frameworks for integrating gender analysis into livestock programming. Country-specific action planning with institutional follow-up is essential for translating training into practice. Gender analysis must address the full spectrum from household dynamics to policy level interventions. Sustained capacity building requires multiple touchpoints and ongoing technical support beyond initial training
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Rural livestock production systems across diverse agro-ecological zones
Publisher: FAO (Food and Agriculture Organization) and ILRI (International Livestock Research Institute)

title: Report of the FAO training 'Gender and Livestock Development in East Africa', Nairobi, Kenya, 28-30 May 2018
summary_snippet: This is a comprehensive training report documenting a 3-day FAO workshop on gender analysis in livestock development held in Nairobi, Kenya in May 2018. The training aimed to build capacity among FAO staff and Ministry representatives from 8 Eastern African countries to integrate gender consideratio...
bm25_text:
Title: Report of the FAO training 'Gender and Livestock Development in East Africa', Nairobi, Kenya, 28-30 May 2018
Resource type: Training Manual
EE pillars: Gender Equality & Social Inclusion, M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Livestock, Value Chains
Overview: This is a comprehensive training report documenting a 3-day FAO workshop on gender analysis in livestock development held in Nairobi, Kenya in May 2018. The training aimed to build capacity among FAO staff and Ministry representatives from 8 Eastern African countries to integrate gender considerations into livestock programming, addressing the persistent gender inequalities that limit women's participation in and benefits from livestock value chains.
Audience: FAO livestock officers, Ministry of Agriculture and Livestock representatives, livestock development practitioners; Practitioner Level; Government agencies, international development organizations, NGOs working in livestock development
Where it applies: countries: Burundi, Ethiopia, Kenya, Rwanda, Somalia, South Sudan, Tanzania, Uganda; regions: Eastern Africa; context: Mixed farming systems, pastoral and agro-pastoral systems; scale: Regional/Multi-country
How it works: The training uses adult learning approaches with interactive sessions, case studies, and practical exercises. It employs livestock value chains and project cycles as entry points to make gender analysis relevant to participants' existing work contexts.
Key steps: Pre-assessment to gauge participant knowledge levels. Day 1: Gender foundations, concepts, and value chain mapping exercises. Day 2: Gender analysis tools application and country presentations. Day 3: Monitoring and evaluation, policy integration, and action planning. Development of individual and country actionable plans. Post-assessment and follow-up questionnaire. Ongoing support for implementation of work plans
Expected outcomes: Enhanced gender analysis capacity among participants, country-specific action plans, improved understanding of gender issues in livestock development; Better integration of gender considerations in livestock projects and policies, improved targeting and effectiveness of livestock interventions, enhanced benefits for rural women and men
Key takeaways: Gender analysis is critical for effective livestock development as women comprise 2/3 of poor livestock keepers but face systematic barriers to resources and decision-making. Interactive, practical training approaches using familiar concepts like value chains and project cycles are most effective for building gender analysis capacity. Country-specific action plans and follow-up support are essential for translating training into actual practice and policy changes. Successful gender integration requires both technical knowledge and institutional commitment at multiple levels. Evidence-based approaches and sex-disaggregated data collection are fundamental for understanding and addressing gender gaps in livestock development
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Mixed farming systems, pastoral and agro-pastoral systems
Publisher: FAO (Food and Agriculture Organization)

Title: Learning module on Anthrax transmission and control
Resource type: Training Manual
EE pillars: M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Livestock, Nutrition & Health
Overview: A comprehensive training module designed to educate farmers in the Ethiopian highlands about anthrax transmission, clinical signs, and prevention measures. The module addresses farmers' limited knowledge about this zoonotic disease that affects livestock and can be transmitted to humans, providing practical guidance for disease prevention and control.
Audience: Men and women farmers in Ethiopian highlands; Community/Farmer Level; Community-based training programs, agricultural extension services, livestock development organizations
Where it applies: countries: Ethiopia; regions: East Africa; context: Highland farming systems with small ruminant production; scale: Community
How it works: Uses active and collaborative learning methods building on farmers' existing knowledge and experiences. Employs visualizations, group activities, practical demonstrations, video clips, role-play and reflective activities to enhance learning and knowledge application.
Key steps: Session 1: Learn about anthrax importance and causes. Session 2: Understand anthrax transmission pathways. Session 3: Identify clinical signs in animals and humans. Session 4: Apply prevention and control measures. Develop action plans for implementation. Conduct follow-up assessment and monitoring
Expected outcomes: Farmers able to describe anthrax causes and transmission, identify clinical signs, apply control measures, avoid consuming infected meat, and regularly vaccinate animals; Prevent transmission of anthrax to humans and reduce livestock losses due to anthrax infection
Key takeaways: Anthrax is a serious zoonotic disease caused by spore-forming bacteria that can persist in soil for decades. Clinical signs include sudden death, high temperature, blood discharge from body openings, and loss of appetite in animals. Prevention requires annual vaccination, proper carcass disposal, protective equipment use, and immediate reporting to veterinary officers. Training uses participatory methods building on farmers' existing knowledge and experiences. Assessment includes pre/post training evaluation and follow-up monitoring for knowledge retention and application
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Highland farming systems with small ruminant production
Publisher: International Livestock Research Institute (ILRI)

summary_snippet: A comprehensive training module designed for farmers in the Ethiopian highlands to learn about coenurosis, a parasitic disease affecting sheep and goats caused by dog tapeworms. The module covers transmission pathways, clinical signs, prevention and control measures, and practical skills like dog fe...
bm25_text:
Title: Learning module on Coenurosis transmission and control
Resource type: Training Manual
EE pillars: M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Livestock, Nutrition & Health
Overview: A comprehensive training module designed for farmers in the Ethiopian highlands to learn about coenurosis, a parasitic disease affecting sheep and goats caused by dog tapeworms. The module covers transmission pathways, clinical signs, prevention and control measures, and practical skills like dog fecal sample collection to help farmers protect their livestock and improve animal health.
Audience: Women and men farmers in the highlands of Ethiopia, community animal health workers, and training facilitators; Community/Farmer Level; Rural farming communities, livestock development organizations, veterinary extension services
Where it applies: countries: Ethiopia; regions: East Africa; context: Highland farming systems with sheep and goat production; scale: Community
How it works: Uses active and collaborative learning methods building on farmers' existing knowledge and experiences. Combines theoretical sessions with practical demonstrations and group activities to teach about coenurosis transmission, prevention, and control.
Key steps: Introduction to coenurosis and its causes. Learning transmission pathways of the disease. Identifying clinical signs in affected animals. Understanding prevention and control measures. Practicing dog fecal sample collection and submission. Developing community action plans. Post-training evaluation and follow-up
Expected outcomes: Farmers able to explain coenurosis transmission, identify clinical signs, implement prevention measures, collect dog fecal samples, and follow community deworming schedules; Improved livestock and dog health, reduced economic losses from coenurosis, prevention of zoonotic disease transmission to humans
Key takeaways: Coenurosis is caused by dog tapeworms and can be prevented through proper management of dogs and infected livestock. Community-based approaches including collective dog deworming every three months are essential for effective control. Farmers must avoid feeding sheep and goat heads to dogs and properly dispose of infected carcasses by burning or burying. The disease has both economic impacts on livestock and public health implications through related zoonotic infections. Practical training combining theory with hands-on activities like fecal sample collection improves learning outcomes
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Highland farming systems with sheep and goat production
Publisher: International Livestock Research Institute (ILRI)

title: Socio-economic factors that constrain or facilitate adoption of soil carbon enhancing land management practices among households in Ethiopia and Kenya
summary_snippet: This research analyzes socio-economic factors that influence farmers' adoption of soil carbon enhancing land management practices in Ethiopia and Kenya. It combines a structured literature review of 144 studies with analysis of household data to identify constraints and facilitators for sustainable ...
bm25_text:
Title: Socio-economic factors that constrain or facilitate adoption of soil carbon enhancing land management practices among households in Ethiopia and Kenya
Resource type: Academic Paper
EE pillars: M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems
Overview: This research analyzes socio-economic factors that influence farmers' adoption of soil carbon enhancing land management practices in Ethiopia and Kenya. It combines a structured literature review of 144 studies with analysis of household data to identify constraints and facilitators for sustainable land management practices that improve soil carbon sequestration.
Audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers working on sustainable land management and soil carbon enhancement; Technical/Expert Level; CGIAR centers, development organizations, government agencies, research institutions
Where it applies: countries: Ethiopia, Kenya; regions: Eastern Africa; context: Areas where farming is primary activity and land degradation through soil and nutrient loss is common; scale: Household
How it works: Combines structured literature review of adoption studies with quantitative analysis of household data using agglomerative hierarchical clustering to identify farmer typologies and factors affecting adoption decisions.
Key steps: Structured literature review of publications on adoption of agricultural and SLM practices. Creation of database containing socio-economic factors from literature. Analysis of WOCAT and CIAT-best bet practices household data. Agglomerative hierarchical clustering to group households into three homogenous groups. Regression analysis to identify determinants of adoption. Synthesis of findings across countries and practice types
Expected outcomes: Identification of key socio-economic factors that constrain or facilitate adoption of soil carbon enhancing practices; Improved policy and strategy formulation for promoting sustainable land management practices that enhance soil carbon sequestration
Key takeaways: Socio-economic factors have varied influence on adoption depending on the nature of the soil carbon enhancing technology. Factors affecting farmer incentive to invest are related to conditions that affect net returns to labor or financial investment. Age, gender, income, education, extension services, and land tenure security show consistent patterns across both countries. Policy frameworks need to incorporate adoption constraints and enablers to facilitate increased uptake. Need for stable land tenure systems and enabling conditions to enhance adoption of soil carbon enhancing practices
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Areas where farming is primary activity and land degradation through soil and nutrient loss is common
Publisher: CGIAR

title: CTA Annual report 2018 - A year in review: Accelerating Agricultural Transformation
summary_snippet: CTA's 2018 Annual Report documents the organization's work accelerating agricultural transformation across ACP countries through three main intervention areas: promoting youth entrepreneurship and employment, increasing productivity and profitability through digitalization, and building resilience t...

Title: CTA Annual report 2018 - A year in review: Accelerating Agricultural Transformation
Resource type: Report
EE pillars: Digital, Market Systems, Gender Equality & Social Inclusion, M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems, Value Chains, Scaling Innovations
Overview: CTA's 2018 Annual Report documents the organization's work accelerating agricultural transformation across ACP countries through three main intervention areas: promoting youth entrepreneurship and employment, increasing productivity and profitability through digitalization, and building resilience to climate change. The report showcases how digital technologies, climate-smart practices, and targeted support for women and youth are transforming agricultural value chains and improving food security.
Audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders; Practitioner Level; Development organizations, government agencies, agricultural research institutions, private sector companies
Where it applies: countries: Kenya, Uganda, Rwanda, Senegal, Mali, Ethiopia, Zambia, Zimbabwe, Malawi, Ghana, Benin, Jamaica, Samoa; regions: ACP; context: Diverse contexts including pastoralist systems, smallholder farming, island agriculture; scale: Regional/Multi-country
How it works: CTA implements integrated approaches combining digital technologies, climate-smart practices, and targeted support for youth and women entrepreneurs across agricultural value chains. Projects focus on capacity building, technology adoption, and market linkages.
Key steps: Digital profiling of farmers and agricultural stakeholders. Training and capacity building for youth entrepreneurs and women. Technology deployment (mobile apps, drones, weather information systems). Market linkage facilitation and value chain development. Climate-smart agriculture promotion including insurance schemes. Partnership building between public and private sectors. Monitoring and evaluation of outcomes
Expected outcomes: Digital profiling of 85,000+ farmers, training of 4,166 individuals in open data, 10,827 pastoralists purchasing livestock insurance; Increased agricultural productivity (25-90% yield increases), improved incomes (€25-850 additional per farmer annually), enhanced climate resilience
Key takeaways: Digital technologies combined with traditional agricultural services can dramatically increase productivity and farmer incomes. Youth and women entrepreneurs are key drivers of agricultural transformation when provided with appropriate training and support. Climate-smart approaches including insurance schemes are essential for building resilience in vulnerable farming communities. Strong partnerships between government, private sector, and development organizations are critical for scaling impact. Experience capitalization and learning approaches help ensure project sustainability and knowledge transfer
Development stage: Widely Deployed
Agro-ecological context: Diverse contexts including pastoralist systems, smallholder farming, island agriculture
Publisher: Technical Centre for Agricultural and Rural Cooperation (CTA)

summary_snippet: A comprehensive monitoring framework and ICT-based data collection tool for assessing Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) adoption and impacts across Climate-Smart Villages (CSV). The framework includes 44 standard indicators covering productivity, adaptation, and mitigation pillars, implemented through...
bm25_text:
Title: Status of CSA adoption and related impact on farming communities across 8 CSV implementing the CSV monitoring plan in 2018
Resource type: Tool
EE pillars: M&E & Learning, Gender Equality & Social Inclusion
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems
Overview: A comprehensive monitoring framework and ICT-based data collection tool for assessing Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) adoption and impacts across Climate-Smart Villages (CSV). The framework includes 44 standard indicators covering productivity, adaptation, and mitigation pillars, implemented through a mobile app to track CSA practices and their effects on farming communities.
Audience: CCAFS researchers, CSV coordinators, local enumerators, and development practitioners working on climate-smart agriculture monitoring; Technical/Expert Level; Research institutions, development organizations, and agricultural extension services implementing CSV programs
Where it applies: countries: Ghana, Colombia, Nicaragua, Guatemala, Honduras, Uganda, Nepal, Bangladesh; regions: Latin America, East Africa, South Asia; context: Diverse agricultural systems across tropical and subtropical regions; scale: Community
How it works: Uses a mobile app (GeoFarmer Smart App) to collect standardized data on CSA adoption and impacts through structured household interviews. Data is collected across 9 thematic modules covering demographics, climate shocks, livelihood security, food security, and CSA options.
Key steps: Set up surveys and tailor Smart App for each CSV site. Conduct 1-week regional and local training for coordinators and enumerators. Implement sampling strategy targeting baseline households and CSA beneficiaries. Conduct household interviews with head of household and opposite-sex partner. Complete farm performance calculator modules with sub-sample of adopting farmers. Analyze data using R scripts to calculate 44 standard indicators. Generate automated reports and visualizations through GeoFarmer dashboard
Expected outcomes: Standardized data on CSA adoption rates, perceived effects on productivity/income/food security, gender-disaggregated impacts, farm-level performance metrics; Evidence-based understanding of CSA effectiveness, identification of adoption barriers and enablers, support for scaling up promising CSA options
Key takeaways: The framework successfully standardizes CSA monitoring across diverse geographical and agricultural contexts using 44 robust indicators. Mobile app-based data collection enables real-time monitoring and systematic deployment across global CSV networks. Top adopted CSA practices (improved varieties, intercropping, organic fertilizer) show consistently high positive effects on production, income, and climate resilience. Gender-disaggregated data collection reveals differential adoption patterns and impacts between male and female-headed households. The system provides critical evidence for evaluating CSA efficacy and scaling up locally relevant practices
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Diverse agricultural systems across tropical and subtropical regions
Publisher: CCAFS (CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security)

title: Strategic Policy Response to Climate Change in the Philippines Vol. 2: Exploring How Climate Change Policies are Translated into Local Actions in the Agriculture Sector
summary_snippet: This handbook explores how national climate change policies in the Philippines are translated into local actions in the agriculture sector, with a focus on rice and irrigation systems. It describes the institutional setting, implementation dynamics, and bottlenecks to effective climate change policy...
bm25_text:
Title: Strategic Policy Response to Climate Change in the Philippines Vol. 2: Exploring How Climate Change Policies are Translated into Local Actions in the Agriculture Sector
Resource type: Guidelines
EE pillars: Policy & Regulatory
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems, Water & Irrigation
Overview: This handbook explores how national climate change policies in the Philippines are translated into local actions in the agriculture sector, with a focus on rice and irrigation systems. It describes the institutional setting, implementation dynamics, and bottlenecks to effective climate change policy implementation at the local level, based on a case study in Central Luzon.
Audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders; Practitioner Level; Government agencies, NGOs, research institutions, and international development organizations
Where it applies: countries: Philippines; regions: Asia, South-eastern Asia; context: Rice-based irrigated systems, particularly in Central Luzon; scale: National
How it works: The handbook maps the climate change policy pathway from national to local levels through institutional actors and describes how policies are mainstreamed, implemented, and monitored at different governance levels.
Key steps: National climate change policy formulation. Sectoral mainstreaming through agencies like DA and NIA. Local legitimization through ordinances and development plans. Program planning and execution at local level. Implementation through production, extension, and R&D programs. Monitoring and evaluation of outcomes. Institutional strengthening and capacity building
Expected outcomes: Enhanced understanding of climate change policy implementation dynamics, identification of bottlenecks, and intervention recommendations; More effective climate change response and mitigation in agriculture through improved policy implementation
Key takeaways: Climate change policy implementation in the Philippines follows a complex multi-level pathway with significant time lags between national policy issuance and local compliance. Major bottlenecks include absence of local government ordinances, structural deficiencies in climate change organizations, and weak coordination mechanisms. Implementation occurs primarily through existing agricultural programs rather than dedicated climate change programs, leading to fragmented approaches. Institutional strengthening is needed at local levels, particularly for LGUs, to enhance capacity for policy mainstreaming and program implementation. A more unified, systems-wide approach to climate change program planning and coordination is essential for effective implementation
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Rice-based irrigated systems, particularly in Central Luzon
Publisher: Southeast Asian Regional Center for Graduate Study and Research in Agriculture (SEARCA) and International Rice Research Institute (IRRI)

title: Strategic Policy Response to Climate Change in the Philippines Vol. 1: Portfolio of Climate Change Policies in Agriculture
summary_snippet: This is a comprehensive policy portfolio documenting climate change policies in the Philippines' agriculture sector, particularly focusing on rice production and irrigation. It provides a systematic overview of the hierarchy of climate change policies from constitutional provisions to local ordinanc...
bm25_text:
Title: Strategic Policy Response to Climate Change in the Philippines Vol. 1: Portfolio of Climate Change Policies in Agriculture
Resource type: Guidelines
EE pillars: Policy & Regulatory
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems, Water & Irrigation
Overview: This is a comprehensive policy portfolio documenting climate change policies in the Philippines' agriculture sector, particularly focusing on rice production and irrigation. It provides a systematic overview of the hierarchy of climate change policies from constitutional provisions to local ordinances, with emphasis on agricultural adaptation and mitigation measures.
Audience: Professionals in agriculture, natural resource management, environment, researchers, policy analysts, policymakers, students and teachers; Technical/Expert Level; Government agencies, research institutions, NGOs, local government units, academic institutions
Where it applies: countries: Philippines; regions: Asia, South-eastern Asia; context: Lowland rice production systems, irrigated agriculture; scale: National
How it works: Presents a hierarchical framework of climate change policies starting from constitutional provisions, through national laws and international treaties, to implementing rules and local ordinances. Uses an inductive approach moving from general climate change response to agriculture-specific policies.
Key steps: Constitutional foundation establishing environmental rights and agricultural development mandates. National legislation including Climate Change Act and agriculture-specific laws. International treaty ratification (UNFCCC, Kyoto Protocol, Paris Agreement). Development of national strategies and action plans. Implementation through administrative orders and memorandum circulars. Translation to local ordinances and enabling mechanisms. Field-level implementation through agency-specific guidelines
Expected outcomes: Comprehensive understanding of climate change policy landscape in Philippine agriculture, identification of policy gaps and implementation challenges; Enhanced climate change readiness and response in agriculture sector, improved policy implementation at local levels, mainstreaming of climate change in agricultural programs
Key takeaways: Philippines has developed a comprehensive hierarchy of climate change policies spanning constitutional provisions to local ordinances. Agriculture sector, particularly rice production, is prioritized in climate change adaptation and mitigation policies. Significant implementation gap exists between policy formulation and ground-level execution. Local government units play crucial frontline role but lack awareness and capacity for climate change ordinance development. Water-saving technologies like alternate wetting and drying are being mainstreamed through administrative orders
Development stage: Widely Deployed
Agro-ecological context: Lowland rice production systems, irrigated agriculture
Publisher: Southeast Asian Regional Center for Graduate Study and Research in Agriculture (SEARCA) and International Rice Research Institute (IRRI)

title: Monitoring and assessment guidelines and options towards land restoration and water resources management in agricultural landscapes
summary_snippet: This technical brief provides monitoring and assessment guidelines for land restoration and water resources management in agricultural landscapes, focusing on soil and water conservation techniques for smallholder farmers in Eastern Africa. It combines practical guidance on in-situ water harvesting ...
bm25_text:
Title: Monitoring and assessment guidelines and options towards land restoration and water resources management in agricultural landscapes
Resource type: Guidelines
EE pillars: M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Water & Irrigation, Agri-Food Systems
Overview: This technical brief provides monitoring and assessment guidelines for land restoration and water resources management in agricultural landscapes, focusing on soil and water conservation techniques for smallholder farmers in Eastern Africa. It combines practical guidance on in-situ water harvesting technologies like tied ridging with case studies from Kenya and Tanzania to help practitioners implement landscape-scale interventions.
Audience: Scientists, extension agents, and development practitioners working with smallholder agricultural settings; Technical/Expert Level; Research institutions, extension services, development organizations working on landscape management
Where it applies: countries: Kenya, Tanzania; regions: Eastern Africa; context: Semi-arid areas with annual rainfall of 300-800 mm, hilly landscapes with varying slope gradients; scale: Community
How it works: The guidelines provide a systematic approach to selecting and implementing soil and water conservation measures based on landscape characteristics, combining biological, mechanical, and bio-engineering methods tailored to local conditions and farmer needs.
Key steps: Define conservation objectives based on local conditions. Assess slope gradients and lengths using threshold limits (≤5% gradient, ≤20m length). Select appropriate conservation measures from biological, mechanical, or bio-engineering options. Implement in-situ water harvesting techniques like tied ridging for slopes <7%. Train farmers and extension agents on proper implementation. Monitor and maintain structures throughout growing season. Evaluate effectiveness using farmer scorecards and technical measurements
Expected outcomes: Reduced soil erosion, increased water infiltration, improved soil moisture retention, enhanced crop yields; Enhanced yield stability, household food security, reduced sediment loads in watersheds, improved landscape health
Key takeaways: No universal conservation practices work everywhere - planning requires selecting techniques relevant to local conditions and combining them into workable systems. Threshold limits of ≤5% slope gradient and ≤20m slope length help determine appropriate intervention spacing and prevent significant soil losses. In-situ water harvesting techniques like tied ridging can increase crop yields 2-3 fold in semi-arid areas with 300-800mm annual rainfall. Successful implementation requires cost-effective solutions with visible short-term benefits, farmer training, and ongoing maintenance. Landscape-scale monitoring using farmer scorecards and hydrological modeling helps identify degradation hotspots and evaluate intervention effectiveness
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Semi-arid areas with annual rainfall of 300-800 mm, hilly landscapes with varying slope gradients
Publisher: CIAT (International Center for Tropical Agriculture)

title: Chifra Woreda participatory land use and development plan, Afar National Regional State
summary_snippet: This is a comprehensive participatory land use and development plan for Chifra Woreda in Ethiopia's Afar region, developed as a pilot program to address rangeland degradation and improve pastoralist livelihoods. The document presents a detailed methodology for conducting woreda-level participatory l...
bm25_text:
Title: Chifra Woreda participatory land use and development plan, Afar National Regional State
Resource type: Tool
EE pillars: M&E & Learning, Policy & Regulatory
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Agri-Food Systems, Livestock
Overview: This is a comprehensive participatory land use and development plan for Chifra Woreda in Ethiopia's Afar region, developed as a pilot program to address rangeland degradation and improve pastoralist livelihoods. The document presents a detailed methodology for conducting woreda-level participatory land use planning in pastoral and agro-pastoral areas, including land capability classification, stakeholder engagement, and implementation strategies.
Audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, and government officials working in pastoral and agro-pastoral areas; Technical/Expert Level; Government agencies (particularly Environmental Protection and Land Use Administration), NGOs, research institutes, and donor organizations
Where it applies: countries: Ethiopia; regions: Africa, Eastern Africa; context: Arid and semi-arid pastoral and agro-pastoral areas; scale: District/Provincial
How it works: The approach involves establishing multi-disciplinary taskforces to conduct participatory assessments of biophysical and socio-economic conditions, engaging pastoralists and agro-pastoralists in problem identification and solution analysis, and developing land capability classifications to guide rangeland management decisions.
Key steps: Establish multi-discipline taskforce with rangeland, socio-economic, land capability and GIS teams. Conduct stakeholder analysis and recognition of primary and secondary stakeholders. Collect biophysical data (slope, soil, vegetation, water, climate) and socio-economic data through PRA tools. Assess rangeland conditions across traditional rangeland regimes. Conduct problem and constraint identification through group discussions. Perform land capability classification using modified matrix for pastoral systems. Hold validation workshop with communities and stakeholders
Expected outcomes: Eight land capability management units identified covering 151,902 hectares: 120,910 ha for rangeland improvement, 4,813 ha for restricted rehabilitation and wildlife sanctuary, 19,001 ha for irrigated cropland, and 7,169 ha for community ranch; Rehabilitation of rangelands, sustainable rangeland management, improved livestock productivity, enhanced traditional management systems, and increased household incomes for pastoralists and agro-pastoralists
Key takeaways: Participatory land use planning can be successfully adapted for pastoral and agro-pastoral contexts by incorporating traditional rangeland management systems and mobility patterns. Multi-disciplinary taskforces combining technical expertise with local knowledge are essential for developing appropriate land use plans. Land capability classification systems need modification for pastoral areas to account for rangeland productivity rather than just crop production potential. Water harvesting techniques are critical for rangeland improvement in arid environments where rainfall is insufficient for vegetation growth. Community validation and consensus-building are crucial for ensuring land use plans reflect pastoralist priorities and can be successfully implemented
Development stage: Piloted
Agro-ecological context: Arid and semi-arid pastoral and agro-pastoral areas
Publisher: Ministry of Agriculture, Ethiopia

title: Building multi-sided business models for stronger digital agribusiness market development
summary_snippet: This resource presents insights from three African digital agriculture entrepreneurs on building multi-sided business models for digital agribusiness startups. It addresses the challenge of creating sustainable business models in African markets where traditional single-product approaches often fail...
bm25_text:
Title: Building multi-sided business models for stronger digital agribusiness market development
Resource type: Case Study
EE pillars: Digital, Market Systems, M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Agri-Food Systems, Scaling Innovations
Overview: This resource presents insights from three African digital agriculture entrepreneurs on building multi-sided business models for digital agribusiness startups. It addresses the challenge of creating sustainable business models in African markets where traditional single-product approaches often fail due to limited market data and customer purchasing power.
Audience: Digital agriculture entrepreneurs and startup founders; Practitioner Level; Digital agriculture startups, agtech companies, and development organizations supporting agricultural innovation
Where it applies: countries: Kenya, Morocco, Burkina Faso, Côte d'Ivoire, Mali, Senegal; regions: Eastern Africa, Western Africa, Northern Africa; context: Various contexts including cereal production systems; scale: Regional/Multi-country
How it works: Entrepreneurs develop multi-sided business models by creating derivative products based on core technology that serve different customer segments, such as farmers and input companies, to diversify revenue streams and explore market opportunities.
Key steps: Develop core technology or service for primary market (farmers). Identify derivative applications for the same technology. Create individual value propositions for different customer segments. Leverage data from one segment to provide insights for another. Establish strong management and communication strategies. Implement resource allocation processes across market segments
Expected outcomes: Multiple revenue streams from different customer segments, market insights, and diversified product offerings; Increased business sustainability, reduced income risk, maximized return on investment, and expanded market exploration
Key takeaways: African digital agriculture startups need diversification strategies due to limited market data and customer purchasing power. Multi-sided business models can leverage the same core technology to serve different customer segments and create multiple revenue streams. Strong management and communication skills are essential to address investor concerns and maintain clear value propositions. Disciplined resource allocation processes are crucial to avoid confusion and maintain focus across different market segments. Technology should be viewed as a means to solve farmer challenges rather than an end in itself
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Various contexts including cereal production systems
Publisher: CTA (Technical Centre for Agricultural and Rural Cooperation)

title: USAID Kenya Crops and Dairy Market Systems Activity: Dairy value chain assessment September 2018
summary_snippet: This is a comprehensive dairy value chain assessment report for Kenya's Crops and Dairy Market Systems Activity, analyzing the dairy sector in 12 target counties in western and lower eastern Kenya. The report identifies productivity gaps, market inefficiencies, and development opportunities in emerg...
bm25_text:
Title: USAID Kenya Crops and Dairy Market Systems Activity: Dairy value chain assessment September 2018
Resource type: Report
EE pillars: Market Systems, M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Livestock, Value Chains, Agri-Food Systems
Overview: This is a comprehensive dairy value chain assessment report for Kenya's Crops and Dairy Market Systems Activity, analyzing the dairy sector in 12 target counties in western and lower eastern Kenya. The report identifies productivity gaps, market inefficiencies, and development opportunities in emerging dairy areas where milk supply falls significantly short of demand.
Audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders working on dairy value chain development; Technical/Expert Level; USAID implementing partners, government agencies, research institutions, and development organizations
Where it applies: countries: Kenya; regions: Eastern Africa; context: Emerging dairy areas in western Kenya (Kisii, Migori, Homa Bay, Kisumu, Siaya, Kakamega, Bungoma, Busia) and lower eastern Kenya (Kitui, Makueni, Taita Taveta); scale: District/Provincial
How it works: The assessment employs value chain analysis methodology to map actors, analyze productivity constraints, examine market channels, and identify intervention opportunities across input supply, production, marketing, and support services.
Key steps: Map value chain actors and their roles from input suppliers to end consumers. Analyze productivity levels and constraints by county and breed type. Examine marketing channels and calculate marketing margins. Assess support services including inputs, breeding, extension, and financial services. Evaluate institutional rules and regulatory framework. Identify collaboration opportunities and development interventions
Expected outcomes: Comprehensive value chain assessment identifying key constraints, opportunities, and intervention points for dairy sector development; Enhanced dairy productivity, improved market linkages, and increased incomes for smallholder farmers in target counties
Key takeaways: All 12 target counties are milk deficit areas with average supply gap of 55%, creating substantial market opportunities for increased production. Dairy productivity in target counties (683-892 liters per cow annually) is far below national average, indicating significant potential for improvement through better breeds, feeding, and management. Producer-to-consumer direct sales dominate (38% of volume), while cooperatives handle 31% and private traders 14% of marketed milk. Key intervention opportunities include promoting quality AI services, improving feeding practices, strengthening cooperatives, and developing market linkages. Support services are fragmented with multiple uncoordinated information sources leading to poor technology adoption
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Emerging dairy areas in western Kenya (Kisii, Migori, Homa Bay, Kisumu, Siaya, Kakamega, Bungoma, Busia) and lower eastern Kenya (Kitui, Makueni, Taita Taveta)
Publisher: International Livestock Research Institute

title: USAID - Kenya Crops and Dairy Market Systems (KCDMS): Feed and fodder value chain assessment report
summary_snippet: This report presents a comprehensive value chain assessment of feed and fodder systems in Kenya, conducted for the USAID Kenya Crops and Dairy Market Systems (KCDMS) program. It analyzes both compound feeds (dairy meal) and fodder value chains across 11 counties, examining production, supply, demand...
bm25_text:
Title: USAID - Kenya Crops and Dairy Market Systems (KCDMS): Feed and fodder value chain assessment report
Resource type: Report
EE pillars: Market Systems, M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Livestock, Value Chains, Agri-Food Systems
Overview: This report presents a comprehensive value chain assessment of feed and fodder systems in Kenya, conducted for the USAID Kenya Crops and Dairy Market Systems (KCDMS) program. It analyzes both compound feeds (dairy meal) and fodder value chains across 11 counties, examining production, supply, demand, market channels, and opportunities for intervention to improve dairy productivity and farmer incomes.
Audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders working on dairy value chains and livestock feed systems; Technical/Expert Level; USAID programs, government agencies, research institutions, NGOs, and private sector actors in agricultural development
Where it applies: countries: Kenya; regions: Africa, Eastern Africa; context: Mixed farming systems in emerging dairy areas including western Kenya and lower eastern regions; scale: Regional/Multi-country
How it works: The assessment uses value chain analysis methodology combining qualitative and quantitative approaches, mapping actors from production to consumption, analyzing performance margins, and identifying intervention opportunities.
Key steps: Conduct focus group discussions with county livestock production staff. Interview value chain actors including feed manufacturers, traders, and farmers. Map value chains for different feed and fodder types. Analyze production costs, margins, and market performance. Estimate supply and demand for feeds and fodder. Identify constraints and opportunities for intervention. Develop recommendations for value chain development
Expected outcomes: Comprehensive value chain maps, cost-benefit analysis of different feed types, market demand projections, identification of business opportunities; Improved dairy productivity through better feed access, increased farmer incomes, strengthened feed and fodder markets
Key takeaways: About 40% of farmers in KCDMS counties use dairy meal as feed supplement, with significant variation across counties (20% in Kitui/Makueni vs 60% in Kisii). Potential demand for dairy meal is estimated at 46,000 MT annually, creating market opportunities worth KES 1.2-1.8 billion. Fodder production shows strong profitability with natural pastures yielding highest margins (KES 165,100/acre) due to seed sales. Small-scale feed manufacturers achieve competitive margins (20-44%) but face challenges with raw material supply and working capital. Government policy removing VAT from raw materials while taxing finished products is driving growth in informal feed formulation and ingredient trading
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Mixed farming systems in emerging dairy areas including western Kenya and lower eastern regions
Publisher: International Livestock Research Institute (ILRI)

The project aimed to build decision-makers' capacity to use these tools to address gender an...

Title: Decision-makers in Ghana and Malawi increase knowledge and capacity to use methods and tools for assessing the effect of SAI for women and the youth
Resource type: Tool

Overview: This resource documents the development and testing of five gender and youth analysis tools for assessing the effects of Sustainable Agricultural Intensification (SAI) on women and youth in Ghana and Malawi. The project aimed to build decision-makers' capacity to use these tools to address gender and intergenerational inequities in agricultural development.
Audience: Decision-makers involved in gender and youth issues in agricultural development; Practitioner Level; Government agencies, NGOs, research institutions, farmer organizations
Where it applies: countries: Ghana, Malawi; regions: Africa, Eastern Africa, Southern Africa, Western Africa; context: Smallholder farming systems in sub-Saharan Africa; scale: Household
How it works: The project developed five tools for analyzing gender and youth impacts of SAI: Gender and Youth Balance Tree, Participatory Mapping Tools, Time Allocation Tools, Gender and Youth Inclusive Value Chain Analysis, and Youth and Land Responsiveness criteria.
Key steps: Select appropriate tool based on assessment needs. Conduct participatory data collection with farmers and stakeholders. Facilitate group discussions using the selected tool. Analyze results to identify gender and youth inequities. Develop recommendations for more inclusive SAI interventions. Integrate findings with gender transformative approaches. Monitor and evaluate outcomes
Expected outcomes: Assessment of gender and youth inequities in SAI interventions, identification of gaps in resource access and benefit sharing; More equitable benefits from SAI investments for women and youth, improved decision-making on inclusive agricultural development
Key takeaways: Five practical tools were developed for assessing gender and youth impacts of SAI interventions. Decision-makers showed significant improvement in knowledge and capacity to use gender analysis tools. Tools need to be culturally sensitive and aligned with local policy frameworks. Effective facilitation skills are critical for successful tool implementation. Integration with gender transformative approaches is essential for addressing root causes of inequity

Agro-ecological context: Smallholder farming systems in sub-Saharan Africa
Publisher: International Institute of Tropical Agriculture

title: Improved livestock feeding and tropical forages for low emission livestock development in West and Central Africa.
summary_snippet: This presentation showcases CIAT's research on improved livestock feeding and tropical forages for reducing greenhouse gas emissions in West and Central Africa. It demonstrates how forage-based system intensification can achieve productivity gains while lowering environmental footprints through bett...
bm25_text:
Title: Improved livestock feeding and tropical forages for low emission livestock development in West and Central Africa.
Resource type: Presentation
EE pillars: M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Climate Resilience, Livestock, Agri-Food Systems
Overview: This presentation showcases CIAT's research on improved livestock feeding and tropical forages for reducing greenhouse gas emissions in West and Central Africa. It demonstrates how forage-based system intensification can achieve productivity gains while lowering environmental footprints through better feed management and improved germplasm.
Audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers working on low emission livestock development; Technical/Expert Level; Research institutions, policy organizations, development agencies
Where it applies: countries: Democratic Republic of Congo, Benin, Rwanda, Tanzania; regions: Western Africa, Middle Africa; context: Mixed crop-livestock systems in tropical environments; scale: Regional/Multi-country
How it works: The LivestockPlus approach focuses on sustainable intensification through three processes: genetic improvements (improved yield, quality, stress resistance), ecological management of mixed systems, and socioeconomic optimization. Farmers adopt improved forage packages combining management practices with adequate germplasm.
Key steps: Identify and produce improved pastures resistant to extreme conditions. Integrate forage legumes and grasses into farming systems. Test germplasm in different sites and conditions. Quantify GHG reduction impacts and trade-offs. Develop context-specific solutions. Build capacity and train stakeholders. Establish science-policy partnerships
Expected outcomes: 200 advanced genotypes released, 4 hybrids commercialized covering 800,000 ha worldwide; Increased animal and crop productivity, reduced environmental impacts, improved resource use efficiency, restoration of degraded lands, reduced per unit animal GHGs
Key takeaways: Improved forages can reduce methane and nitrous oxide emissions while increasing productivity. Context-specific solutions are needed for different farming systems and regions. Productivity increases can go hand-in-hand with improved resource-use and GHG emission efficiency. Feed basket data is core to environmental assessment modeling. Strong science-policy partnerships are essential for scaling low emission livestock technologies
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Mixed crop-livestock systems in tropical environments
Publisher: CIAT

summary_snippet: The AVCD National Conference Report documents a comprehensive agricultural development program implemented across 21 counties in Kenya from 2015-2018, focusing on livestock, dairy, and staple crops value chains. bm25_text:
Title: The Accelerated Value Chain Development program national conference report, 26–27 April 2018: Developing value chains to farming as business with technology and innovations in Kenya
Resource type: Report
EE pillars: Market Systems, M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Value Chains, Livestock, Agri-Food Systems, Nutrition & Health
Overview: The AVCD National Conference Report documents a comprehensive agricultural development program implemented across 21 counties in Kenya from 2015-2018, focusing on livestock, dairy, and staple crops value chains. The program aimed to lift 326,000 households out of poverty by applying technologies and innovations to transition farmers from subsistence to market-oriented farming.
Audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders; Practitioner Level; Government agencies, development organizations, research institutions, private sector
Where it applies: countries: Kenya; regions: Africa, Eastern Africa; context: Semi-arid and arid lands, diverse agricultural production zones; scale: National
How it works: The program employed a whole value chain approach with push (supply) and pull (demand) strategies, focusing on technology application, market development, and capacity building across livestock, dairy, and staple crops value chains.
Key steps: Technology introduction and farmer training. Capacity building through village-based advisors. Market linkage development. Innovation platform establishment. Private sector engagement. Policy development support. Nutrition education integration
Expected outcomes: 266,568 households taken out of poverty, 229,594 farmers learning farming as business, 1.2 million hectares under improved management; Increased productivity, improved nutrition, enhanced food security, transition from subsistence to commercial farming
Key takeaways: Technology application combined with market development can significantly improve smallholder productivity and incomes. Multi-stakeholder partnerships involving government, private sector, and research institutions are essential for scaling agricultural innovations. Value chain approaches addressing both supply and demand sides are more effective than single-intervention strategies. Nutrition-sensitive programming requires multi-sector collaboration between agriculture, health, and education ministries. Sustainability requires transitioning from donor support to private sector and government ownership
Development stage: Widely Deployed
Agro-ecological context: Semi-arid and arid lands, diverse agricultural production zones
Publisher: International Livestock Research Institute (ILRI)

title: Mechanizing water lifting through pumps: a case study in Sri Lanka
summary_snippet: This IWMI working paper presents a comprehensive case study analyzing the rapid adoption and impacts of motorized water pumps for agricultural irrigation in Sri Lanka. The study examines the drivers, benefits, and challenges of water-lifting technology adoption across four case study areas, with par...
bm25_text:
Title: Mechanizing water lifting through pumps: a case study in Sri Lanka
Resource type: Case Study
EE pillars: M&E & Learning, Policy & Regulatory
Thematic areas: Water & Irrigation, Agri-Food Systems, Scaling Innovations
Overview: This IWMI working paper presents a comprehensive case study analyzing the rapid adoption and impacts of motorized water pumps for agricultural irrigation in Sri Lanka. The study examines the drivers, benefits, and challenges of water-lifting technology adoption across four case study areas, with particular focus on smallholder farmers' experiences with groundwater irrigation and its effects on cropping patterns, income, and gender dynamics.
Audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders working on agricultural water management; Technical/Expert Level; Government agencies, NGOs, donor organizations, research institutions, and water management organizations
Where it applies: countries: Sri Lanka; regions: Southern Asia; context: Dry zone areas with seasonal rainfall patterns, small tank cascade systems, and shallow groundwater aquifers; scale: National
How it works: The study employed a mixed methods approach combining secondary data analysis from customs records, key informant interviews, and detailed household surveys across four case study locations representing different irrigation contexts and groundwater use patterns.
Key steps: Data collection from Sri Lanka Customs on pump imports (2000-2016). Key informant interviews with customs officials, private sector actors, and farmer leaders. Selection of four diverse case study locations with different irrigation practices. Structured household surveys with 198 farmers (158 pump users, 40 non-users). Analysis of socioeconomic characteristics, hydrological features, and impacts. Assessment of enabling factors and policy interventions. Documentation of positive impacts and emerging challenges
Expected outcomes: Comprehensive analysis of water pump adoption trends, identification of key drivers and barriers, documentation of socioeconomic impacts, and policy recommendations; Inform evidence-based policy decisions for sustainable agricultural water management and inclusive technology adoption
Key takeaways: Water pump adoption in Sri Lanka increased by 110% between 2003-2015, enabling smallholders to shift from subsistence to market-oriented agriculture. Key drivers include affordable Chinese pumps, government agro-well programs, higher profits from cash crops, and individual control over water use decisions. Pump users achieve higher incomes through increased cropping intensity (up to 300% annually), crop diversification to high-value vegetables, and expanded cultivation areas. Technology adoption creates mixed gender impacts - increased women's participation and income opportunities but also additional labor burdens. Urgent need for governance frameworks to balance promotion of inclusive technology adoption with sustainable groundwater management and environmental protection
Development stage: Widely Deployed
Agro-ecological context: Dry zone areas with seasonal rainfall patterns, small tank cascade systems, and shallow groundwater aquifers
Publisher: International Water Management Institute (IWMI)

title: Potato agri-food systems program
