The program aims to enhance food security, nutrition, and farmer livelihoods by de...
bm25_text:
Title: Potato agri-food systems program
Resource type: Program Description
EE pillars: Market Systems, M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Agri-Food Systems, Climate Resilience, Scaling Innovations, Nutrition & Health, Value Chains, Biodiversity
Overview: The Potato Agri-food Systems Program is a comprehensive research-for-development initiative by the International Potato Center (CIP) that addresses agricultural development challenges through potato-focused solutions. The program aims to enhance food security, nutrition, and farmer livelihoods by developing resilient potato varieties, improving seed systems, and strengthening value chains across Africa, Asia, and Latin America.
Audience: Small-scale farmers, national research partners, policymakers, and development practitioners; Practitioner Level; CGIAR centers, national agricultural research organizations, development agencies, and private sector partners
Where it applies: countries: ; regions: Africa, Asia, Latin America; context: Highland and lowland agroecosystems, rice- and wheat-based systems, Andean regions; scale: Regional/Multi-country
How it works: The program operates through seven integrated components: genetic diversity conservation, breeding for improved varieties, seed system development, sustainable intensification practices, value chain enhancement, inclusion of women and youth, and monitoring/evaluation. It combines advanced biotechnology with participatory breeding methods and capacity building for national partners.
Key steps: Conserve and manage genetic diversity through genebank resources. Develop improved potato varieties using breeding and biotechnology. Establish quality seed production and distribution systems. Implement sustainable intensification and crop management practices. Strengthen value chains and market linkages. Ensure inclusion of women and young people. Monitor, evaluate and scale successful innovations
Expected outcomes: Resilient biofortified potato varieties, disease-resistant varieties, hybrid potato seeds, seed technology tools, value chain innovations, scaling guidelines, impact assessment tools; Improved productivity and farm incomes for more than 7 million households in Africa, Asia and Latin America by 2023; enhanced food security and nutrition; climate resilience of agri-food systems
Key takeaways: Potato is the world's third most important food crop, feeding one in seven people globally and supporting millions of livelihoods. CIP's integrated approach combines advanced breeding, biotechnology, seed systems, and value chain development to address multiple agricultural challenges. The program has demonstrated significant impact with varieties like Cooperation 88 generating billions in economic benefits. Focus on biofortification, disease resistance, and early maturity addresses malnutrition, climate resilience, and land pressure challenges. Success requires strong partnerships with national research organizations and inclusive approaches targeting women and youth
Development stage: Widely Deployed
Agro-ecological context: Highland and lowland agroecosystems, rice- and wheat-based systems, Andean regions
Publisher: International Potato Center (CIP)

title: Sweetpotato agri-food systems program
summary_snippet: The Sweetpotato Agri-Food Systems Program is a comprehensive research-for-development initiative by the International Potato Center (CIP) focused on developing and scaling orange-fleshed sweetpotato (OFSP) varieties to combat vitamin A deficiency and improve food security. bm25_text:
Title: Sweetpotato agri-food systems program
Resource type: Program Description
EE pillars: Market Systems
Thematic areas: Agri-Food Systems, Nutrition & Health, Climate Resilience, Value Chains, Scaling Innovations
Overview: The Sweetpotato Agri-Food Systems Program is a comprehensive research-for-development initiative by the International Potato Center (CIP) focused on developing and scaling orange-fleshed sweetpotato (OFSP) varieties to combat vitamin A deficiency and improve food security. The program addresses malnutrition affecting over 140 million children under 5 worldwide through an integrated approach combining breeding, seed systems, value chains, and nutrition education.
Audience: Development practitioners, researchers, policymakers, and agricultural organizations working on nutrition and food security; Technical/Expert Level; CGIAR centers, research institutions, NGOs, government agencies, and private sector partners involved in agricultural development
Where it applies: countries: ; regions: Africa, Asia, Latin America; context: Smallholder farming systems in developing countries, particularly areas affected by vitamin A deficiency; scale: Regional/Multi-country
How it works: The program uses an integrated approach combining breeding of biofortified OFSP varieties, seed system development, sustainable intensification practices, value chain development, and nutrition education. It operates through seven key components: genetic diversity management, breeding, seed system development, sustainable intensification, value chains, inclusion (targeting women and youth), and monitoring/evaluation.
Key steps: Develop climate-resilient, nutritious OFSP varieties through accelerated breeding. Establish improved seed systems for quality planting material production. Implement sustainable intensification practices and agronomic training. Develop inclusive value chains linking farmers to markets. Conduct nutrition education and behavioral change interventions. Build partnerships and scaling mechanisms. Monitor and evaluate impact on nutrition and livelihoods
Expected outcomes: Improved OFSP varieties, enhanced seed systems, strengthened value chains, nutrition education materials, and scaling guidelines; Reduced vitamin A deficiency, improved nutritional status of vulnerable populations (especially women and children), increased farmer incomes, and enhanced climate resilience of farming systems
Key takeaways: OFSP can provide 125g daily vitamin A needs for preschool children, making it a cost-effective solution for combating vitamin A deficiency. Integrated approach combining breeding, seed systems, value chains, and nutrition education is essential for large-scale impact. Public-private partnerships and multi-stakeholder collaboration are critical for successful scaling. Focus on women and youth inclusion creates additional income-generating opportunities. Climate-smart varieties with drought, heat, and salinity tolerance enhance system resilience
Development stage: Widely Deployed
Agro-ecological context: Smallholder farming systems in developing countries, particularly areas affected by vitamin A deficiency
Publisher: International Potato Center (CIP)

The review addresses critical knowledge gaps in disease epidemiology and proposes integrated management approache...
bm25_text:
Title: Fusarium wilt of banana: Current knowledge on epidemiology and research needs toward sustainable disease management
Resource type: Academic Paper
EE pillars: M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Agri-Food Systems, Climate Resilience
Overview: This is a comprehensive academic review that synthesizes current knowledge on Fusarium wilt of banana, a devastating soil-borne disease caused by Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. cubense (Foc). The review addresses critical knowledge gaps in disease epidemiology and proposes integrated management approaches for sustainable disease control, with particular focus on the emerging tropical race 4 (TR4) strain that threatens global banana production.
Audience: Plant pathologists, banana researchers, agricultural scientists, and disease management specialists; Academic/Research Level; Research institutions, agricultural universities, plant protection organizations, and international agricultural research centers
Where it applies: countries: Global scope with specific mentions of China, Taiwan, Philippines, Indonesia, Australia, Jordan, Oman, Mozambique, Lebanon, Pakistan, Vietnam, Laos, Myanmar, Israel, Brazil, Colombia, Nicaragua, India; regions: Global; context: Tropical and subtropical banana production systems; scale: Global
How it works: The review synthesizes existing knowledge on Fusarium wilt epidemiology, identifies research gaps, and proposes integrated management strategies combining exclusion, resistant cultivars, soil health practices, and biological control approaches.
Key steps: Pathogen exclusion and quarantine measures. Early detection and plant eradication. Use of resistant or tolerant cultivars when available. Soil health management through organic amendments and cover crops. Application of beneficial microorganisms. Integrated cultural practices including proper nutrition management. Continuous monitoring and containment strategies
Expected outcomes: Reduced disease incidence, improved soil health, enhanced plant resistance, better pathogen containment; Sustainable banana production, reduced crop losses, improved food security and livelihoods for banana-dependent communities
Key takeaways: Fusarium wilt tropical race 4 (TR4) poses a major threat to global banana production, having spread from Asia to Africa and the Middle East since 2010. Integrated management combining exclusion, resistant cultivars, soil health practices, and biological control is more effective than single approaches. Soil suppressiveness can be enhanced through organic amendments, beneficial microorganisms, and proper nutrient management, particularly avoiding ammonium-based nitrogen sources. Early detection, rapid response, and strict biosecurity measures are critical for preventing pathogen introduction and spread. Research gaps remain in understanding pathogen survival in alternative hosts, dispersal mechanisms, and microbiome interactions that could inform better management strategies
Development stage: Concept/Research
Agro-ecological context: Tropical and subtropical banana production systems
Publisher: Frontiers Media S.A.

summary_snippet: The PoSHAN surveys represent a nationally representative, multi-year research platform designed to understand the complex pathways linking agriculture to nutrition outcomes in Nepal. This framework provides a systematic approach for collecting annual data on markets, household food security, dietary...
bm25_text:
Title: Pathways from agriculture-to-nutrition: Design and conduct of the National PoSHAN surveys of Nepal
Resource type: Framework
EE pillars: Policy & Regulatory, M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Agri-Food Systems, Nutrition & Health
Overview: The PoSHAN surveys represent a nationally representative, multi-year research platform designed to understand the complex pathways linking agriculture to nutrition outcomes in Nepal. This framework provides a systematic approach for collecting annual data on markets, household food security, dietary patterns, and nutritional status to inform policy and program interventions.
Audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders working on agriculture-nutrition linkages; Technical/Expert Level; Research institutions, government agencies, international development organizations, and policy-making bodies
Where it applies: countries: Nepal; regions: South Asia; context: Designed to represent major agroecological zones (Mountains, Hills, and Terai plains); scale: National
How it works: The framework employs annual, same-season surveys of nationally representative households with preschool children, combined with seasonal assessments in sentinel sites. It uses a mixed longitudinal design to track households over time while continuously adding newly eligible households.
Key steps: Stratify country by major agroecological zones and systematically sample VDCs. Conduct district official interviews and VDC focus groups. Perform ward enumeration to identify all eligible households. Collect market survey data on food and agricultural input prices. Conduct household interviews on socioeconomic status and agricultural practices. Interview women and children on dietary intake, health, and nutrition. Perform anthropometric measurements and hemoglobin testing
Expected outcomes: Annual reports with zone-specific and national estimates of nutritional status, dietary intake, food security, agricultural productivity, and participation in interventions; Enhanced understanding of agriculture-to-nutrition pathways to inform policy and program design for improving food security and nutritional outcomes
Key takeaways: Nationally representative, longitudinal design enables both cross-sectional and temporal analysis of agriculture-nutrition pathways. Same-season annual surveys are essential for minimizing seasonal variability and enabling year-to-year comparisons. Agroecological stratification is vital in countries with diverse farming systems and environmental conditions. Seasonal assessments in sentinel sites provide crucial insights into timing of interventions to mitigate food shortages. Rigorous training, standardization, and quality control procedures are paramount for large-scale, multi-team data collection
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Designed to represent major agroecological zones (Mountains, Hills, and Terai plains)
Publisher: Johns Hopkins University Center for Human Nutrition

title: Lessons for promotion of new agricultural technology: a case of Vijay wheat variety in Nepal
summary_snippet: This academic paper analyzes the commercialization of Vijay wheat variety in Nepal, the first Ug99-resistant wheat variety released in the country. It examines seed supply systems, factors contributing to adoption, and provides lessons for promoting new agricultural technologies through a case study...
bm25_text:
Title: Lessons for promotion of new agricultural technology: a case of Vijay wheat variety in Nepal
Resource type: Academic Paper
EE pillars: M&E & Learning, Market Systems
Thematic areas: Agri-Food Systems, Scaling Innovations
Overview: This academic paper analyzes the commercialization of Vijay wheat variety in Nepal, the first Ug99-resistant wheat variety released in the country. It examines seed supply systems, factors contributing to adoption, and provides lessons for promoting new agricultural technologies through a case study of seed flow and commercialization patterns across five Terai districts.
Audience: Agricultural researchers, policymakers, seed system developers, and development practitioners; Academic/Research Level; Research institutions, agricultural development organizations, seed companies, government agencies
Where it applies: countries: Nepal; regions: South Asia; context: Terai (plain) regions suitable for wheat cultivation; scale: National
How it works: The study used multivariate regression analysis to identify factors contributing to Vijay wheat seed commercialization by surveying 87 agro-vets across five districts representing different Terai regions of Nepal.
Key steps: Purposive selection of study sites in major wheat-growing Terai districts. Random sampling of wheat seed selling agro-vets including national seed company dealers. Institutional surveys with agro-vets and seed companies. Expert consultation meetings with district agriculture offices and researchers. Data compilation and multivariate regression analysis. Analysis of seed flow patterns and commercialization factors
Expected outcomes: Understanding of seed commercialization patterns, identification of key factors affecting adoption, recommendations for seed system improvement; Improved seed delivery systems and faster adoption of disease-resistant wheat varieties to mitigate Ug99 stem rust epidemics
Key takeaways: Pre-release seed multiplication by private companies significantly accelerates variety adoption and commercialization. Dealership with national seed companies, perceived variety performance, and total wheat seed sales volume are key factors driving commercialization. Decentralization of foundation seed production to private actors while maintaining public sector focus on breeder seed can improve resource efficiency. Close coordination between public research, private seed companies, and community organizations is essential for effective seed delivery. Awareness programs comparing new varieties with existing popular varieties are crucial for rapid adoption
Development stage: Field-tested
Agro-ecological context: Terai (plain) regions suitable for wheat cultivation
Publisher: Agriculture & Food Security journal

title: ICT for food security in a changing climate: A path to women’s empowerment
countries: Global coverage with specific data from 72+ countries including Bangladesh, India, Pakistan, South Africa, Colombia, Peru, Argentina, Finland, Turkey, Saudi Arabia
summary_snippet: This is a comprehensive research report by the EQUALS Research Group that surveys the global landscape of gender equality in digital access, skills, and leadership. It provides preliminary findings on gender digital divides across three core areas: ICT access, advanced digital skills development, an...
bm25_text:
Title: ICT for food security in a changing climate: A path to women’s empowerment
Resource type: Report
EE pillars: Gender Equality & Social Inclusion, Digital, M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Agri-Food Systems
Overview: This is a comprehensive research report by the EQUALS Research Group that surveys the global landscape of gender equality in digital access, skills, and leadership. It provides preliminary findings on gender digital divides across three core areas: ICT access, advanced digital skills development, and participation in ICT industry leadership roles.
Audience: Policymakers, practitioners, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders interested in addressing gender inequality in digital technologies; Technical/Expert Level; Development organizations, government agencies, research institutions, private sector, and international partnerships
Where it applies: countries: Global coverage with specific data from 72+ countries including Bangladesh, India, Pakistan, South Africa, Colombia, Peru, Argentina, Finland, Turkey, Saudi Arabia; regions: Africa, Asia, Americas, Europe, Oceania; context: Rural and urban contexts with emphasis on agricultural information systems; scale: Global
How it works: The report conducts systematic analysis of existing research, official statistics, and case studies across three action areas: ICT access, skills development, and leadership participation. It identifies barriers, assesses data availability, and provides evidence-based recommendations.
Key steps: Review official statistics and academic literature on gender digital divides. Analyze sex-disaggregated data on ICT access, skills, and leadership. Identify barriers to gender equality across the three action areas. Assess availability and quality of gender-disaggregated data. Document case studies and best practices. Develop recommendations for addressing identified gaps. Provide framework for future research and policy interventions
Expected outcomes: Comprehensive analysis of gender digital divides, identification of barriers and recommendations, assessment of data gaps, case studies of successful interventions; Inform policy and programming decisions to reduce gender digital inequalities, guide future research priorities, support evidence-based interventions
Key takeaways: Gender gaps exist across most aspects of ICT access, skills, and leadership, but patterns vary significantly by region and country context. Barriers to gender digital equality include infrastructure availability, financial constraints, ability/aptitude issues, perceived relevance, safety concerns, and socio-cultural contexts. Women are underrepresented in advanced digital skills, ICT employment, and technology leadership positions globally. Digital technologies can both empower women and expose them to new forms of violence and discrimination. Addressing gender digital inequality requires multi-sectoral coordination beyond telecommunications, including education, employment, and social policy interventions
Development stage: Widely Deployed
Agro-ecological context: Rural and urban contexts with emphasis on agricultural information systems
Publisher: EQUALS Partnership

Overview: This is a call for proposals issued by the CGIAR System Organization in 2019 for establishing a Gender Equality in Food Systems Research Platform. Key steps: Centers prepare proposals following detailed template requirements. Submit proposals by July 2, 2019 deadline with cover letter, full proposal, and budget. External independent assessment process moderated by ISDC. Possible re-submission period if required. System Management Board review and endorsement recommendation. System Council consideration and approval. Implementation begins January 1, 2020
Expected outcomes: Elevated gender integration within CRP research, strategic research to close evidence gaps on women's empowerment in agriculture, culture change embedding across CGIAR; Transform CGIAR's gender equality research agenda, demonstrate CGIAR stewardship of gender research work, influence broader agriculture research for development ecosystem
Key takeaways: Comprehensive proposal requirements covering strategy, structure, governance, and budget for gender equality platform. Emphasis on multi-Center collaboration and maximizing involvement of CGIAR entities. Assessment focuses on relevance, scientific credibility, legitimacy, effectiveness, and budget justification. Platform aims to elevate gender integration across all CGIAR research programs. Structured timeline from proposal submission to implementation over 8-month period

title: Across country dairy breeding strategies in sub-Saharan Africa
summary_snippet: This PhD thesis investigates the feasibility and potential benefits of across-country genetic evaluations for dairy cattle in sub-Saharan Africa, using performance data from Holstein-Friesian and Jersey breeds in Kenya, South Africa, and Zimbabwe. The research addresses the challenge of limited gene...
bm25_text:
Title: Across country dairy breeding strategies in sub-Saharan Africa
Resource type: Academic Paper
EE pillars: M&E & Learning
Thematic areas: Agri-Food Systems, Livestock
Overview: This PhD thesis investigates the feasibility and potential benefits of across-country genetic evaluations for dairy cattle in sub-Saharan Africa, using performance data from Holstein-Friesian and Jersey breeds in Kenya, South Africa, and Zimbabwe. The research addresses the challenge of limited genetic improvement in African dairy cattle due to poor data recording, small herd sizes, and lack of systematic breeding programs.
Audience: Scientists, researchers, and dairy breeding specialists; Academic/Research Level; Research institutions, universities, agricultural research centers, and breeding organizations
Where it applies: countries: Kenya, South Africa, Zimbabwe; regions: Sub-Saharan Africa; context: Various dairy production systems from small-scale to large-scale intensive and extensive farming; scale: Regional/Multi-country
How it works: The research pooled dairy performance and pedigree data from three countries to conduct joint genetic evaluations, comparing within-country versus across-country genetic parameter estimation and breeding value prediction for Holstein-Friesian and Jersey cattle.
Key steps: Conduct stakeholder survey on dairy improvement infrastructure. Collect and edit performance data from multiple countries. Identify genetic links through common sires across countries. Estimate genetic parameters within and across countries. Calculate breeding values and reliabilities. Predict genetic gains from different selection strategies. Compare benefits of joint versus national evaluations
Expected outcomes: More accurate genetic parameters, improved breeding value estimates, and enhanced genetic gain predictions; Improved dairy cattle productivity through better genetic selection and enhanced breeding programs across sub-Saharan Africa
Key takeaways: Across-country genetic evaluations provide more accurate genetic parameters than within-country analyses for dairy cattle in sub-Saharan Africa. Countries with limited data benefit most from joint evaluations, while those with established programs can serve as genetic material hubs. Systematic data recording and international collaboration are essential prerequisites for successful joint breeding programs. Joint evaluations could accelerate genetic progress and provide wider choice of animals for selection than national programs alone. Harmonized data collection methods and trait definitions across countries are crucial for effective across-country genetic evaluations
Development stage: Concept/Research
Agro-ecological context: Various dairy production systems from small-scale to large-scale intensive and extensive farming
Publisher: University of Edinburgh
